[]{#Frontmatter_004.xhtml}

:::::: {.section .Foreword .foreword aria-labelledby="forewordtitle" role="doc-foreword"}
# Foreword {#Frontmatter_004.xhtml_forewordtitle .ForewordTitle lang="en"}

::::: ForewordBody
[]{#Frontmatter_004.xhtml_PBxxxi .pagebreak aria-label="xxxi" role="doc-pagebreak" title="xxxi"}The web\'s strength is giving us all personalized space; its worst may be having to deal with everyone else\'s. That may be why browsers have been extendable for over 30 years. It can be easy to forget that features that feel basic, if not bedrock to modern users, came from developers and users extending the browser.

When ViolaWWW pioneered the concept of extensions way back in 1992, we got with scripting, stylesheets, and client-side document insertion. Just a few months later, Netscape Navigator allowed developers to extend the browser with video playback with RealPlayer and Quicktime plug-ins, and never before seen interactivity with Shockwave/Flash. Shortly after, Internet Explorer 4 added ActiveX, Browser Helper Objects, and \"Web Accessories\" to make extending the browser easier and more user friendly. An innovation, for sure. But anyone who had to suffer through fixing their family members' \"toolbar bloat\" can attest to this customization being a double-edged sword.

When Firefox launched in 2004, it came out of the ashes of Netscape Navigator. Building on top of Navigator\'s plugin-focused nature of extensibility, the Firefox team prioritized making this extensibility available to anyone. With XUL and XPCOM, extension developers gained unprecedented power to modify the browser\'s interface and behavior. From these incredibly powerful APIs, we got the concept of user scripts with Greasemonkey and the Firebug [--]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional} the extension that launched a million web dev careers. However, this model was complex, leading to instability, performance issues, and security concerns due to its deep system access.

[]{#Frontmatter_004.xhtml_PBxxxii .pagebreak aria-label="xxxii" role="doc-pagebreak" title="xxxii"}Google Chrome brought a new approach to extensions, focusing on web standards, security, and performance. Its APIs were built on HTML, CSS, and JavaScript directly. As a result, more developers than ever before could use it. A multi-process model, sandboxing, and a declarative permissions system were implemented from day one as a direct response to the perceived inadequacies of other extension models. In its heady early days, nearly everything in the browser was extendable [--]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional} to this day, a number of Chrome and ChromeOS features are directly implemented on top of extension APIs. But as with all other extension systems beforehand, Chrome\'s also began to show issues.

In 2021, nearly 30 years after ViolaWWW introduced the concept of an extendable web, the W3C WebExtensions Community Group (WECG) was created. In an effort to stop reinventing the wheel, browser developers from all major vendors got together to formalize the concept of WebExtensions [--]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional} aiming to create a common architectural model and define core APIs for cross-browser compatibility.

It is that newfound cross-browser collaboration and the incredibly exciting possibilities of deeper AI integration and user customization that make today such a wonderful time to start creating WebExtensions. Matt is truly one of the most knowledgeable and battle-tested developers I have ever had the pleasure of speaking with, and I have been honored to help edit this book. Whether you have been making extensions for years or are just starting your journey, I know that it will be incredibly helpful to you!

::: Para
> Patrick Kettner
:::

::: Para
> Chrome Extensions Developer Advocate at Google
:::
:::::
::::::

[]{#Frontmatter_005.xhtml}

:::: {.section .Preface .preface aria-labelledby="prefacetitle" role="doc-preface"}
# Introduction {#Frontmatter_005.xhtml_prefacetitle .PrefaceTitle lang="en"}

::: PrefaceBody
[]{#Frontmatter_005.xhtml_PBxxxiii .pagebreak aria-label="xxxiii" role="doc-pagebreak" title="xxxiii"}I wrote the first edition of *Building Browser Extensions* in 2022 to address what I judged to be a dire need in the browser extension ecosystem. Extension development was poorly documented and under-resourced, with no practical, developer-focused books or guides available. Powerful APIs were available to extension developers, but most web developers were unaware of how much they could accomplish inside the browser environment. The transition to Manifest V3 was reshaping the extension landscape, and developers urgently needed guidance to navigate the changes.

A few months after the first edition was published, OpenAI released ChatGPT to the public, and the ensuing large language model (LLM) land grab radically transformed all forms of software development, including and *especially* web and extension development. In 2025, it is now easier than ever to build and publish a browser extension. At the same time, an entirely new class of browser extensions was introduced: LLM-powered extensions.

Browser extensions are *insanely* powerful pieces of software. In the hands of a careful developer, extensions are an elegant means to unlock browser capabilities that elude the grasp of traditional websites. In the hands of an inexperienced developer, a sloppily constructed extension can be at best an annoyance and at worst a gaping security hole. In the hands of a malicious actor, an extension is a convenient vehicle for browser infiltration and theft. This book is designed to give you all the tools to ensure your extensions are efficient, secure, and reliable.

The WebExtensions API has advanced considerably in recent years. Manifest V3 is now the standard across Chromium browsers, and this book []{#Frontmatter_005.xhtml_PBxxxiv .pagebreak aria-label="xxxiv" role="doc-pagebreak" title="xxxiv"}has been updated to reflect this. Additions such as side panel, offscreen documents, user scripts, and in-browser LLM prompt APIs are all covered in this book.

The most frequent feedback on the first edition was the desire for more tutorials. In response to this, I've dedicated an entire chapter of this book to hands-on tutorials that show you how to build working extensions from scratch. You'll learn how to build tab managers, screen recorders, ad blockers, password managers, user script managers, and AI chatbots.

The world of extension development tooling has come a long way since the first edition. React remains the most popular JavaScript framework, but there are a handful of extremely popular development platforms that have emerged such as Vite, Extension.js, and WXT. The chapter on tooling and frameworks has been updated to cover all of these and more.

Like many developers, I learn by example. I created a companion extension for the book: *Example Chrome Extension*. It's an open source browser extension with dozens of interactive demos. Each demo shows how various browser extension pieces and APIs work, and each includes links to the specific source files so you can see how it was built.

You can download *Example Chrome Extension* from the Chrome Web Store, or find a link to it on the book's website: [[[[https://buildingbrowserextensions.com]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional}]{.RefSource}](https://buildingbrowserextensions.com)]{.ExternalRef}.
:::
::::

[]{#Frontmatter_006.xhtml}

Any source code or other supplementary material referenced by the author in this book is available to readers on GitHub. For more detailed information, please visit https://www.apress.com/gp/services/source-code.
:::

[]{#Frontmatter_007.xhtml}

:::: {.section .BookAcknowledgments .acknowledgments aria-labelledby="acknowledgmenttitle" role="doc-acknowledgments"}
# Acknowledgments {#Frontmatter_007.xhtml_acknowledgmenttitle .BookAcknowledgmentsTitle lang="en"}

::: BookAcknowledgmentsBody
I'd like to acknowledge the tremendous work done by editor Sowmya Thodur and everyone else involved with the book's production, including Divya Modi, Gryffin Winkler, James Robinson-Prior, and Joseph Quatela. Writing a new edition with a newborn was an ambitious undertaking, and I am deeply appreciative of everyone's flexibility.

I'd also like to thank the book's technical reviewer Patrick Kettner. I was truly blown away at your attention to detail [--]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional} what an exceptional job you\'ve done. I am incredibly grateful for your meticulous analysis and expertise, and the book is better for it. I often laughed to myself while reading chapter comments, thinking "That was *definitely* written by a Googler."

Finally, I would like to thank Apress for running it back with another edition of *Building Browser Extensions*. Your vote of confidence is deeply appreciated, and greenlighting a second edition is critically important for growing and educating the browser extension developer community.
:::
::::

[]{#Frontmatter_008.xhtml}

They are wearing glasses and standing outdoors on a patio with a scenic background of trees and a building. The sky is partly cloudy. The expression is friendly and engaging." />

::: {#Frontmatter_009.xhtml_Par25 .Para}
[]{#Frontmatter_009.xhtml_PBxxv .pagebreak aria-label="xxv" role="doc-pagebreak" title="xxv"}has over a decade of experience in web development, is the founder of a supply chain software company, and is a Google Developer Expert.
:::

As a Google software engineer, Matt worked on both the AdSense and Accelerated Mobile Pages (AMP) platforms; his code contributions run on most of the planet\'s web browsing devices. Prior to this, Matt was the first engineer at DoorDash, where he helped lay the foundation for a company that has become the leader in online food delivery.

Matt is the author of six books, a frequent podcast guest, and a speaker at conferences and meetups focused on front-end development.

Matt lives near Chicago with his family and two dopey dogs and can be reached at [[[[mattfriz.com]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional}]{.RefSource}](https://mattfriz.com)]{.ExternalRef}.
:::::

won an eighth-grade talent show competition singing Gloria Gaynor's 1978 disco hit "I Will Survive", and things have been looking up ever since. A web developer for over 30 years, he has committed code to all major browser engines of the past 20 years and is currently working at Google on Chrome Extensions. [M. Frisbie]{.ContextInformationAuthorEditorNames}[[Building Browser Extensions]{.BookTitle}]{.ContextInformationBookTitles}[<https://doi.org/10.1007/979-8-8688-1594-2_1>]{.ChapterDOI}
::::
:::::

`<!--Begin Abstract-->`{=html}

::: MainTitleSection
# 1. What Are Browser Extensions? {#534317_2_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_chaptertitle .ChapterTitle lang="en"}
:::

:::::::: AuthorGroup
::: AuthorNames
[[Matt Frisbie]{.AuthorName}^[1](#534317_2_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_Aff2) [ ]{.ContactIcon}^]{.Author}
:::

:::::: Affiliations
:::: {#534317_2_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_Aff2 .Affiliation}
[(1)]{.AffiliationNumber}

::: AffiliationText
Glen Ellyn, IL, USA
:::
::::

::: ClearBoth
 
:::
::::::
::::::::

`<!--End Abstract-->`{=html}

:::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::: Fulltext
Browser extensions are strange and powerful parasites.

[]{#534317_2_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_PB1 .pagebreak aria-label="1" role="doc-pagebreak" title="1"}*Parasites*, because browser extensions reside in an unusual corner of the world of software: they run on top of web pages yet are totally independent of any website or server. [*Powerful*]{#534317_2_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm1}, because browser extensions are afforded an unusual degree of agency: browser extensions can view and modify everything you see in your browser, inspect and manage every HTTP request your browser sends, and record and store credentials for all your digital accounts. [*Strange*]{#534317_2_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm2}, because browser extensions directly antagonize the very companies that support them: ad blockers, one of the most commonly installed extensions in Google Chrome, exist only to prevent showing the very ads that make up Google's primary revenue channel.

Browser extensions afford the end user a [degree of control]{#534317_2_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm3} over their web browsing experience that is not possible in any other way. The most popular browser extensions have tens of millions of installs, and the Chrome Web Store has well over 100,000 extensions published. In recognition of the broad adoption and utility, in 2021, the W3C formed the WebExtensions Community Group to "explore how browser vendors and other interested parties can work together to advance a common browser extension platform."

::::::::::: {#534317_2_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_Sec1 .section .Section1 .RenderAsSection1 aria-labelledby="Sec1Heading"}
## History of Browser Extensions {#534317_2_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_Sec1Heading .Heading}

[]{#534317_2_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_PB2 .pagebreak aria-label="2" role="doc-pagebreak" title="2"}Much in the same way that evolutionary biologists learn about modern life forms through the study of transitional fossils, browser extensions can be better understood by examining their software antecedents.

:::: {#534317_2_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_Sec2 .section .Section2 .RenderAsSection2 aria-labelledby="Sec2Heading"}
### Customizing Software with Plug-ins {#534317_2_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_Sec2Heading .Heading}

::: {#534317_2_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_Par6 .Para}
Support for *plug-ins*[]{#534317_2_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm4}[]{#534317_2_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm5}, software components that alter the behavior of an existing computer program, was first implemented in the 1970s on the [UNIVAC Series 90 mainframe computer]{#534317_2_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm6} (Figure [[1-1](#534317_2_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_Fig1)]{.InternalRef}). EDT, a text editor running on the mainframe's Unisys VS/9 operating system, allowed other programs to "plug in" and access its in-memory buffer, as well as send commands back to it for processing. Granted these abilities, plug-in programs were capable of programmatically altering the flow of other programs or invoking text editor commands, tasks that were normally accomplished via direct user input.[]{#534317_2_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_PB3 .pagebreak aria-label="3" role="doc-pagebreak" title="3"}

<figure id="Fig1" class="Figure">
<div id="534317_2_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_MO1" class="MediaObject">
<img src="/app/temp/1773197898499_images/images/534317_2_En_1_Chapter/534317_2_En_1_Fig1_HTML.jpg" class="ImageStyle3" alt="A black and white photograph of a vintage computer labeled &quot;Sperry UNIVAC 90/60.&quot; The machine features a panel with rows of lights, dials, and switches. The background includes shelves and boxes, suggesting a storage or archive setting. The image highlights the retro design and technology of early computing equipment." />
</div>
<figcaption><div class="CaptionContent">
<span class="CaptionNumber">Figure 1-1</span>
<p><span id="534317_2_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm7">UNIVAC Series 90 mainframe computer</span> (<span class="ExternalRef"><a href="https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Univac9060.jpg"><span class="RefSource"><span class="EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional">https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Univac9060.jpg</span></span></a></span>, Source: United States Navy)</p>
</div></figcaption>
</figure>
:::
::::

::: {#534317_2_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_Sec3 .section .Section2 .RenderAsSection2 aria-labelledby="Sec3Heading"}
### History of Web Browsers {#534317_2_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_Sec3Heading .Heading}

The fundamentals of [web browser]{#534317_2_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm8} behavior were a major contributor to the explosive adoption of the Internet. Unlike the conventional software delivery model of distributing executables -- binary programs that are difficult to inspect or change -- software delivered over HTTP was open source. Anyone who receives an HTML page has the ability to do what they like with it: view the source, modify existing content and styling, or duplicate it and reuse it for their own needs. Good ideas could quickly propagate because it was easy to reverse-engineer how a website achieved a feature.

[]{#534317_2_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_PB4 .pagebreak aria-label="4" role="doc-pagebreak" title="4"}The release of [JavaScript]{#534317_2_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm9}[]{#534317_2_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm10} in 1995 continued with this idea. Because JavaScript is an interpreted language, scripts delivered to the browser via HTTP were open source. Furthermore, part of JavaScript's usefulness was the [Document Object Model (DOM)]{#534317_2_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm11}, which allowed scripts to use an API to programmatically inspect and modify what the browser was showing the user. Even decades later, it remains true that every web page in the world can be inspected and modified in JavaScript via the same set of browser APIs.
:::

::: {#534317_2_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_Sec4 .section .Section2 .RenderAsSection2 aria-labelledby="Sec4Heading"}
### Native Browser Plug-ins {#534317_2_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_Sec4Heading .Heading}

At the same [time]{#534317_2_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm12} that JavaScript was getting on its feet, software companies were rolling out new ways that software could run in the browser. This software took the form of "[browser plug-ins]{#534317_2_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm13}," which were closed source modules that were able to run programs natively on the host computer from within the browser. Examples of these were Java applets, Adobe Flash, Microsoft Silverlight and ActiveX, and Apple QuickTime. JavaScript at the time was quite slow and feature-limited, so offloading computation to the host system allowed for browsers to utilize more system memory, persistent storage, and graphics APIs that would otherwise be off-limits. Because these plug-ins lived in a totally different execution context, they were largely sandboxed from the web page around them.

These [plug-ins]{#534317_2_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm14} were adopted out of necessity, but they were replete with problems. Plug-ins were clunky to install and use, and because they were provided with privileged access to the host system, they introduced browser bugs and security holes. As browsers became more and more capable with HTML5, CSS3, and more fully featured ECMAScript specifications, these plug-ins served less of a purpose, and as a result, modern browsers have dropped [support]{#534317_2_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm15} for them almost entirely.
:::

::::: {#534317_2_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_Sec5 .section .Section2 .RenderAsSection2 aria-labelledby="Sec5Heading"}
### Origins of Browser Customization {#534317_2_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_Sec5Heading .Heading}

:::: {#534317_2_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_Par11 .Para}
[]{#534317_2_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_PB5 .pagebreak aria-label="5" role="doc-pagebreak" title="5"}Early [versions]{#534317_2_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm16}[]{#534317_2_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm17} of Internet Explorer and Firefox supported "add-ons" capable of customizing the browser. However, these relied on proprietary integrations, limiting them to a single browser and vendor-specific APIs.

::: UnorderedList
-   In 1999, [Internet Explorer 4]{#534317_2_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm18} introduced support for "Explorer Bars" and custom entries in context menus, allowing limited interface modification.

-   In 2004, the newly released [Firefox]{#534317_2_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm19} introduced a more flexible customization system using XUL (XML User Interface Language), which enabled developers to modify core parts of the browser's UI, such as toolbars and menus.

-   The first userscript manager, [Greasemonkey]{#534317_2_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm20}, was released for Firefox in 2005. It allowed users to inject small, customizable JavaScript scripts into web pages, modifying behavior without needing to develop a full browser extension.
:::
::::

These solutions were not without issues. Though powerful, XUL eventually proved problematic due to security vulnerabilities and the instability caused by conflicting extensions. Userscripts lacked the deep integration of extensions and introduced security risks, particularly as [scripts]{#534317_2_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm21}[]{#534317_2_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm22} could be shared informally, bypassing browser security reviews.
:::::

::: {#534317_2_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_Sec6 .section .Section2 .RenderAsSection2 aria-labelledby="Sec6Heading"}
### Emergence of Modern Extensions {#534317_2_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_Sec6Heading .Heading}

It wasn't until September 2009 that the first modern browser [extensions]{#534317_2_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm23} became available in Google Chrome. Unlike add-ons for other browsers, these [Chrome extensions]{#534317_2_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm24} could be built using HTML, CSS, and JavaScript. They interacted with the browser using a JavaScript extension API, and developers could publish their extensions to the Chrome Web Store.

[]{#534317_2_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_PB6 .pagebreak aria-label="6" role="doc-pagebreak" title="6"}By June 2012, Chrome passed Internet Explorer as the most popular web browser, and the Chrome Web Store reached 750 million total installs. In the ensuing years, rival browsers began to progressively adopt the APIs and [extension]{#534317_2_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm25} model that Google Chrome had pioneered.
:::
:::::::::::

::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::: {#534317_2_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_Sec7 .section .Section1 .RenderAsSection1 aria-labelledby="Sec7Heading"}
## The Browser Extension Landscape {#534317_2_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_Sec7Heading .Heading}

In the wake of years of trial and error, the browser extensions of today are a mature software platform. They enjoy broad adoption by developers and consumers, have well-defined APIs and documentation, and can easily reach the end user via a robust app store pipeline. In this section, we'll explore how to think about browser extensions, where users go to install them, and what the different categories of browser extension are.

::: {#534317_2_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_Sec8 .section .Section2 .RenderAsSection2 aria-labelledby="Sec8Heading"}
### Comparing Mobile Apps and Browser Extensions {#534317_2_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_Sec8Heading .Heading}

In many [respects]{#534317_2_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm26}, modern browser extensions resemble mobile apps. They both explicitly declare which permissions they need from the host system, are restricted by well-defined security models, and utilize a broad collection of APIs to interact with that host. They are both bundled into releases and deployed via a vendor-specific app store. Both mobile app and browser extension releases undergo a review process, and updates are automatically downloaded and installed via the host system.

Whereas mobile [apps]{#534317_2_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm27} typically exist as a stand-alone user interface, extensions usually are built to run atop one or many web pages with a supplementary user interface. Unlike mobile apps, browser extensions do not necessarily have a user interface; many popular browser extensions are nothing more than a piece of JavaScript that runs in the background to execute a handler upon some event.
:::

::::: {#534317_2_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_Sec9 .section .Section2 .RenderAsSection2 aria-labelledby="Sec9Heading"}
### Browser Extension Marketplaces {#534317_2_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_Sec9Heading .Heading}

:::: {#534317_2_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_Par21 .Para}
[]{#534317_2_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_PB7 .pagebreak aria-label="7" role="doc-pagebreak" title="7"}All major browser [vendors]{#534317_2_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm28} offer a marketplace where extensions can be published and downloaded:

::: UnorderedList
-   [Chrome extensions]{#534317_2_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm29} can be installed from the Chrome Web Store ([[[[https://chromewebstore.google.com]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional}]{.RefSource}](https://chromewebstore.google.com)]{.ExternalRef}). This extension store is the largest and most popular.

-   [Safari extensions]{#534317_2_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm30} can only be downloaded from the Apple App Store.

-   [Mozilla Firefox extensions]{#534317_2_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm31} can be installed from the Add-ons marketplace ([[[[https://addons.mozilla.org]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional}]{.RefSource}](https://addons.mozilla.org)]{.ExternalRef}).

-   [Microsoft Edge extensions]{#534317_2_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm32} can be installed from the Edge Add-ons marketplace ([[[[https://microsoftedge.microsoft.com]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional}]{.RefSource}](https://microsoftedge.microsoft.com)]{.ExternalRef}). The Edge browser uses the Chromium rendering engine, so Chrome Web Store extensions can be installed in the Edge browser by enabling the "Allow extensions from other stores" setting.

-   [Opera extensions]{#534317_2_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm33}[]{#534317_2_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm34} can be downloaded from the Opera Addons marketplace ([[[[https://addons.opera.com]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional}]{.RefSource}](https://addons.opera.com)]{.ExternalRef}). Like the Edge browser, Opera is a Chromium-based browser and can be configured to install extensions from the Chrome Web Store.
:::
::::
:::::

:::::::::::::::::::::::::: {#534317_2_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_Sec10 .section .Section2 .RenderAsSection2 aria-labelledby="Sec10Heading"}
### Types of Browser Extensions {#534317_2_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_Sec10Heading .Heading}

Because of the broad API at their disposal, developers can build browser extensions capable of performing a remarkable range of tasks. However, just as Unix programs are expected to "do one thing and do it well," browser extensions are generally geared toward being smaller and more targeted in their purview.

::::: {#534317_2_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_Sec11 .section .Section3 .RenderAsSection3 aria-labelledby="Sec11Heading"}
#### Ad and Tracking Blockers {#534317_2_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_Sec11Heading .Heading}

[]{#534317_2_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_PB8 .pagebreak aria-label="8" role="doc-pagebreak" title="8"}Easily the most common extension type, [ad and tracking blockers]{#534317_2_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm35}[]{#534317_2_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm36} serve a very simple purpose: to block unwanted content. Modern web pages are constructed from a cascade of requests for individual pieces of content. Most of these requests are for essential pieces of content like CSS and JavaScript to render the page, but many are for scripts that render ads, collect analytics, or track the user.

The core strategy these blocking extensions utilize is [simple]{#534317_2_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm37}[]{#534317_2_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm38}. When installed, a blocking extension is granted the ability to inspect and manage all HTTP requests a page makes. The extension also comes bundled with a list of URL domains and regular expressions that are known to serve ads or tracking content. As each page request goes out, the extension checks it against the list and kills the request if there is a match.

From the page's perspective, it will appear that the request simply failed -- something that is completely normal and expected when sending requests to a remote resource. From the user's perspective, the web page will *usually* be correctly rendered with only the ads and tracking scripts teased out. From the ad or tracker server's perspective, they have no idea that the user ever visited the page, since the outgoing request never reaches the server.

Of course, this [sort]{#534317_2_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm39}[]{#534317_2_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm40} of extension is problematic for entities that depend on the blocked content to generate information or revenue. In response, the ad and tracker industries have begun to roll out countermeasures to address the widespread use of blocker extensions. Some pages now will only render their content if the ad and tracker scripts load; they assume that a failed request is *always* because a blocking extension killed the request. Some ad and tracking servers serve their scripts using "CNAME cloaking," where the ad or tracking script request pretends to be regular [content]{#534317_2_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm41}[]{#534317_2_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm42} loaded from the page's domain.

:::: {#534317_2_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_FPar1 .FormalPara .FormalParaRenderingStyle1 .ParaTypeImportant}
::: Heading
Note
:::

[]{#534317_2_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_PB9 .pagebreak aria-label="9" role="doc-pagebreak" title="9"}The APIs used by extensions to manage network requests are covered in detail in Chapter [[[11]{.RefSource}](#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml)]{.ExternalRef}.
::::
:::::

::::: {#534317_2_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_Sec12 .section .Section3 .RenderAsSection3 aria-labelledby="Sec12Heading"}
#### Password Managers {#534317_2_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_Sec12Heading .Heading}

[Extensions]{#534317_2_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm43}[]{#534317_2_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm44} can manage a JavaScript execution environment that is completely separated from any web page. This sandboxed environment is ideally suited to load and store secret information, where it can be securely passed to and from web pages on demand. [Password managers]{#534317_2_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm45} leverage this to record username/password combinations, save them locally or remotely, and type them in for the user.

:::: {#534317_2_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_Par34 .Para}
A password manager extension allows the user to log in to the password manager service inside a sandboxed extension interface. Once credentialed, they will load the user's encrypted passwords from a remote server and decrypt them locally. With the passwords now available in memory, the extension manager is granted permissions that allow it to [effectively]{#534317_2_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm46}[]{#534317_2_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm47} manage a user's passwords:

::: UnorderedList
-   View and manage the page DOM to automatically detect username and password inputs, type in usernames and passwords, and render additional content on the page to manage user credentials.

-   View and manage outgoing requests to automatically detect requests that contain user credentials.
:::
::::

Perhaps the most important aspect of using [password managers]{#534317_2_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm48}[]{#534317_2_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm49} is their inherent ability to almost completely prevent any phishing attempts. Password managers can accurately associate a user's credentials with the domains upon which they should be used. For example, a user's credentials on [examplebank.com]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional} should only ever be filled when the []{#534317_2_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_PB10 .pagebreak aria-label="10" role="doc-pagebreak" title="10"}browser is on [examplebank.com]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional}. Suppose the user receives a phishing email and is tricked to click on a link that takes them to [example-bank.com]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional} (pretending to be [examplebank.com]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional}). Even though [example-bank.com]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional} may appear to the user to be an exact replica of [examplebank.com]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional}, the password manager [extension]{#534317_2_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm50}[]{#534317_2_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm51} sees the URL is not [examplebank.com]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional}, and it will decline to fill the credentials. Of course, this doesn't prevent the user from entering their password manually, but nevertheless the URL matching feature is a robust point of defense against this type of attack.

Newer versions of password managers also support different forms of authentication: they can authenticate with [FIDO2/WebAuthn passkeys]{#534317_2_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm52}[]{#534317_2_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm53}[]{#534317_2_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm54}, store two-factor authentication secrets, and generate TOTP codes.
:::::

::: {#534317_2_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_Sec13 .section .Section3 .RenderAsSection3 aria-labelledby="Sec13Heading"}
#### Smart Writing Management Tools {#534317_2_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_Sec13Heading .Heading}

[Extensions]{#534317_2_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm55}[]{#534317_2_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm56} can be granted full access to the page DOM, and as a result, they are capable of viewing and managing page content -- including text inputs and their text content. Browser extensions that listen for keyboard events and clicks are afforded a real-time feed of exactly what the user is typing. This text content can be piped through to any number of text management tools, such as spell checkers, grammar checkers, and smart writing assistants. These extensions can then style the text and insert popup boxes into the page that provide the user an on-demand interactive interface to manage the smart text management tools alongside the actual HTML input fields.
:::

::: {#534317_2_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_Sec14 .section .Section3 .RenderAsSection3 aria-labelledby="Sec14Heading"}
#### Accessibility Tools {#534317_2_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_Sec14Heading .Heading}

The primary [way]{#534317_2_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm57} to consume content on the Internet is via text, and not everyone has an easy time reading a computer screen. Just as smart writing management extensions can read and change input text, [accessibility extensions]{#534317_2_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm58} can take the existing page text and format it in a way to make it more accessible to the user. This can take the form of automatically []{#534317_2_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_PB11 .pagebreak aria-label="11" role="doc-pagebreak" title="11"}translating page text, piping the page text to screen reading software, reformatting the page text to make it more legible (by increasing size, contrast, or choosing alternate fonts), or by offering quick access to a dictionary.
:::

::: {#534317_2_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_Sec15 .section .Section3 .RenderAsSection3 aria-labelledby="Sec15Heading"}
#### Content and Link Aggregators {#534317_2_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_Sec15Heading .Heading}

Plenty of web [users]{#534317_2_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm59} find the need to quickly and easily save web page URLs or partial content of those pages. [Aggregator extensions]{#534317_2_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm60} can add in a user interface to quickly grab URLs or portions of page HTML and save them, either locally or on a third-party server. Extensions also have access to the Bookmark API, allowing them to view and manage the browser's bookmarked sites.
:::

::: {#534317_2_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_Sec16 .section .Section3 .RenderAsSection3 aria-labelledby="Sec16Heading"}
#### Tab Management Tools {#534317_2_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_Sec16Heading .Heading}

Browser [extensions]{#534317_2_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm61} have access to an extensive tab management API, allowing them to perform a wide range of tab-centric actions that otherwise would require user action. Custom new tab behavior, reordering existing tabs, discarding stale tabs, and quickly accessing commonly used tabs are just a few of the [possible]{#534317_2_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm62} features these types of extensions provide.
:::

::::: {#534317_2_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_Sec17 .section .Section3 .RenderAsSection3 aria-labelledby="Sec17Heading"}
#### Screen Recording Tools {#534317_2_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_Sec17Heading .Heading}

Not only are browser [extensions]{#534317_2_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm63}[]{#534317_2_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm64} capable of viewing the page DOM, they are also able to make use of HTML5 APIs to *literally* see what your browser page looks like. These are useful for tools like screen recording, screen sharing, and capturing screenshots.

:::: {#534317_2_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_FPar2 .FormalPara .FormalParaRenderingStyle1 .ParaTypeImportant}
::: Heading
Note
:::

[]{#534317_2_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_PB12 .pagebreak aria-label="12" role="doc-pagebreak" title="12"}Extensions that use these HTML5 APIs still require the user to explicitly grant permission. Permission-gated APIs such as the Screen Capture API will generate a browser dialog box asking the user to grant access when a browser extension requests it.
::::
:::::

::: {#534317_2_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_Sec18 .section .Section3 .RenderAsSection3 aria-labelledby="Sec18Heading"}
#### Integrations for Software Platforms {#534317_2_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_Sec18Heading .Heading}

Plenty of software [platforms]{#534317_2_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm65} with a public API have seen fit to publish browser extensions that can directly connect to that API. This is especially useful when the content sent to those APIs is drawn from a web page the user is currently viewing. Sending content to remote file storage, automated access to a user's calendar, and accessing a third-party note-taking API are just a few of the ways that browser extensions use tight integration with a web [page]{#534317_2_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm66} to improve user experience.
:::

::::: {#534317_2_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_Sec19 .section .Section3 .RenderAsSection3 aria-labelledby="Sec19Heading"}
#### AI Assistants {#534317_2_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_Sec19Heading .Heading}

Artificial [intelligence]{#534317_2_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm67}[]{#534317_2_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm68}, especially [large language models (LLMs)]{#534317_2_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm69}, rapidly found a home in browser extensions. LLMs are well-suited for ingesting large [quantities]{#534317_2_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm70} of unstructured text, web pages contain large bodies of rich text, and browser extensions are an ideal pipeline for extracting page text.

:::: {#534317_2_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_Par47 .Para}
The form factor of these assistants varies widely, but the underlying [process]{#534317_2_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm71} is identical: web extension APIs are used to read text out of the page, the content is passed to and processed by the LLM, and the extension incorporates the LLM response into the browser. Here are a few examples of common AI [assistant]{#534317_2_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm72} tasks made possible with a browser extension and an LLM:[]{#534317_2_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_PB13 .pagebreak aria-label="13" role="doc-pagebreak" title="13"}

::: UnorderedList
-   A user generates a bullet point summary of a news article.

-   The user asks a question about how to write code based on the software documentation.

-   A user generates several options for a reply to a parent comment based on previous replies.

-   A user requests an analysis of the important email draft they are composing.

-   A user [requests]{#534317_2_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm73} a summary of a YouTube video based on the transcript.
:::
::::
:::::

::::: {#534317_2_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_Sec20 .section .Section3 .RenderAsSection3 aria-labelledby="Sec20Heading"}
#### Digital Currency Wallets {#534317_2_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_Sec20Heading .Heading}

:::: {#534317_2_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_Par53 .Para}
The world of digital [currencies]{#534317_2_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm74}[]{#534317_2_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm75} brings along with it unique ways of managing payments. The details vary between platforms and currencies, but the tasks that digital currency users must perform generally fall into one of the following buckets:

::: UnorderedList
-   **The user must read a long string of characters presented to them.** This might be a cryptocurrency address that they wish to send funds to or receive funds from. These strings are usually dozens of random characters, so typing or writing them down without making a mistake is impractical.

-   **The user must perform a digital handshake with a platform.** This takes different forms depending on the technology involved, but digital currency platforms will often support some form of "connection" with your wallet that allows for payments to be sent or actions to be signed or explicitly authorized.
:::
::::

[]{#534317_2_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_PB14 .pagebreak aria-label="14" role="doc-pagebreak" title="14"}Browser extensions are well-suited for both tasks. Their ability to automatically read page content means long character strings become trivial to manage. Furthermore, their separate execution context is a very [secure]{#534317_2_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm76}[]{#534317_2_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm77} place for developers to host a digital wallet.
:::::

::::: {#534317_2_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_Sec21 .section .Section3 .RenderAsSection3 aria-labelledby="Sec21Heading"}
#### Developer Tools {#534317_2_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_Sec21Heading .Heading}

::: {#534317_2_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_Par57 .Para}
[Modern web development]{#534317_2_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm78}[]{#534317_2_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm79} could not have happened without developer browser extensions. In the early days of the Web, developers were desperate for ways to more easily debug the websites they were building. One of the earliest solutions to this was a Firefox add-on released in 2006 called [Firebug]{#534317_2_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm80}, which allowed developers to inspect and tease apart the web page they were currently viewing (Figure [[1-2](#534317_2_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_Fig2)]{.InternalRef}). This greatly simplified their debug process for HTML, [JavaScript]{#534317_2_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm81}, and CSS, which, without the add-on, would incur a [patchwork]{#534317_2_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm82} mess of using print statements and viewing the raw page source.

<figure id="Fig2" class="Figure">
<div id="534317_2_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_MO2" class="MediaObject">
<img src="/app/temp/1773197898499_images/images/534317_2_En_1_Chapter/534317_2_En_1_Fig2_HTML.jpg" class="ImageStyle3" alt="Screenshot of a Wikipedia webpage displayed in a web browser. The page features the Wikipedia logo, navigation links, and sections like &quot;From today&#39;s featured article&quot; and &quot;In the news.&quot; The featured article discusses Empress Matilda, while the news section mentions topics like the West African Ebola outbreak. Below the webpage, a browser developer tools window is open, showing HTML code and CSS styles for the page." />
</div>
<figcaption><div class="CaptionContent">
<span class="CaptionNumber">Figure 1-2</span>
<p><span id="534317_2_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm83">Firebug</span> user interface (<span class="ExternalRef"><a href="https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Firebug_extension_screenshot.png"><span class="RefSource"><em>https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Firebug_extension_screenshot.png</em></span></a></span>, Source: Article: Wikipedia contributors; Firebug: Firebug contributors; screenshot: SteveSims, CC BY-SA 3.0, via Wikimedia Commons)</p>
</div></figcaption>
</figure>
:::

[]{#534317_2_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_PB15 .pagebreak aria-label="15" role="doc-pagebreak" title="15"}Browsers quickly [caught]{#534317_2_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm84} on that there was incredible demand for this sort of tooling, and they began to roll out native versions of debuggers that matched the feature set of Firebug. The Firebug extension became redundant and was shut down in 2017.

With the mass [adoption]{#534317_2_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm85} of single page applications like React, demand for developer tooling has followed in turn. The native browser debugger is not well-suited for debugging these sorts of applications, as the logic and architecture governing how the page renders are sequestered in large blobs of third-party JavaScript that is, at best, inscrutable.

To [address]{#534317_2_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm86} this issue, many single page application framework teams *also* release a companion browser extension. The browser extension is intimately familiar with the internals of how a specific single page application behaves and comes equipped with a broad toolkit that allows the developer to peer into the framework and understand what it is doing. This affords developers a much richer insight into what is happening on the page -- and more importantly, how to fix it.

::: {#534317_2_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_Par61 .Para}
Furthermore, browser [extensions]{#534317_2_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm87} have evolved to support direct integration into the browser's developer tools. Extensions can insert a custom *devtools page* into the browser's debug interface, interact with the inspected page, and debug network requests. They also have access to a custom DevTools API. The [React Developer Tools user interface]{#534317_2_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm88} embedded inside the browser's [developer tools]{#534317_2_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm89} is shown in Figure [[1-3](#534317_2_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_Fig3)]{.InternalRef}.[]{#534317_2_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_PB16 .pagebreak aria-label="16" role="doc-pagebreak" title="16"}

<figure id="Fig3" class="Figure">
<div id="534317_2_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_MO3" class="MediaObject">
<img src="/app/temp/1773197898499_images/images/534317_2_En_1_Chapter/534317_2_En_1_Fig3_HTML.jpg" class="ImageStyle3" alt="Screenshot of the WolframAlpha website displaying a search bar and various categories like Science &amp; Technology, Society &amp; Culture, and Everyday Life. The left panel shows mathematical topics, while the right panel displays browser developer tools with HTML code. The page header includes the WolframAlpha logo and a prompt to enter queries." />
</div>
<figcaption><div class="CaptionContent">
<span class="CaptionNumber">Figure 1-3</span>
<p><span id="534317_2_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm90">React Developer Tools user interface</span></p>
</div></figcaption>
</figure>
:::
:::::
::::::::::::::::::::::::::
:::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::

::: {#534317_2_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_Sec22 .section .Section1 .RenderAsSection1 aria-labelledby="Sec22Heading"}
## Summary {#534317_2_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_Sec22Heading .Heading}

Browser extensions are an essential component of modern computing. From the humble beginnings of the mainframe computer, they have evolved over decades into a veritable Swiss Army Knife for the modern browser.

The power of browser extensions is acutely expressed in the broad range of tooling that they enable. From powerful [security and privacy tools]{#534317_2_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm91} like ad blockers and password managers, to a suite of tools for in-browser writing, to a broad set of single page application developer tools, browser extensions enable and augment more of the modern web experience than most people might realize.

In the next chapter, we'll explore the fundamental elements that make up a browser extension and how each one functions within the browser. We'll examine the role of the manifest, background scripts, content scripts, popup pages, options pages, side panels, and devtools interfaces, along with how they interact with browser tabs, domains, and native APIs.
:::
::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::

[]{#534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml}
