::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::: Fulltext
[]{#534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_PB17 .pagebreak aria-label="17" role="doc-pagebreak" title="17"}As with most software platforms, browser extensions can be conceptually divided into a handful of discrete pieces. Developers new to the world of browser extensions may find the idiosyncrasies of these pieces tricky to internalize, as some of the behavior is redundant or not intuitive. Understanding how the pieces of browser extensions fit together is critical for becoming an expert at browser extension development.

:::: {#534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_FPar1 .FormalPara .FormalParaRenderingStyle1 .ParaTypeImportant}
::: Heading
Note
:::

This chapter will individually cover each piece of browser extensions broadly without diving into too much code. Subsequent chapters will probe each of these pieces in depth.
::::

::::::::::: {#534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_Sec1 .section .Section1 .RenderAsSection1 aria-labelledby="Sec1Heading"}
## The Browser Model {#534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_Sec1Heading .Heading}

::: {#534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_Par4 .Para}
Before exploring the various elements of browser extensions, let's first begin by reviewing the default behavior of a browser. Consider the diagram of a web browser with an open tab (Figure [[2-1](#534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_Fig1)]{.InternalRef}).

<figure id="Fig1" class="Figure">
<div id="534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_MO1" class="MediaObject">
<img src="/app/temp/1773197898499_images/images/534317_2_En_2_Chapter/534317_2_En_2_Fig1_HTML.jpg" class="ImageStyle3" alt="Diagram illustrating the structure of a web browser. At the top, &quot;Web Browser&quot; lists Chrome, Firefox, and Safari. Below, &quot;Browser Tab&quot; shows the URL buildingbrowserextensions.com. The diagram is divided into two main sections: &quot;JavaScript Runtime&quot; and &quot;Webpage.&quot; Under &quot;JavaScript Runtime,&quot; components include &quot;Browser/Web APIs&quot; (DOM, Fetch, Storage, Events), &quot;Engine&quot; (Memory Heap, Call Stack), and &quot;Event Loop&quot; (Task/Microtask Queues). Under &quot;Webpage,&quot; components include &quot;Rendering Engine&quot; (Blink, Webkit, Gecko), &quot;User Interface&quot; (HTML, CSS), and &quot;User Input&quot; (Mouse, Keyboard). At the bottom, &quot;Networking&quot; includes HTTP and WebSockets." />
</div>
<figcaption><div class="CaptionContent">
<span class="CaptionNumber">Figure 2-1</span>
<p><span id="534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm1">Simplified model</span> of the web browser</p>
</div></figcaption>
</figure>
:::

:::: {#534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_FPar2 .FormalPara .FormalParaRenderingStyle1 .ParaTypeImportant}
::: Heading
Note
:::

[]{#534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_PB18 .pagebreak aria-label="18" role="doc-pagebreak" title="18"}A basic understanding of how a web page is rendered, how JavaScript executes, and how the browser performs network requests is a prerequisite for browser extension development. If these topics are unfamiliar to you, an excellent list of resources can be found at [[[[https://developer.mozilla.org/docs/Learn_web_development]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional}]{.RefSource}](https://developer.mozilla.org/docs/Learn_web_development)]{.ExternalRef}.
::::

This diagram illustrates the high-level concepts at play when a web browser has a single tab directed to a URL. The web page renders in the [browser's primary user interface]{#534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm2} and has its own JavaScript runtime. Both the web page itself and the scripts within it make subsequent network []{#534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_PB19 .pagebreak aria-label="19" role="doc-pagebreak" title="19"}requests after the initial HTML loads: the page head will load assets like CSS, images, and additional scripts, and the scripts can execute additional requests to load assets and send and receive data.

:::::: {#534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_Sec2 .section .Section2 .RenderAsSection2 aria-labelledby="Sec2Heading"}
### Browser Tabs {#534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_Sec2Heading .Heading}

::: {#534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_Par7 .Para}
Let's extend the previous diagram to show when multiple [tabs]{#534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm3} are open in a browser (Figure [[2-2](#534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_Fig2)]{.InternalRef}).

<figure id="Fig2" class="Figure">
<div id="534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_MO2" class="MediaObject">
<img src="/app/temp/1773197898499_images/images/534317_2_En_2_Chapter/534317_2_En_2_Fig2_HTML.jpg" class="ImageStyle3" alt="Diagram illustrating the structure of a web browser. The top section labeled &quot;Web Browser&quot; includes examples: Chrome, Firefox, Safari. Below, two &quot;Browser Tab&quot; sections show URLs: buildingbrowserextensions.com and apress.com. Each tab contains &quot;JavaScript Runtime&quot; and &quot;Webpage&quot; components. The bottom section labeled &quot;Networking&quot; includes HTTP and WebSockets." />
</div>
<figcaption><div class="CaptionContent">
<span class="CaptionNumber">Figure 2-2</span>
<p>Web browser with multiple tabs</p>
</div></figcaption>
</figure>
:::

:::: {#534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_Par8 .Para}
Browsers will "sandbox" each tab, providing the tab with its own document object, JavaScript runtime, memory, and, depending on the browser, its own system thread and [process]{#534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm4}. Of course, there are a myriad of critical considerations when it comes to matching origins. Although each tab will be afforded separate runtimes, two separate tabs with matching origins are able to share resources. To name a few:

::: UnorderedList
-   Cookies

-   LocalStorage

-   IndexedDB

-   Shared Workers

-   Service Workers

-   HTTP [Cache]{#534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm5}
:::
::::
::::::

::: {#534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_Sec3 .section .Section2 .RenderAsSection2 aria-labelledby="Sec3Heading"}
### Same-Origin Policy {#534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_Sec3Heading .Heading}

[]{#534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_PB20 .pagebreak aria-label="20" role="doc-pagebreak" title="20"}Origins are also a consideration when applying the [Same-Origin Policy (SOP)]{#534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm6}[]{#534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm7}. In brief, the SOP is a set of security policies implemented by all browsers that control access to data between web applications. When two web applications have different origins, the browser automatically applies a set of restrictions to protect potentially sensitive information from an untrusted origin. Web developers typically encounter the SOP in three places: when sending network requests to foreign origins, when executing scripts from a different origin, and when accessing storage [APIs]{#534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm8}[]{#534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm9} from different origins.
:::
:::::::::::

:::::::::::::::: {#534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_Sec4 .section .Section1 .RenderAsSection1 aria-labelledby="Sec4Heading"}
## The Browser Extension Model {#534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_Sec4Heading .Heading}

From the perspective of the web page, browser extensions are a mostly invisible supplemental entity. They execute JavaScript in their own runtime, they render pages in their own [sandboxed contexts]{#534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm10}, and they have access to their own separate set of APIs. For the most part, a web page will have no idea a browser extension is installed or what it is doing.

::: {#534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_Par17 .Para}
Consider the diagram of a browser with an extension installed as in Figure [[2-3](#534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_Fig3)]{.InternalRef}.

<figure id="Fig3" class="Figure">
<div id="534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_MO3" class="MediaObject">
<img src="/app/temp/1773197898499_images/images/534317_2_En_2_Chapter/534317_2_En_2_Fig3_HTML.jpg" class="ImageStyle3" alt="Diagram illustrating the architecture of a web browser and its extensions. The top layer shows &quot;Web Browser&quot; with examples: Chrome, Firefox, Safari. Below are &quot;Browser Tabs&quot; with URLs: buildingbrowserextensions.com and apress.com. The next layer includes &quot;JavaScript Runtime&quot; and &quot;Webpage.&quot; The &quot;Browser Extension&quot; layer features &quot;Browser Extension Explorer.&quot; Underneath, &quot;JavaScript Runtimes&quot; lists background script, content scripts, and popup/options/devtools. &quot;HTML User Interfaces&quot; includes popup page, options page, and devtools pages. &quot;Native User Interface&quot; mentions native APIs, toolbar/context menu, and omnibox. The bottom layer is &quot;Networking&quot; with HTTP and WebSockets." />
</div>
<figcaption><div class="CaptionContent">
<span class="CaptionNumber">Figure 2-3</span>
<p><span id="534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm11">Web browser with multiple tabs</span> and an extension installed</p>
</div></figcaption>
</figure>
:::

Let's examine each of the important concepts shown in this diagram.

::::::: {#534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_Sec5 .section .Section2 .RenderAsSection2 aria-labelledby="Sec5Heading"}
### Independent JavaScript Pages and Runtimes {#534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_Sec5Heading .Heading}

:::: {#534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_Par19 .Para}
[]{#534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_PB21 .pagebreak aria-label="21" role="doc-pagebreak" title="21"}Once installed, browser [extensions]{#534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm12} can offer multiple custom HTML user interfaces in several places: the popup page, the options page, the side panel page, and the devtools pages. These pages behave like normal web pages in nearly every way:

::: UnorderedList
-   Each page is rendered in the same way as a normal web page, with load events and a conventional document object.

-   Each page is provided a dedicated native browser container and completely separated from all other web pages.

-   Each interface is provided its own JavaScript runtime.
:::
::::

[]{#534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_PB22 .pagebreak aria-label="22" role="doc-pagebreak" title="22"}Background scripts and content scripts are provided their own [runtime]{#534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm13}, but they do not exist as a stand-alone user interface.

:::: {#534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_FPar3 .FormalPara .FormalParaRenderingStyle1 .ParaTypeImportant}
::: Heading
Note
:::

The details of popup, options, side panel, devtools, background script, and content script are covered later in this chapter.
::::
:::::::

::::::: {#534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_Sec6 .section .Section2 .RenderAsSection2 aria-labelledby="Sec6Heading"}
### Native APIs and User Interfaces {#534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_Sec6Heading .Heading}

:::: {#534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_Par25 .Para}
Browser [extensions]{#534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm14} have access to a suite of native browser APIs that allow them to control the behavior, appearance, and data of the browser. Some examples:

::: UnorderedList
-   **Tabs API:** Manages browser tabs, enabling extensions to create, update, rearrange, or close tabs

-   **Bookmarks API:** Allows extensions to interact with bookmarks by adding, removing, or organizing them

-   **Downloads API:** Grants extensions control over file downloads, such as initiating, pausing, resuming, or canceling

-   **Context Menu API:** Permits extensions to add interactive options to the native right click menu
:::
::::

:::: {.FormalPara .FormalParaRenderingStyle1 .ParaTypeImportant}
::: Heading
Note
:::

These APIs will be covered in depth in Chapter [[[9]{.RefSource}](#534317_2_En_9_Chapter.xhtml)]{.ExternalRef}.
::::

[]{#534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_PB23 .pagebreak aria-label="23" role="doc-pagebreak" title="23"}Furthermore, browser extensions are afforded several ways to integrate with the browser user interface itself. This includes the ability to manage how the toolbar icon appears and behaves, specifying behavior for the URL bar omnibox [interface]{#534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm15}, and defining what should appear in the extension context menu when it is opened.
:::::::

::: {#534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_Sec7 .section .Section2 .RenderAsSection2 aria-labelledby="Sec7Heading"}
### Tab and Domain Access {#534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_Sec7Heading .Heading}

Unlike web [pages]{#534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm16}, which are restricted to a single domain and tab, browser extensions live in the browser itself. When given the necessary permissions, browser extensions can interact with multiple tabs on multiple domains. This means that a browser extension is able to inspect and manage a portion or the entirety of a browser's open pages.

Browser extensions are also not required to use a web page at all. Extensions are adequately equipped to exist as stand-alone pieces of software that live only in the browser itself. Of course, some aspects of browser extensions like content scripts and devtools [interfaces]{#534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm17} are only useful when manipulating web pages.
:::

::: {#534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_Sec8 .section .Section2 .RenderAsSection2 aria-labelledby="Sec8Heading"}
### Observing and Intercepting Network Requests {#534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_Sec8Heading .Heading}

Normally, [network]{#534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm18} requests dispatched from a web page are handed off directly to the browser. Browser extensions can be given permission to act as a network request shim, where requests dispatched to the page will be preprocessed by the extension before being passed off to the browser. This means that the extension can add rules for conditionally modifying requests, or even blocking them outright. It also means that the extension can see a stream of traffic in an out of the page in real time.
:::
::::::::::::::::

:::::::::::::::::::::::::::: {#534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_Sec9 .section .Section1 .RenderAsSection1 aria-labelledby="Sec9Heading"}
## Elements of Browser Extensions {#534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_Sec9Heading .Heading}

[]{#534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_PB24 .pagebreak aria-label="24" role="doc-pagebreak" title="24"}The previous section covered browser extensions as a holistic entity. This is useful when trying to get a grasp on the concept as a whole. However, as alluded to in the introduction to this chapter, browser extensions exist as a collection of semi-independent elements. This section will examine each of these elements at a high level.

:::::::::: {#534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_Sec10 .section .Section2 .RenderAsSection2 aria-labelledby="Sec10Heading"}
### Extension Manifest {#534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_Sec10Heading .Heading}

:::: {#534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_Par36 .Para}
The [manifest]{#534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm19}[]{#534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm20} is the rulebook for the browser extension. Every extension is required to provide this manifest as JSON data in a [manifest.json]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional} file. Some examples of what is contained in the manifest:

::: UnorderedList
-   Public information such as the extension name, description, semantic version, icons, and author

-   Pointers to entrypoint files for background scripts, popup pages, options pages, side panel pages, devtools pages, and content scripts

-   Requirements and [configurations]{#534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm21}[]{#534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm22} the extension needs to properly operate, such as permissions, content security policies, cross-origin policies, and minimum browser versions

-   Pattern-match rule sets for managing network requests, enabled domains, and resources the extension wishes to inject into the page via content script

-   Miscellaneous extension-specific options like enabling offline and incognito and [keyboard]{#534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm23}[]{#534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm24} shortcuts
:::
::::

:::: {#534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_FPar4 .FormalPara .FormalParaRenderingStyle1 .ParaTypeImportant}
::: Heading
Note
:::

[]{#534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_PB25 .pagebreak aria-label="25" role="doc-pagebreak" title="25"}The specifics of how to write a [manifest.json]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional} file can be found in Chapter [[[5]{.RefSource}](#534317_2_En_5_Chapter.xhtml)]{.ExternalRef}.
::::

::::: {#534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_Sec11 .section .Section3 .RenderAsSection3 aria-labelledby="Sec11Heading"}
#### Manifest V2 and V3 {#534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_Sec11Heading .Heading}

The world of browser extensions is in the final phases of a yearslong transitional period between two versions of the [manifest.json]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional} file: version 2 [(v2)]{#534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm25} and version 3 (v3). The new Manifest [V3]{#534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm26} (often referred to as "MV3") significantly alters how extensions execute, and it [changes]{#534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm27}[]{#534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm28} the APIs that they can access.

:::: {#534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_FPar5 .FormalPara .FormalParaRenderingStyle1 .ParaTypeImportant}
::: Heading
Note
:::

At the time of the writing of this book, [Chromium browsers]{#534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm29} have effectively phased out Manifest V2 extensions entirely. For better or worse, Manifest V3 is now the dominant form of extensions. As a result, this edition of *Building Browser Extensions* offers minimal coverage of Manifest V2 concepts.
::::
:::::
::::::::::

::::: {#534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_Sec12 .section .Section2 .RenderAsSection2 aria-labelledby="Sec12Heading"}
### Background Scripts {#534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_Sec12Heading .Heading}

The primary purpose of [background scripts]{#534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm30}[]{#534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm31} is to handle browser events. These events might be extension lifecycle events such as an install or uninstall, or they might be browser events like navigating to a web page or adding a new bookmark.

Although background scripts are provided their own JavaScript runtime, they do not exist in isolation. They can access the WebExtensions APIs and therefore are capable of performing actions such as exchanging messages with other parts of the extension, exchanging messages with other extensions, or programmatically injecting content [scripts]{#534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm32}[]{#534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm33} into a page.

[]{#534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_PB26 .pagebreak aria-label="26" role="doc-pagebreak" title="26"}Background scripts transformed significantly between Manifest V2 and Manifest V3. In Manifest V2, the background script had the option of being either "persistent," meaning the background script is initialized exactly once and lives in memory until the browser is closed, or nonpersistent, where the background script exists as an "event page" that is initialized on demand whenever a relevant browser event occurs. In Manifest V3, background scripts exist as service [workers]{#534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm34}[]{#534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm35}, which largely replicate the behavior of the Manifest V2 nonpersistent background scripts.

:::: {#534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_FPar6 .FormalPara .FormalParaRenderingStyle1 .ParaTypeImportant}
::: Heading
Note
:::

Chapter [[[6]{.RefSource}](#534317_2_En_6_Chapter.xhtml)]{.ExternalRef} includes more details on background scripts.
::::
:::::

::: {#534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_Sec13 .section .Section2 .RenderAsSection2 aria-labelledby="Sec13Heading"}
### Extension UI Pages {#534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_Sec13Heading .Heading}

[*Extension UI pages*]{#534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm36}[]{#534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm37} are custom interface elements that enable browser extensions to interact with users. Although written and rendered like web pages, they are integrated directly into the browser. These pages offer extended functionality through the WebExtensions API, allowing extensions to manage settings, interact with web content, and display contextual information beyond standard web pages.
:::

::: {#534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_Sec14 .section .Section2 .RenderAsSection2 aria-labelledby="Sec14Heading"}
### The Popup Page {#534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_Sec14Heading .Heading}

The [popup page]{#534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm38}[]{#534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm39} is a native browser container that browser extensions can use to display a custom user interface. The popup page behaves as a dialog box that "pops" over the web page when the user clicks the extension toolbar button. The popup page will always appear directly below the toolbar. Because it can be quickly accessed and can show over any web page, the popup page typically contains content that users need easy access to. An example of a popup page is shown in Figure [[2-9](#534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_Fig9)]{.InternalRef}.

[]{#534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_PB27 .pagebreak aria-label="27" role="doc-pagebreak" title="27"}Popup pages are rendered just like regular web pages, but their dialog-like nature means they are disposable: the popup will be freshly rendered each time the popup is opened and unloaded when the popup is closed. Like background scripts, popup pages can access the WebExtensions [API]{#534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm40}[]{#534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm41}, meaning they have the capabilities not available to regular web pages.
:::

::::: {#534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_Sec15 .section .Section2 .RenderAsSection2 aria-labelledby="Sec15Heading"}
### The Options Page {#534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_Sec15Heading .Heading}

The [options page]{#534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm42}[]{#534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm43} is a native browser container that browser extensions can use to display a custom user interface. The options page behaves as a stand-alone web page that opens when the user clicks "Options" in the extension toolbar context menu. An example of opening an options page is shown in Figure [[2-13](#534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_Fig13)]{.InternalRef}.

The "options" name is misleading: this page is not restricted to just showing options for the extension. Like the popup page, this is a fully featured web page with access to the WebExtensions API, meaning you are [capable]{#534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm44}[]{#534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm45} of using it as a full web application.

:::: {#534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_FPar7 .FormalPara .FormalParaRenderingStyle1 .ParaTypeImportant}
::: Heading
Note
:::

Refer to Chapter [[[7]{.RefSource}](#534317_2_En_7_Chapter.xhtml)]{.ExternalRef} for more details on how these pages work and how to use them.
::::
:::::

::::: {#534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_Sec16 .section .Section2 .RenderAsSection2 aria-labelledby="Sec16Heading"}
### The Side Panel {#534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_Sec16Heading .Heading}

The [side panel]{#534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm46}[]{#534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm47} is a native browser container that Chromium extensions can use to display a custom user interface within a sidebar alongside the main content of a web page. The side panel behaves as a resizable, vertical space that opens on the side of the browser window, usually on the right or left, providing users with persistent access to tools and information while they navigate. An example of a side panel is shown in Figure [[2-12](#534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_Fig12)]{.InternalRef}.

[]{#534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_PB28 .pagebreak aria-label="28" role="doc-pagebreak" title="28"}Side panels are ideal for content that users need to reference or interact with frequently as they browse, such as notes, to-do lists, or contextual information related to the open web page. Unlike popup pages, [side panels]{#534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm48}[]{#534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm49} persist across multiple pages and can remain open until manually closed by the user. Similar to options and popup pages, side panels have access to the WebExtensions API, allowing developers to leverage enhanced capabilities not available to standard web pages.

:::: {#534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_FPar8 .FormalPara .FormalParaRenderingStyle1 .ParaTypeImportant}
::: Heading
Note
:::

Chromium extensions use side panels, whereas Firefox extensions use sidebars. These elements are defined differently in their respective manifests but are nearly identical in both appearance and behavior.
::::
:::::

::::: {#534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_Sec17 .section .Section2 .RenderAsSection2 aria-labelledby="Sec17Heading"}
### Content Scripts {#534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_Sec17Heading .Heading}

The term [*content script*]{#534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm50}[]{#534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm51} refers to any script or style injected into a web page by a browser extension. JavaScript can be injected either declaratively via the manifest or programmatically from an extension page or background script using the WebExtensions API. Content scripts may include JavaScript, CSS, or both.

[Injected JavaScript]{#534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm52} runs in one of two environments. By default, it operates in a separate execution context, isolated from the web page's own scripts. In this setup, the script can fully interact with the DOM but cannot access JavaScript [variables]{#534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm53}[]{#534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm54} or functions defined by the page. Alternatively, extensions can inject scripts into the same execution context as the page itself, allowing direct access to its variables and functions. This mode enables deeper integration, such as modifying page behavior or hooking into in-page logic.

[]{#534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_PB29 .pagebreak aria-label="29" role="doc-pagebreak" title="29"}While [content scripts]{#534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm55}[]{#534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm56} can fully manipulate the DOM and implement features like in-page widgets, they have limited access to the WebExtensions API. They cannot, for example, access privileged APIs available to the popup, options page, or background script. However, they can communicate with these privileged components via messaging, allowing them to delegate restricted actions. Examples of content script widgets can be [found]{#534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm57}[]{#534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm58} in Figures [[2-5](#534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_Fig5)]{.InternalRef}, [[2-7](#534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_Fig7)]{.InternalRef}, and [[2-10](#534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_Fig10)]{.InternalRef}.

:::: {#534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_FPar9 .FormalPara .FormalParaRenderingStyle1 .ParaTypeImportant}
::: Heading
Note
:::

Refer to Chapter [[[8]{.RefSource}](#534317_2_En_8_Chapter.xhtml)]{.ExternalRef} for more details on how content scripts work and how to use them.
::::
:::::

::::: {#534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_Sec18 .section .Section2 .RenderAsSection2 aria-labelledby="Sec18Heading"}
### Devtools Panels and Sidebars {#534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_Sec18Heading .Heading}

[Devtools panel]{#534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm59} and sidebar [pages]{#534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm60} are native browser containers that browser extensions can use to display a custom user interface. The devtools interfaces behave as panes nested in the native browser's developer tools when the user selects their corresponding tabs in the developer tools interface, shown in Figure [[2-11](#534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_Fig11)]{.InternalRef}.

Like the extension UI [pages]{#534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm61}, this is a fully featured web page, but like content scripts, it has limited access to the [WebExtensions API]{#534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm62}. However, it is provided access to the additional Devtools API that can be used to perform actions like inspecting the page and observing the page's [network]{#534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm63} traffic.

:::: {#534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_FPar10 .FormalPara .FormalParaRenderingStyle1 .ParaTypeImportant}
::: Heading
Note
:::

Refer to Chapter [[[9]{.RefSource}](#534317_2_En_9_Chapter.xhtml)]{.ExternalRef} for more details on how these pages work and how to use them.
::::
:::::
::::::::::::::::::::::::::::

:::::::::: {#534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_Sec19 .section .Section1 .RenderAsSection1 aria-labelledby="Sec19Heading"}
## Extension Elements in Action {#534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_Sec19Heading .Heading}

[]{#534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_PB30 .pagebreak aria-label="30" role="doc-pagebreak" title="30"}To aid in understanding how all these elements fit together, let's examine how these pieces fit into a handful of popular browser extensions. Based on what we know about how the various elements of browser extensions behave, we can understand how the extension is accomplishing these user interfaces even without having access to the code bases.

:::: {#534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_Sec20 .section .Section2 .RenderAsSection2 aria-labelledby="Sec20Heading"}
### Bitwarden {#534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_Sec20Heading .Heading}

[Bitwarden]{#534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm64}[]{#534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm65} is a browser extension that can securely store and autofill a user's passwords, as well as record new passwords when they are submitted. Its UI includes a popup menu from the browser toolbar for quick access to stored logins, a vault view for managing all saved passwords, and a password generator tool for creating strong, unique passwords. Bitwarden also features inline autofill buttons that appear directly in login fields, allowing users to fill in credentials without leaving the web page.

::: {#534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_Par67 .Para}
[Bitwarden]{#534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm66}[]{#534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm67} uses a content script to find and fill authentication form fields, as well as to render widgets for managing passwords from within the form fields (Figure [[2-4](#534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_Fig4)]{.InternalRef}). Bitwarden also watches traffic in and out of the browser for requests that set or update passwords and records the request payload in your encrypted account credential list (Figure [[2-5](#534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_Fig5)]{.InternalRef}). [Bitwarden]{#534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm68}[]{#534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm69} also adds options to the right click menu (Figure [[2-6](#534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_Fig6)]{.InternalRef}). This is accomplished with the context menu API.

<figure id="Fig4" class="Figure">
<div id="534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_MO4" class="MediaObject">
<img src="/app/temp/1773197898499_images/images/534317_2_En_2_Chapter/534317_2_En_2_Fig4_HTML.jpg" class="ImageStyle4" alt="Screenshot of the REI Co-op login page. The page prompts users to sign in or create an account, with fields for entering an email and password. A dropdown suggests email options like &quot;rei.com&quot; and &quot;example@gmail.com.&quot; There are links for &quot;Forgot password?&quot; and terms of use. The page header includes navigation links such as Shop, REI Outlet, and Membership. A feedback link is at the bottom." />
</div>
<figcaption><div class="CaptionContent">
<span class="CaptionNumber">Figure 2-4</span>
<p><span id="534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm70">Bitwarden</span><span id="534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm71"></span> browser extension with the content script widget used for filling form values</p>
</div></figcaption>
</figure>

<figure id="Fig5" class="Figure">
<div id="534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_MO5" class="MediaObject">
<img src="/app/temp/1773197898499_images/images/534317_2_En_2_Chapter/534317_2_En_2_Fig5_HTML.jpg" class="ImageStyle4" alt="Screenshot of an REI membership webpage. The page announces, &quot;Success! Now boost the power of your account with a lifetime membership.&quot; It offers a $30 one-time membership with benefits like an annual Co-op Member Reward and discounts. Options to add the membership to the cart or decline are available. The page includes navigation links, a search bar, and a feedback button. An illustration of people with outdoor gear is shown." />
</div>
<figcaption><div class="CaptionContent">
<span class="CaptionNumber">Figure 2-5</span>
<p><span id="534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm72">Bitwarden browser extension</span><span id="534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm73"></span> showing the content script widget after it detects a network request that looks like a signup</p>
</div></figcaption>
</figure>

<figure id="Fig6" class="Figure">
<div id="534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_MO6" class="MediaObject">
<img src="/app/temp/1773197898499_images/images/534317_2_En_2_Chapter/534317_2_En_2_Fig6_HTML.jpg" class="ImageStyle3" alt="Screenshot of the REI.com login page. The page includes fields for entering an email and password, with options to sign in or create an account. A context menu is open on the left, showing options from a password manager, including &quot;Autofill login&quot; and &quot;Copy username.&quot; The URL bar displays &quot;rei.com/login.&quot; The page footer has a feedback link and small tree icons." />
</div>
<figcaption><div class="CaptionContent">
<span class="CaptionNumber">Figure 2-6</span>
<p>Bitwarden’s <span id="534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm74">context</span><span id="534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm75"></span> menu options</p>
</div></figcaption>
</figure>
:::
::::

:::: {#534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_Sec21 .section .Section2 .RenderAsSection2 aria-labelledby="Sec21Heading"}
### Grammarly {#534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_Sec21Heading .Heading}

[]{#534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_PB32 .pagebreak aria-label="32" role="doc-pagebreak" title="32"}[Grammarly]{#534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm76}[]{#534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm77} is a browser extension that observes the text a user types into the browser and automatically offers suggestions on how to fix or improve it. Its UI includes a popup card directly over text fields, showing quick suggestions for spelling, grammar, and style improvements. Grammarly also integrates a tone detector that appears in real time to suggest adjustments for clarity, engagement, and formality, and provides a detailed insights panel for tracking writing performance across multiple pieces of content.

::: {#534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_Par69 .Para}
[]{#534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_PB33 .pagebreak aria-label="33" role="doc-pagebreak" title="33"}Similar to how [Bitwarden]{#534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm78}[]{#534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm79} detects authentication form fields, Grammarly uses a content script to intelligently detect when the user is typing content into the page, parses that content, and renders suggestions on top of that content (Figure [[2-7](#534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_Fig7)]{.InternalRef}). The extension is also able to collate all active suggestions into the unified content script widget (Figure [[2-8](#534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_Fig8)]{.InternalRef}). [Grammarly]{#534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm80}[]{#534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm81} also offers toggles for extension settings in the popup page (Figure [[2-9](#534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_Fig9)]{.InternalRef}).

<figure id="Fig7" class="Figure">
<div id="534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_MO7" class="MediaObject">
<img src="/app/temp/1773197898499_images/images/534317_2_En_2_Chapter/534317_2_En_2_Fig7_HTML.jpg" class="ImageStyle3" alt="Screenshot of a webpage from JustNotepad.com. The page features a text editor with a question, &quot;What is browser extension?&quot; and a suggestion box from Grammarly offering corrections. The page includes options to create a temporary link, add new text, or delete drafts. A notification mentions saving drafts in browser storage. The URL is visible at the top." />
</div>
<figcaption><div class="CaptionContent">
<span class="CaptionNumber">Figure 2-7</span>
<p><span id="534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm82">Grammarly browser extension</span><span id="534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm83"></span> showing the content script widget rendered alongside a detected grammatical error</p>
</div></figcaption>
</figure>

<figure id="Fig8" class="Figure">
<div id="534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_MO8" class="MediaObject">
<img src="/app/temp/1773197898499_images/images/534317_2_En_2_Chapter/534317_2_En_2_Fig8_HTML.jpg" class="ImageStyle4" alt="Screenshot of a webpage from JustNotepad.com displaying a text editor with the question &quot;What is browser extension?&quot; A grammar tool suggests correcting the text to &quot;What is a browser extension?&quot; with options to accept or dismiss the suggestion. The page includes buttons for creating a new note and deleting drafts, with a note about saving drafts in browser storage." />
</div>
<figcaption><div class="CaptionContent">
<span class="CaptionNumber">Figure 2-8</span>
<p><span id="534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm84">Grammarly browser extension</span><span id="534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm85"></span> showing the collated content script widget rendered in the corner of a textual input</p>
</div></figcaption>
</figure>

<figure id="Fig9" class="Figure">
<div id="534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_MO9" class="MediaObject">
<img src="/app/temp/1773197898499_images/images/534317_2_En_2_Chapter/534317_2_En_2_Fig9_HTML.jpg" class="ImageStyle4" alt="Screenshot of a webpage from JustNotepad.com displaying a text editor with the text &quot;What is browser extension?&quot; A Grammarly extension sidebar is open on the right, showing settings for writing suggestions and generative AI features. The page includes options to delete drafts and a timestamp indicating the draft was saved on October 27, 2024." />
</div>
<figcaption><div class="CaptionContent">
<span class="CaptionNumber">Figure 2-9</span>
<p><span id="534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm86">Grammarly browser extension</span><span id="534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm87"></span> showing the popup for configuring overall extension behavior</p>
</div></figcaption>
</figure>
:::
::::

:::: {#534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_Sec22 .section .Section2 .RenderAsSection2 aria-labelledby="Sec22Heading"}
### React Developer Tools {#534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_Sec22Heading .Heading}

::: {#534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_Par70 .Para}
[]{#534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_PB35 .pagebreak aria-label="35" role="doc-pagebreak" title="35"}[React Developer Tools]{#534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm88}[]{#534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm89} is a browser extension that allows the user to understand how a React application is rendering in the page. The browser extension can analyze the web page to detect if it is a React application. If it is, the toolbar icon will indicate as much, and the popup page informs the user what type of React application is present (Figure [[2-10](#534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_Fig10)]{.InternalRef}). The extension uses the Devtools API to create a custom panel in the browser's developer tools interface. It parses the page content and [unpacks]{#534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm90}[]{#534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm91} it into a browsable component tree inside the panel page (Figure [[2-11](#534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_Fig11)]{.InternalRef}).

<figure id="Fig10" class="Figure">
<div id="534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_MO10" class="MediaObject">
<img src="/app/temp/1773197898499_images/images/534317_2_En_2_Chapter/534317_2_En_2_Fig10_HTML.jpg" class="ImageStyle3" alt="Screenshot of the React website homepage. The page features the React logo and the text &quot;React: The library for web and native user interfaces.&quot; There are buttons labeled &quot;Learn React&quot; and &quot;API Reference.&quot; A notification in the top right corner states, &quot;This page is using the production build of React.&quot; The URL in the browser is &quot;react.dev.&quot;" />
</div>
<figcaption><div class="CaptionContent">
<span class="CaptionNumber">Figure 2-10</span>
<p>React Developer Tools <span id="534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm92">browser</span><span id="534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm93"></span> extension showing the popup widget</p>
</div></figcaption>
</figure>

<figure id="Fig11" class="Figure">
<div id="534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_MO11" class="MediaObject">
<img src="/app/temp/1773197898499_images/images/534317_2_En_2_Chapter/534317_2_En_2_Fig11_HTML.jpg" class="ImageStyle3" alt="Screenshot of the React website and developer tools. The left side shows the React homepage with the logo, version 18.3.1, and text: &quot;React, The library for web and native user interfaces.&quot; Buttons labeled &quot;Learn React&quot; and &quot;API Reference&quot; are visible. Below, text reads: &quot;Create user interfaces from components.&quot; The right side displays the browser&#39;s developer tools, highlighting a component tree with elements like &quot;ContextMenuProvider&quot; and &quot;Anonymous.&quot; The URL in the address bar is &quot;react.dev.&quot;" />
</div>
<figcaption><div class="CaptionContent">
<span class="CaptionNumber">Figure 2-11</span>
<p>React Developer Tools <span id="534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm94">browser</span><span id="534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm95"></span> extension showing a devtools panel page with element highlighted</p>
</div></figcaption>
</figure>
:::
::::

::: {#534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_Sec23 .section .Section2 .RenderAsSection2 aria-labelledby="Sec23Heading"}
### Sider.ai {#534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_Sec23Heading .Heading}

[]{#534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_PB36 .pagebreak aria-label="36" role="doc-pagebreak" title="36"}[Sider.ai]{#534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm96}[]{#534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm97} is a browser extension that enhances the web browsing experience by offering AI-powered insights and contextual information alongside the content users view. Its UI includes a responsive side panel that users can open to access summaries, explanations, and related resources for any web page, making it ideal for research and content exploration. The extension's options page offers customization settings, allowing users to control which types of insights are displayed, adjust language preferences, and personalize the [interface]{#534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm98}[]{#534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm99} to better suit their research or reading habits.

<figure id="Fig12" class="Figure">
<div id="534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_MO12" class="MediaObject">
<img src="/app/temp/1773197898499_images/images/534317_2_En_2_Chapter/534317_2_En_2_Fig12_HTML.jpg" class="ImageStyle3" alt="Screenshot of a Google homepage with a dark theme. The search bar is centered below the Google logo, with buttons for &quot;Google Search&quot; and &quot;I&#39;m Feeling Lucky.&quot; The top right corner shows options for Gmail and Images. A sidebar on the right displays a chat interface with a greeting, &quot;Hi, How can I assist you today?&quot; and suggestions for tasks like writing a Shakespearean-style sonnet about smartphones. The bottom of the page includes links for Advertising, Business, and Privacy." />
</div>
<figcaption><div class="CaptionContent">
<span class="CaptionNumber">Figure 2-12</span>
<p>The Sider.ai side panel interface functioning as an AI chat interface</p>
</div></figcaption>
</figure>

<figure id="Fig13" class="Figure">
<div id="534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_MO13" class="MediaObject">
<img src="/app/temp/1773197898499_images/images/534317_2_En_2_Chapter/534317_2_En_2_Fig13_HTML.jpg" class="ImageStyle3" alt="Screenshot of the Sider ChatGPT Sidebar options page in a web browser. The page displays the &quot;General&quot; settings section, explaining how to access ChatGPT using three methods: Sider, ChatGPT Webapp, and OpenAI API key. The Sider option is recommended and selected, with a note about upgrading to premium for increased daily credit limits. The language is set to English, and there are navigation options on the left, including Search Page, Sidebar, and others. The browser tabs and URL bar are visible at the top." />
</div>
<figcaption><div class="CaptionContent">
<span class="CaptionNumber">Figure 2-13</span>
<p>The Sider.ai options page used to configure overall <span id="534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm100">extension</span><span id="534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm101"></span> behavior</p>
</div></figcaption>
</figure>
:::
::::::::::

::: {#534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_Sec24 .section .Section1 .RenderAsSection1 aria-labelledby="Sec24Heading"}
## Summary {#534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_Sec24Heading .Heading}

[]{#534317_2_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_PB38 .pagebreak aria-label="38" role="doc-pagebreak" title="38"}In this chapter, we examined the fundamental components that make up a browser extension, including the execution model of modern browsers and how extensions integrate into that environment. We reviewed how extensions operate independently of web pages, with their own runtimes, user interface surfaces, and access to privileged browser APIs.

We introduced each major extension element, such as the manifest, background scripts, popup pages, side panels, options pages, devtools interfaces, and content scripts. We then explained how these elements interact with both web content and the browser itself. We also compared execution contexts, permission models, and real-world implementations found in popular extensions.

The next chapter will take you through a crash course covering how to build and install a basic browser extension.
:::
:::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::

[]{#534317_2_En_3_Chapter.xhtml}
