[Extension UIs]{#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm4}[]{#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm5} are nearly identical to a traditional website, and therefore, they are a natural choice for sending network requests when building a browser extension. They enjoy access to the bulk of the WebExtensions API while still being isolated from host pages. If your entire user interface is built inside one of these, you will not have any problem sending network requests or using any form of authentication, be it cookie auth, JSON Web Token [(JWT)]{#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm6} auth, or OAuth.

[]{#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_PB374 .pagebreak aria-label="374" role="doc-pagebreak" title="374"}An options page or side panel is a good option as a supplement for browser extensions that wish to rely heavily on a background script but also wish to execute long-running requests. Unless the options page is rendered as a modal ([open_in_tab=false]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional}), it will enjoy the same lifecycle as a browser tab, so long-running requests can be safely dispatched without risk of early termination. Popup and devtools pages are expected to be closed early and often. Thus, they are not a good vehicle for long-running [network]{#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm7}[]{#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm8} requests.
:::

::: {#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_Sec4 .section .Section2 .RenderAsSection2 aria-labelledby="Sec4Heading"}
### Content Scripts {#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_Sec4Heading .Heading}

[Content scripts]{#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm9} provide a unique way to send network requests in a browser extension. Since they run within the context of the host page, they are subject to the same cross-origin restrictions as the page itself. This means that if a content script needs to communicate with your extension's backend server, the request will likely need to be routed through the background script, which has broader network permissions.

One key advantage of making network requests from a content script is that they are treated as originating from the host page. As a result, they can access and use the host page's cookies, allowing requests to be sent as the authenticated user. Because the host page has access to anything injected into the DOM or shared through global APIs, content scripts should not handle authentication credentials for resources that are not explicitly controlled by the host page.

Additionally, [content scripts]{#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm10} are inherently tied to the lifecycle of the host page. Long-running network requests, such as those made via WebSockets or streaming APIs, depend on the page remaining open. If the user navigates away or closes the tab, any ongoing requests initiated by the content script will be abruptly terminated. To maintain persistent communication, it's often better to delegate such tasks to the background script, which is not tied to the page lifecycle.
:::

::::: {#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_Sec5 .section .Section2 .RenderAsSection2 aria-labelledby="Sec5Heading"}
### Background Scripts {#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_Sec5Heading .Heading}

[]{#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_PB375 .pagebreak aria-label="375" role="doc-pagebreak" title="375"}[Background scripts]{#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm11} are the backbone of networking in a browser extension. Unlike extension UIs or content scripts, background scripts operate independently of any web page, making them the best choice for handling API requests, managing authentication flows, and routing messages between different parts of the extension. They also have broader network permissions than content scripts, allowing direct cross-origin requests to external servers.

:::: {#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_Par11 .Para}
Manifest V3 background scripts are service workers, which present several key networking and authentication limitations:

::: UnorderedList
-   Since background scripts are service workers, they cannot access cookies directly. Requests sent from a background script do not include the user's session cookies unless handled explicitly via the fetch API with [credentials: \"include\"]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional} (which still won't work for [HttpOnly]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional} cookies).

-   The browser does not consider active network requests when deciding to terminate a background service worker. This means WebSockets, streaming APIs, or long-lived requests will be cut off when the service worker is suspended. (There are officially recommended ways to prevent termination: [[[[https://developer.chrome.com/docs/extensions/develop/migrate/to-service-workers#keep_a_service_worker_alive_until_a_long-running_operation_is_finished]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional}]{.RefSource}](https://developer.chrome.com/docs/extensions/develop/migrate/to-service-workers#keep_a_service_worker_alive_until_a_long-running_operation_is_finished)]{.ExternalRef}.)

-   [Background scripts]{#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm12} cannot open OAuth login windows using [window.open()]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional}. Instead, authentication flows must be handled through the [chrome.identity]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional} API.
:::
::::

[]{#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_PB376 .pagebreak aria-label="376" role="doc-pagebreak" title="376"}Despite these restrictions, background scripts remain the best option for most networking tasks. Extensions that rely on JWT-based authentication, OAuth flows, or short-lived API requests will work seamlessly. For cases where long-running network activity is required, strategies like keeping an options page open, using alarms to periodically wake the service worker, or delegating requests to content scripts can help mitigate these new limitations.
:::::
::::::::

:::::::: {#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_Sec6 .section .Section1 .RenderAsSection1 aria-labelledby="Sec6Heading"}
## Pinning an Extension ID {#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_Sec6Heading .Heading}

In the context of extension networking, it is useful to be able to predict and control the origin of a browser extension. When developing locally, the browser will assign your extension an ID automatically; when uploading to the Chrome Web Store, it will assign your extension an entirely different ID. For developers that wish to have a consistent ID, this is problematic.

:::: {#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_Par17 .Para}
Fortunately, it is possible to pin your extension's ID ahead of time to make it consistent and predictable. Doing so involves the following steps:

::: OrderedList
1.  ::: ItemNumber
    1\.
    :::

    ::: ItemContent
    **Upload the initial version of your extension to the Chrome Web Store.** The store will generate a public key for your [extension]{#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm13}, which in turn is used to generate the extension ID. Your local browser will generate the same extension ID if it is provided that public key (Figure [[11-1](#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_Fig1)]{.InternalRef}).
    :::

    ::: ClearBoth
     
    :::

2.  ::: ItemNumber
    2\.
    :::

    ::: ItemContent
    **Retrieve the public key**[]{#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm14} **from the web store** (Figure [[11-2](#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_Fig2)]{.InternalRef}).
    :::

    ::: ClearBoth
     
    :::

3.  ::: ItemNumber
    3\.
    :::

    ::: ItemContent
    []{#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_PB377 .pagebreak aria-label="377" role="doc-pagebreak" title="377"}**Add the public key to your manifest**[]{#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm15} **under the "key" property**. Strip out the BEGIN/END lines and all the whitespace and pass it in as one big string (Figure [[11-3](#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_Fig3)]{.InternalRef}).
    :::

    ::: ClearBoth
     
    :::

4.  ::: ItemNumber
    4\.
    :::

    ::: ItemContent
    **Load the extension locally**. Your local [browser]{#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm16} should generate an identical extension ID to the Chrome Web Store (Figure [[11-4](#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_Fig4)]{.InternalRef}).
    :::

    ::: ClearBoth
     
    :::
:::
::::

:::: {#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_FPar1 .FormalPara .FormalParaRenderingStyle1 .ParaTypeImportant}
::: Heading
Caution
:::

Keep in mind that the production extension and the local extension having the same ID means the browser will consider them to be the same. To prevent collision conflicts, ensure you only have the production or local extension installed at any given time.
::::

::: {#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_Par23 .Para}
This process is shown in Figure [[11-1](#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_Fig1)]{.InternalRef}.

<figure id="Fig1" class="Figure">
<div id="534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_MO1" class="MediaObject">
<img src="/app/temp/1773197898499_images/images/534317_2_En_11_Chapter/534317_2_En_11_Fig1_HTML.jpg" class="ImageStyle3" alt="Screenshot of the Chrome Web Store Developer Dashboard showing details for a browser extension named &quot;Browser Extension Explorer.&quot; The status is published, with version 1.0.5. The item type is an extension, and the language is English. There are no requirements listed. Permissions include activeTab, contextMenus, declarativeNetRequest, scripting, storage, tabs, webNavigation, and host permission. The CRX file is named &quot;main.crx,&quot; and there is a link to view the public key. The publisher&#39;s name is displayed at the top right." />
</div>
<figcaption><div class="CaptionContent">
<span class="CaptionNumber">Figure 11-1</span>
<p>The Chrome Web Store package page including the public key link. Note the <span id="534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm17">ID</span> beginning with “jnof”</p>
</div></figcaption>
</figure>

<figure id="Fig2" class="Figure">
<div id="534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_MO2" class="MediaObject">
<img src="/app/temp/1773197898499_images/images/534317_2_En_11_Chapter/534317_2_En_11_Fig2_HTML.jpg" class="ImageStyle3" alt="A screenshot displaying a dialog box titled &quot;Public key&quot; with a block of text representing a public key. The text begins with &quot;-----BEGIN PUBLIC KEY-----&quot; and ends with &quot;-----END PUBLIC KEY-----&quot;. An &quot;OK&quot; button is visible at the bottom right of the dialog box." />
</div>
<figcaption><div class="CaptionContent">
<span class="CaptionNumber">Figure 11-2</span>
<p>The revealed public <span id="534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm18">key</span></p>
</div></figcaption>
</figure>

<figure id="Fig3" class="Figure">
<div id="534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_MO3" class="MediaObject">
<img src="/app/temp/1773197898499_images/images/534317_2_En_11_Chapter/534317_2_En_11_Fig3_HTML.jpg" class="ImageStyle3" alt="Screenshot of a code editor displaying a JSON file named &quot;manifest.json&quot;. The file includes a JSON object with three key-value pairs: &quot;manifest_version&quot; set to 3, &quot;version&quot; set to &quot;1.0.5&quot;, and &quot;key&quot; containing a long string of characters. The &quot;key&quot; value is highlighted in blue, indicating selection. The editor interface shows line numbers and a file tab at the top." />
</div>
<figcaption><div class="CaptionContent">
<span class="CaptionNumber">Figure 11-3</span>
<p>The flattened public key passed to the <span id="534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm19">manifest</span></p>
</div></figcaption>
</figure>

<figure id="Fig4" class="Figure">
<div id="534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_MO4" class="MediaObject">
<img src="/app/temp/1773197898499_images/images/534317_2_En_11_Chapter/534317_2_En_11_Fig4_HTML.jpg" class="ImageStyle3" alt="Screenshot of a browser extension card titled &quot;Browser Extension Explorer 1.0.5.&quot; It describes an open-source extension with interactive demos, no NPM packages, and one-click installation using HTML, JS, and CSS. The ID is &quot;jnofdoejfipgalklopidpdeofjebihcf.&quot; There are options to inspect views via a service worker, and buttons labeled &quot;Details,&quot; &quot;Remove,&quot; and &quot;Errors.&quot; A toggle switch is shown in the bottom right corner." />
</div>
<figcaption><div class="CaptionContent">
<span class="CaptionNumber">Figure 11-4</span>
<p>The extension loaded <span id="534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm20">locally</span>. Note the ID beginning with “jnof”</p>
</div></figcaption>
</figure>
:::
::::::::

::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::: {#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_Sec7 .section .Section1 .RenderAsSection1 aria-labelledby="Sec7Heading"}
## Authentication Styles {#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_Sec7Heading .Heading}

[]{#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_PB379 .pagebreak aria-label="379" role="doc-pagebreak" title="379"}The web wouldn't have gotten far without the ability to authenticate. Browser extensions can use the same foundational technologies as websites to authenticate. However, due to the innate architecture of extensions, the way you go about authenticating must be carefully considered.

::: {#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_Sec8 .section .Section2 .RenderAsSection2 aria-labelledby="Sec8Heading"}
### No Authentication {#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_Sec8Heading .Heading}

Browser [extensions]{#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm21} don't necessarily need to authenticate. Often, a clever application of a small portion of the [WebExtensions API]{#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm22} is all that is needed to make an extension useful. What's more, when a content script interacts with the raw DOM, the browser extension's ability to interact with authenticated sessions is totally disjoint from any concept of a logged-in []{#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_PB380 .pagebreak aria-label="380" role="doc-pagebreak" title="380"}state. In other words, the host website can do the heavy lifting of logging in the user, the browser extension can wait for a logged-in state, and then the content script can read from and write to the DOM.

Some APIs allow you to take advantage of native browser identity separation without managing authentication. For example, the storage API allows you to use [chrome.storage.sync]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional}, which is attached to the user's browser account and will be synced to their account, or [chrome.storage.local]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional}, which is localized to the current browser instance.
:::

:::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::: {#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_Sec9 .section .Section2 .RenderAsSection2 aria-labelledby="Sec9Heading"}
### Spoofed Authentication {#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_Sec9Heading .Heading}

"Spoofing" [authentication]{#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm23} allows extensions to send requests as if they were the user, enabling deeper integration while working within the constraints of web security models. This technique allows the extension to circumvent handling user credentials, thereby protecting user privacy and eliminating a wide range of security vulnerabilities.

:::: {#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_FPar2 .FormalPara .FormalParaRenderingStyle1 .ParaTypeImportant}
::: Heading
Caution
:::

[Spoofing authentication]{#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm24} requires care and introduces risk. Handle credentials responsibly and ethically. Misuse can lead to security breaches, and abuse can lead to legal consequences.
::::

::: {#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_Sec10 .section .Section3 .RenderAsSection3 aria-labelledby="Sec10Heading"}
#### Content Script Cookie Spoofing {#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_Sec10Heading .Heading}

Network requests made from a content [script]{#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm25} are treated the same as those made from a host page script. If a website relies on [HttpOnly]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional} cookie authentication, a content script can exploit this by piggybacking on the authenticated session, automatically including those cookies in its requests. One complicating factor is the presence of CSRF tokens, but these tokens are often stored in predictable locations, such as inside [\<form\>]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional} elements. A content script can inspect the DOM, extract the CSRF token, and include it in outgoing requests.

[]{#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_PB381 .pagebreak aria-label="381" role="doc-pagebreak" title="381"}As content [scripts]{#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm26} operate within the same execution environment as the page, requests made via [fetch()]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional} or [XMLHttpRequest]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional} will automatically include session cookies, just as they would for a normal user request. Importantly, this also means that content script network requests are not affected by cross-origin [restrictions]{#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm27} set by the server, as they are treated as same-origin requests.
:::

:::::::::::::::::::::: {#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_Sec11 .section .Section3 .RenderAsSection3 aria-labelledby="Sec11Heading"}
#### Extension UI/Background Script Cookie Spoofing {#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_Sec11Heading .Heading}

Unlike content scripts, which operate in the same context as the web page, extension pages and background scripts are considered separate origins. This means that requests made using [fetch()]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional} will not include cookies by default.

:::: {#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_Par32 .Para}
To send authenticated requests from an extension's UI or background [script]{#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm28}, you must

::: UnorderedList
-   Declare the [cookies]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional} permission in manifest.json

-   Add the required origins in [host_permissions]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional} in [manifest.json]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional} to access stored cookies

-   Use [credentials: include]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional} in [fetch()]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional} to allow cookies to be sent with requests

-   Ensure the server allows cross-origin credentials by permitting requests with [Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional} and specifying the exact origin in [Access-Control-Allow-Origin]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional}
:::
::::

The following example demonstrates how this can be achieved:

:::::::::: {#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_Par38 .Para}
[]{#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_PB382 .pagebreak aria-label="382" role="doc-pagebreak" title="382"}*manifest.json*

::::::::: {#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_PC1 .ProgramCode}
:::::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
{
:::

::: FixedLine
  \...,
:::

::: FixedLine
  \"permissions\": \[\"cookies\"\],
:::

::: FixedLine
  \"host_permissions\": \[\"https://example.com/\"\]
:::

::: FixedLine
}
:::
::::::::
:::::::::
::::::::::

::::::::: {#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_Par39 .Para}
*script.js*

:::::::: {#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_PC2 .ProgramCode}
::::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
fetch(\"https://example.com/protected-resource\", {
:::

::: FixedLine
  method: \"GET\",
:::

::: FixedLine
  credentials: \"include\"
:::

::: FixedLine
})[]{#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm29}
:::
:::::::
::::::::
:::::::::

:::: {#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_FPar3 .FormalPara .FormalParaRenderingStyle1 .ParaTypeImportant}
::: Heading
Note
:::

This will be treated as a cross-origin request. This setup works only if the server allows cross-origin requests with credentials. If the server blocks such requests, an alternative approach is needed.
::::
::::::::::::::::::::::

:::::::::::::::::: {#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_Sec12 .section .Section3 .RenderAsSection3 aria-labelledby="Sec12Heading"}
#### JWT Spoofing {#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_Sec12Heading .Heading}

::::::::::::::::: {#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_Par41 .Para}
Many modern web applications authenticate users using [JSON Web Tokens (JWTs)]{#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm30}[]{#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm31} instead of or in addition to cookies. JWTs are often included in API requests as part of the Authorization header ([Bearer \<token\>]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional}). JWTs are usually included in request headers, making them retrievable through request interception. Extensions can extract JWTs by using [chrome.webRequest.onBeforeSendHeaders]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional} to inspect outgoing []{#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_PB383 .pagebreak aria-label="383" role="doc-pagebreak" title="383"}requests and capture the [Authorization]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional} header. The following example demonstrates inspecting an outgoing authenticated request and extracting the [JWT]{#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm32}[]{#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm33}:

:::::::::::::::: {#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_PC3 .ProgramCode}
::::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
chrome.webRequest.onBeforeSendHeaders.addListener(
:::

::: FixedLine
  ({ requestHeaders }) =\> {
:::

::: FixedLine
    const h = requestHeaders.find(
:::

::: FixedLine
        h =\> h.name.toLowerCase() === \"authorization\");
:::
:::::::

:::::::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
    if (h?.value.startsWith(\"Bearer \")) {
:::

::: FixedLine
      console.log(\"JWT:\", h.value);
:::

::: FixedLine
    }
:::

::: FixedLine
  },
:::

::: FixedLine
  { urls: \[\"https://example.com/\*\"\] },
:::

::: FixedLine
  \[\"requestHeaders\"\]
:::

::: FixedLine
);
:::
::::::::::
::::::::::::::::
:::::::::::::::::
::::::::::::::::::
::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::

::: {#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_Sec13 .section .Section2 .RenderAsSection2 aria-labelledby="Sec13Heading"}
### Cookie Authentication {#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_Sec13Heading .Heading}

[Cookie authentication]{#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm34}[]{#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm35} with an extension-controlled server is technically possible for browser extension user interfaces, but I don't recommend it. This style of authentication works best when the web page is rendered directly by a server, and for browser extensions, this is never the case. Furthermore, a cookie-based authenticated state can't be directly accessed by the background service worker. Overall, because it involves so much unnecessary overhead to implement, I recommend you avoid [cookie authentication]{#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm36}[]{#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm37} unless you have a particularly compelling reason to do so.
:::

::: {#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_Sec14 .section .Section2 .RenderAsSection2 aria-labelledby="Sec14Heading"}
### JSON Web Token Authentication {#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_Sec14Heading .Heading}

Token-style authentication works well for app-based authentication where cookies aren't practical. For browser extensions, token authentication schemes like [JWT]{#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm38}[]{#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm39} (JSON Web Token) are ideal. Extension UIs and []{#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_PB384 .pagebreak aria-label="384" role="doc-pagebreak" title="384"}background scripts can all directly authenticate with a server and share the authentication token between extension components without issue. Content scripts are able to use JWTs, but the host page's cross-origin policy may block requests to the server, so sending requests indirectly via the background service worker is the preferred workaround.
:::

::::: {#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_Sec15 .section .Section2 .RenderAsSection2 aria-labelledby="Sec15Heading"}
### OAuth and OpenID {#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_Sec15Heading .Heading}

With the [chrome.identity]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional} API, browser extensions have first-class support for [OAuth]{#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm40} and [OpenID]{#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm41}. This API is not supported inside content scripts, but it can be effectively deployed anywhere else in a browser extension. For developers that do not wish to implement a backend server, authorization and authentication via OAuth or OpenID is extremely practical.

The [chrome.identity]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional} API is spearheaded by Google Chrome, but it enjoys at least partial support in all Chromium browsers and Firefox. In Google Chrome, browser [extensions]{#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm42}[]{#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm43} enjoy additional identity API methods including a streamlined OAuth authorization flow (more on this later in the chapter).

:::: {#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_FPar4 .FormalPara .FormalParaRenderingStyle1 .ParaTypeImportant}
::: Heading
Note
:::

Safari currently has no support for the identity API.
::::
:::::
:::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::

:::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::: {#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_Sec16 .section .Section1 .RenderAsSection1 aria-labelledby="Sec16Heading"}
## OAuth, OpenID, and the Identity API {#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_Sec16Heading .Heading}

:::: {#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_FPar5 .FormalPara .FormalParaRenderingStyle1 .ParaTypeImportant}
::: Heading
Note
:::

This section assumes you have basic knowledge of how the OAuth2 protocol works. For an introduction to OAuth2, Auth0 has an excellent writeup on it: [[[[https://auth0.com/intro-to-iam/what-is-oauth-2/]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional}]{.RefSource}](https://auth0.com/intro-to-iam/what-is-oauth-2/)]{.ExternalRef}.
::::

:::: {#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_Par48 .Para}
[]{#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_PB385 .pagebreak aria-label="385" role="doc-pagebreak" title="385"}The ability to delegate authentication and authorization to a third-party platform is incredibly useful, but implementing this in browser extensions is tricky. Consider some of the [challenges]{#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm44} involved:

::: UnorderedList
-   Delegating a login to a third-party platform requires the ability to open a trusted interface to collect the credentials. This is typically done using [window.open(),]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional} which launches a dedicated popup window that preserves a [reference]{#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm45} to the main application via [window.opener]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional}. This link is crucial for securely completing the OAuth flow and passing back tokens or authorization codes. However, background service workers cannot invoke [window.open()]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional}.

-   Configuring a platform to support OAuth requires knowledge of the extension ID ahead of time.

-   OAuth and OpenID utilize the same underlying protocol, which includes a redirect URL. Browser extensions can only render inside private URLs with an extension protocol.
:::
::::

Fortunately, the [identity API]{#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm46} solves all these problems by providing a flexible set of tools for implementing delegated authentication and authorization.

::::: {#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_Sec17 .section .Section2 .RenderAsSection2 aria-labelledby="Sec17Heading"}
### OAuth API Methods {#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_Sec17Heading .Heading}

:::: {#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_Par53 .Para}
There are two ways of using OAuth in an extension:

::: UnorderedList
-   [[chrome.identity.getAuthToken()]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional}]{#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm47} allows you to natively authenticate. This method allows you to skip providing a redirect URL and making the authorization token request. Instead, you simply provide the required values inside the [\"oauth2\"]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional} manifest property and call []{#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_PB386 .pagebreak aria-label="386" role="doc-pagebreak" title="386"}this method. The browser will kick open the OAuth dialog, and the method's callback will be passed the OAuth token. This is *by far* the simplest way of implementing OAuth in an extension, but it is only available inside Google Chrome. **This requires a "Chrome Extension" client ID.**

-   [[chrome.identity.launchWebAuthFlow()]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional}]{#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm48} is the more generalized method for using OAuth2. It is cross-browser (works on Firefox, Edge, etc.) and cross-platform (supports OAuth2 with Facebook, LinkedIn, etc.). It is much more labor intensive, as it requires you to manually implement each of the OAuth2 steps.
:::
::::
:::::

::::::: {#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_Sec18 .section .Section2 .RenderAsSection2 aria-labelledby="Sec18Heading"}
### OAuth Redirect URLs {#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_Sec18Heading .Heading}

:::: {#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_Par56 .Para}
To solve the OAuth redirect [URL]{#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm49} problem, the browser supports a special URL that will direct the auth flow back to the extension. To access this URL, you can use the [chrome.identity.getRedirectURL()]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional} method. The Chrome documentation describes its behavior:

::: UnorderedList
-   *This method enables auth flows with non-Google identity providers by launching a web view and navigating it to the first URL in the provider's auth flow. When the provider redirects to a URL matching the pattern https://\<app-id\>.*chromiumapp.org*/\*, the window will close, and the final redirect URL will be passed to the callback function.*
:::
::::

In other [words]{#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm50}, the browser gives this URL special treatment in order to support authentication in the context of browser extensions. All Chromium browsers and Firefox support this method.

:::: {#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_FPar6 .FormalPara .FormalParaRenderingStyle1 .ParaTypeImportant}
::: Heading
Tip
:::

[]{#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_PB387 .pagebreak aria-label="387" role="doc-pagebreak" title="387"}You can see this special URL handling logic in the Chromium source code: [[[[https://chromium.googlesource.com/chromium/chromium/+/master/chrome/browser/extensions/api/identity/web_auth_flow.cc]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional}]{.RefSource}](https://chromium.googlesource.com/chromium/chromium/+/master/chrome/browser/extensions/api/identity/web_auth_flow.cc)]{.ExternalRef}.
::::
:::::::

:::::::::::::::::::: {#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_Sec19 .section .Section2 .RenderAsSection2 aria-labelledby="Sec19Heading"}
### Configuring the Authorization Platform {#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_Sec19Heading .Heading}

The first step in setting up OAuth for a Chrome extension is to configure the [authorization platform]{#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm51} to provide access to the extension. This involves configuring the OAuth consent screen and generating a client ID (Figures [[[13-5]{.RefSource}](#534317_2_En_13_Chapter.xhtml#Fig5)]{.ExternalRef}, [[[13-6]{.RefSource}](#534317_2_En_13_Chapter.xhtml#Fig6)]{.ExternalRef}, [[[13-7]{.RefSource}](#534317_2_En_13_Chapter.xhtml#Fig7)]{.ExternalRef}, [[[13-8]{.RefSource}](#534317_2_En_13_Chapter.xhtml#Fig8)]{.ExternalRef}, and [[[13-9]{.RefSource}](#534317_2_En_13_Chapter.xhtml#Fig9)]{.ExternalRef}). The consent screen is what will be shown in the popup when the user begins the OAuth authentication flow; the client ID is the string that uniquely identifies the extension and allows it to engage with the authorization platform (Figure [[11-10](#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_Fig10)]{.InternalRef}).

:::: {#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_FPar7 .FormalPara .FormalParaRenderingStyle1 .ParaTypeImportant}
::: Heading
Note
:::

This process will be slightly different for various authorization platforms and OAuth flows, but it will always provide you with a client ID.
::::

The following screenshots demonstrate some of these steps when [configuring]{#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm52} Google OAuth (Figures [[11-5](#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_Fig5)]{.InternalRef}, [[11-6](#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_Fig6)]{.InternalRef}, [[11-7](#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_Fig7)]{.InternalRef}, [[11-8](#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_Fig8)]{.InternalRef} and [[11-9](#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_Fig9)]{.InternalRef}).

<figure id="Fig5" class="Figure">
<div id="534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_MO5" class="MediaObject">
<img src="/app/temp/1773197898499_images/images/534317_2_En_11_Chapter/534317_2_En_11_Fig5_HTML.jpg" class="ImageStyle3" alt="Screenshot of the Google Cloud API &amp; Services interface showing the OAuth consent screen setup. The left panel lists options like &quot;Enabled APIs &amp; services&quot; and &quot;Credentials.&quot; The main section displays &quot;App information&quot; with fields for &quot;App name,&quot; &quot;User support email,&quot; and &quot;App logo.&quot; The right panel explains how the consent screen appears to users, with steps for granting access to a Google Account." />
</div>
<figcaption><div class="CaptionContent">
<span class="CaptionNumber">Figure 11-5</span>
<p>Configuring the OAuth consent screen in the Google Cloud dashboard</p>
</div></figcaption>
</figure>

<figure id="Fig6" class="Figure">
<div id="534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_MO6" class="MediaObject">
<img src="/app/temp/1773197898499_images/images/534317_2_En_11_Chapter/534317_2_En_11_Fig6_HTML.jpg" class="ImageStyle3" alt="Screenshot of a credentials management interface showing options to create different types of credentials. A dropdown menu lists options: &quot;API key,&quot; &quot;OAuth client ID,&quot; &quot;Service account,&quot; and &quot;Help me choose.&quot; The interface includes sections for &quot;API Keys&quot; and &quot;OAuth 2.0 Client IDs,&quot; both displaying no entries. The &quot;CREATE CREDENTIALS&quot; and &quot;DELETE&quot; buttons are visible at the top." />
</div>
<figcaption><div class="CaptionContent">
<span class="CaptionNumber">Figure 11-6</span>
<p>Creating OAuth <span id="534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm53">client ID credentials</span> for a browser extension in the Google Cloud dashboard</p>
</div></figcaption>
</figure>

<figure id="Fig7" class="Figure">
<div id="534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_MO7" class="MediaObject">
<img src="/app/temp/1773197898499_images/images/534317_2_En_11_Chapter/534317_2_En_11_Fig7_HTML.jpg" class="ImageStyle3" alt="Screenshot of the &quot;Create OAuth client ID&quot; page on Google&#39;s platform. The page explains that a client ID is used to identify a single app to Google&#39;s OAuth servers. A dropdown menu labeled &quot;Application type&quot; is open, showing options such as Web application, Android, Chrome Extension, iOS, TVs and Limited Input devices, Desktop app, and Universal Windows Platform (UWP). Links for &quot;Setting up OAuth 2.0&quot; and &quot;Learn more&quot; are also visible." />
</div>
<figcaption><div class="CaptionContent">
<span class="CaptionNumber">Figure 11-7</span>
<p>Google’s options for an OAuth client <span id="534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm54">ID</span></p>
</div></figcaption>
</figure>

:::: {#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_Par63 .Para}
[]{#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_PB389 .pagebreak aria-label="389" role="doc-pagebreak" title="389"}Not all platforms will support this distinction. In the case of Google OAuth, both "Chrome extension" and "Web application" can be used for browser extensions.

::: UnorderedList
-   [**Chrome extension**]{#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm55} means you are using the manifest [oauth2]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional} field and the [getAuthToken()]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional} method. This will also authenticate the user in the active Google Chrome browser profile, which may not be desirable.

-   [**Web application**]{#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm56} means you are using the [launchWebAuthFlow()]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional} method. This allows you to authenticate separately from the Google Chrome browser profile.
:::
::::

<figure id="Fig8" class="Figure">
<div id="534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_MO8" class="MediaObject">
<img src="/app/temp/1773197898499_images/images/534317_2_En_11_Chapter/534317_2_En_11_Fig8_HTML.jpg" class="ImageStyle3" alt="Screenshot of the &quot;Create OAuth client ID&quot; page for a Chrome Extension. It includes fields for &quot;Application type&quot; set to &quot;Chrome Extension,&quot; &quot;Name&quot; with &quot;Example Chrome Extension,&quot; and &quot;Item ID&quot; with a unique identifier. Instructions mention verifying app ownership and provide a &quot;Verify ownership&quot; button. Options to &quot;Create&quot; or &quot;Cancel&quot; are at the bottom. Links to learn more about OAuth and app ownership are present." />
</div>
<figcaption><div class="CaptionContent">
<span class="CaptionNumber">Figure 11-8</span>
<p>Configuring a Chrome extension OAuth client <span id="534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm57">ID</span></p>
</div></figcaption>
</figure>

<figure id="Fig9" class="Figure">
<div id="534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_MO9" class="MediaObject">
<img src="/app/temp/1773197898499_images/images/534317_2_En_11_Chapter/534317_2_En_11_Fig9_HTML.jpg" class="ImageStyle7" alt="Screenshot of the &quot;Create OAuth client ID&quot; page for a web application. It includes fields for &quot;Application type&quot; and &quot;Name,&quot; with &quot;Web application&quot; and &quot;Example Chrome Extension (Web)&quot; as their respective entries. There are sections for &quot;Authorized JavaScript origins&quot; and &quot;Authorized redirect URIs,&quot; with a sample URI provided. Buttons for adding URIs and options to &quot;Create&quot; or &quot;Cancel&quot; are visible. Links to learn more about OAuth 2.0 are included." />
</div>
<figcaption><div class="CaptionContent">
<span class="CaptionNumber">Figure 11-9</span>
<p>Configuring a Web application client <span id="534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm58">ID</span></p>
</div></figcaption>
</figure>

<figure id="Fig10" class="Figure">
<div id="534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_MO10" class="MediaObject">
<img src="/app/temp/1773197898499_images/images/534317_2_En_11_Chapter/534317_2_En_11_Fig10_HTML.jpg" class="ImageStyle8" alt="Google account sign-in prompt for &quot;Browser Extension Explorer&quot; requesting access. It asks for permission to associate with personal Google info, view personal info, and see the primary email address. Options to &quot;Cancel&quot; or &quot;Allow&quot; are at the bottom. A reminder to trust the app and links to Google&#39;s data safety and privacy policy are included." />
</div>
<figcaption><div class="CaptionContent">
<span class="CaptionNumber">Figure 11-10</span>
<p>The working OAuth <span id="534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm59">consent screen</span></p>
</div></figcaption>
</figure>

[]{#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_PB393 .pagebreak aria-label="393" role="doc-pagebreak" title="393"}Google will eventually provide you with your [client ID]{#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm60} in a downloadable JSON. A dummy example is shown here:

::::::::::::::: {#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_Par67 .Para}
*client_secret.json*

:::::::::::::: {#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_PC4 .ProgramCode}
::::::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
{
:::

::: FixedLine
  \"installed\": {
:::

::: FixedLine
    **\"client_id\": \"\<some_id\>.apps.googleusercontent.com\",**
:::

::: FixedLine
    \"project_id\": \"browser-extension-explorer\",
:::

::: FixedLine
    \"auth_uri\": \"https://accounts.google.com/o/oauth2/auth\",
:::

::: FixedLine
    \"token_uri\": \"https://oauth2.googleapis.com/token\",
:::
:::::::::

:::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
\"auth_provider_x509_cert_url\": \"https://www.googleapis.com/oauth2/v1/certs\"
:::

::: FixedLine
  }
:::

::: FixedLine
}
:::
::::::
::::::::::::::
:::::::::::::::

Other platforms will provide you the client ID in a different fashion and format, but ultimately this is what must make it into your browser extension to enable OAuth authentication.
::::::::::::::::::::

::::: {#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_Sec20 .section .Section2 .RenderAsSection2 aria-labelledby="Sec20Heading"}
### Additional Help {#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_Sec20Heading .Heading}

:::: {#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_Par69 .Para}
[Setting up]{#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm61} OAuth is extremely confusing. I've hand-selected some links that walk you through setting it up in various ways:

::: UnorderedList
-   [[[[https://developer.chrome.com/docs/extensions/how-to/integrate/oauth]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional}]{.RefSource}](https://developer.chrome.com/docs/extensions/how-to/integrate/oauth)]{.ExternalRef}

-   [[[[https://blog.plasmo.com/p/firebase-chrome-extension]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional}]{.RefSource}](https://blog.plasmo.com/p/firebase-chrome-extension)]{.ExternalRef}
:::
::::
:::::
::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::

:::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::: {#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_Sec21 .section .Section1 .RenderAsSection1 aria-labelledby="Sec21Heading"}
## OAuth and OpenID Examples {#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_Sec21Heading .Heading}

[]{#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_PB394 .pagebreak aria-label="394" role="doc-pagebreak" title="394"}When it comes to OAuth for extensions, there really isn't a good substitute for getting your hands dirty with actual working code. The following examples demonstrate slightly different ways of configuring and triggering an OAuth flow. **These examples are provided in source code, but they will not work without some legwork on your end. You will need to configure your own OAuth provider information such as client IDs to make them work correctly.**

:::: {#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_FPar8 .FormalPara .FormalParaRenderingStyle1 .ParaTypeImportant}
::: Heading
Tip
:::

The book's companion extension includes working OAuth examples. Visit [buildingbrowserextensions.com]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional} to install it and view the source code.
::::

:::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::: {#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_Sec22 .section .Section2 .RenderAsSection2 aria-labelledby="Sec22Heading"}
### Google OAuth with getAuthToken() {#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_Sec22Heading .Heading}

:::: {#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_Par74 .Para}
This example uses Google Chrome's native OAuth faculties to authenticate the user. Clicking the toolbar icon triggers the OAuth flow, and a successful authentication will log some basic fields. Some things to note:

::: UnorderedList
-   The [permissions]{#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm62} requested include [identity]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional}, which gives access to the identity API, and [identity.email]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional}, which allows the extension to easily access the email and Gaia ID of the user account signed into the current profile via [chrome.identity.getProfileUserInfo()]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional}.

-   [[getAuthToken()]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional}]{#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm63} is idempotent. After the user authenticates, it will cache the OAuth token. Following successful authentication, the next time this method is called, no OAuth dialog will appear: the method will retrieve the token from the cache and pass it to the []{#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_PB395 .pagebreak aria-label="395" role="doc-pagebreak" title="395"}callback. The [interactive]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional} option controls whether it will attempt to launch the OAuth dialog if the user is *not* authenticated. If [false]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional}, it will exit and throw an error if not authenticated. Otherwise, the OAuth dialog will open.

-   Note that nowhere in this flow are any [URLs]{#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm64} other than what is provided in the manifest. The redirect URL and OAuth endpoints are all automatically handled by the browser!
:::
::::

:::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::: {#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_Par78 .Para}
:::::::::::::::::::::::: {#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_PC5 .ProgramCode}
::::::::::::::::::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
{
:::

::: FixedLine
  \"name\": \"Google OAuth Example\",
:::

::: FixedLine
  \"version\": \"0.0.1\",
:::

::: FixedLine
  \"manifest_version\": 3,
:::

::: FixedLine
  \"action\": {},
:::

::: FixedLine
  \"background\": {
:::

::: FixedLine
    \"service_worker\": \"background.js\",
:::

::: FixedLine
    \"type\": \"module\"
:::

::: FixedLine
  },
:::

::: FixedLine
  \"permissions\": **\[\"identity\", \"identity.email\"**\],
:::

::: FixedLine
  \"oauth2\": {
:::

::: FixedLine
    **\"client_id\": \"\<YOUR_CLIENT_ID\>\",**
:::

::: FixedLine
    \"scopes\": \[
:::

::: FixedLine
      \"https://www.googleapis.com/auth/userinfo.email\",
:::

::: FixedLine
      \"https://www.googleapis.com/auth/userinfo.profile\"
:::

::: FixedLine
    \]
:::

::: FixedLine
  }
:::

::: FixedLine
}[]{#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm65}
:::
:::::::::::::::::::::

:::: {.Caption lang="en"}
::: CaptionContent
[Example 11-1a]{.CaptionNumber}

[manifest.json]{#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm66}
:::
::::
::::::::::::::::::::::::

[]{#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_PB396 .pagebreak aria-label="396" role="doc-pagebreak" title="396"}

::::::::::::::::::::: {#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_PC6 .ProgramCode}
:::::::::::::::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
chrome.action.onClicked.addListener(() =\> {
:::

::: FixedLine
  **chrome.identity.getAuthToken(**
:::

::: FixedLine
    {
:::

::: FixedLine
      **interactive: true,**
:::

::: FixedLine
    },
:::

::: FixedLine
    (token) =\> {
:::

::: FixedLine
      if (token) {
:::

::: FixedLine
        **chrome.identity.getProfileUserInfo**(
:::

::: FixedLine
          { accountStatus: \"ANY\" },
:::

::: FixedLine
          (info) =\> console.log(info)
:::

::: FixedLine
        );
:::

::: FixedLine
      }
:::

::: FixedLine
    }
:::

::: FixedLine
  );
:::

::: FixedLine
});
:::
::::::::::::::::::

:::: {.Caption lang="en"}
::: CaptionContent
[Example 11-1b]{.CaptionNumber}

[background.js]{#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm67}
:::
::::
:::::::::::::::::::::
::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::

::::::::: {#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_Par79 .Para}
*Console output:*

:::::::: {#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_PC7 .ProgramCode}
::::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
{
:::

::: FixedLine
  \"email\": \<google_email\>,
:::

::: FixedLine
  \"id\": \<google_gaia_id\>
:::

::: FixedLine
}
:::
:::::::
::::::::
:::::::::
::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::

::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::: {#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_Sec23 .section .Section2 .RenderAsSection2 aria-labelledby="Sec23Heading"}
### Google OpenID with launchWebAuthFlow( ) {#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_Sec23Heading .Heading}

:::: {#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_FPar9 .FormalPara .FormalParaRenderingStyle1 .ParaTypeImportant}
::: Heading
Note
:::

This section assumes you have basic knowledge of how the OpenID Connect protocol works. For an introduction to OpenID, Auth0 has an excellent writeup on it: [[[[https://auth0.com/intro-to-iam/what-is-openid-connect-oidc/]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional}]{.RefSource}](https://auth0.com/intro-to-iam/what-is-openid-connect-oidc/)]{.ExternalRef}.
::::

:::: {#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_Par81 .Para}
[]{#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_PB397 .pagebreak aria-label="397" role="doc-pagebreak" title="397"}This example implements a Google OpenID Connect flow to authenticate the user using [[launchWebAuthFlow()]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional}]{#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm68}. Clicking the toolbar icon triggers the OpenID flow, and a successful authentication will log the OpenID payload. Some things to note:

::: UnorderedList
-   Compared to the previous example, we no longer have a need for the [identity.email]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional} permission since we are not using the [getProfileUserInfo()]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional} [method]{#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm69}.

-   Note that the [client ID]{#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm70} is being passed inside JavaScript rather than provided in the manifest.

-   With [[launchWebAuthFlow()]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional}]{#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm71}, you must now manually provide the redirect URL that the browser will use to redirect back into your extension's callback.

-   [[launchWebAuthFlow()]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional}]{#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm72} has an [interactive]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional} control that behaves identically to that of [getAuthToken().]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional}

-   OpenID requires specific [query string parameters]{#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm73} in the initial URL and returns a JSON Web Token [(JWT)]{#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm74}. This example unpacks the JWT so you can see the data packaged inside.
:::
::::

:::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::: {#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_Par87 .Para}
::::::::::::::::: {#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_PC8 .ProgramCode}
:::::::::::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
{
:::

::: FixedLine
  \"name\": \"OpenID Example\",
:::

::: FixedLine
  \"version\": \"0.0.1\",
:::

::: FixedLine
  \"manifest_version\": 3,
:::

::: FixedLine
  \"action\": {},
:::

::: FixedLine
  \"background\": {
:::

::: FixedLine
    \"service_worker\": \"background.js\",
:::

::: FixedLine
    \"type\": \"module\"
:::

::: FixedLine
  },
:::

::: FixedLine
  \"permissions\": \[**\"identity\"**\]
:::

::: FixedLine
}
:::
::::::::::::::

:::: {.Caption lang="en"}
::: CaptionContent
[Example 11-2a]{.CaptionNumber}

[manifest.json]{#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm75}
:::
::::
:::::::::::::::::

:::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::: {#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_PC9 .ProgramCode}
::::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
chrome.action.onClicked.addListener(() =\> {
:::

::: FixedLine
  const clientId = \"\<YOUR_CLIENT_ID\>\";
:::

::: FixedLine
  const redirectUri = chrome.identity.getRedirectURL();
:::

::: FixedLine
  const nonce = generateNonce();
:::
:::::::

:::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
  const authUrl = buildAuthUrl(clientId, redirectUri, nonce);
:::
::::

::::::::::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
  launchAuthFlow(authUrl, (redirectUrl) =\> {
:::

::: FixedLine
    if (redirectUrl) {
:::

::: FixedLine
      const jwt = extractIdToken(redirectUrl);
:::

::: FixedLine
      if (jwt) {
:::

::: FixedLine
        const token = parseJwt(jwt);
:::

::: FixedLine
        console.log(token);
:::

::: FixedLine
      }
:::

::: FixedLine
    }[]{#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm76}
:::

::: FixedLine
  });
:::

::: FixedLine
});
:::
:::::::::::::

::::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
// Generates a unique nonce value
:::

::: FixedLine
function generateNonce() {
:::

::: FixedLine
  return Math.random().toString(36).substring(2, 15);
:::

::: FixedLine
}
:::
:::::::

:::::::::::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
// Constructs the authentication URL for Google\'s OAuth2
:::

::: FixedLine
function buildAuthUrl(clientId, redirectUri, nonce) {
:::

::: FixedLine
  const authUrl = new URL(\"https://accounts.google.com/o/oauth2/v2/auth\");
:::

::: FixedLine
  authUrl.searchParams.set(\"client_id\", clientId);
:::

::: FixedLine
  authUrl.searchParams.set(\"response_type\", \"id_token\");
:::

::: FixedLine
  authUrl.searchParams.set(\"redirect_uri\", redirectUri);
:::

::: FixedLine
  authUrl.searchParams.set(\"scope\", \"openid profile email\");
:::

::: FixedLine
  authUrl.searchParams.set(\"nonce\", nonce);
:::

::: FixedLine
  authUrl.searchParams.set(\"prompt\", \"consent\");
:::

::: FixedLine
  return authUrl.href;
:::

::: FixedLine
}
:::
::::::::::::::

::::::::::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
// Initiates the authentication flow
:::

::: FixedLine
function launchAuthFlow(authUrl, callback) {
:::

::: FixedLine
  chrome.identity.launchWebAuthFlow(
:::

::: FixedLine
    {
:::

::: FixedLine
      url: authUrl,
:::

::: FixedLine
      interactive: true,
:::

::: FixedLine
    },
:::

::: FixedLine
    callback
:::

::: FixedLine
  );
:::

::: FixedLine
}[]{#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm77}
:::
:::::::::::::

::::::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
// Extracts the ID token from the redirect URL
:::

::: FixedLine
function extractIdToken(redirectUrl) {
:::

::: FixedLine
  const urlHash = redirectUrl.split(\"#\")\[1\];
:::

::: FixedLine
  const params = new URLSearchParams(urlHash);
:::

::: FixedLine
  return params.get(\"id_token\");
:::

::: FixedLine
}
:::
:::::::::

:::::::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
// Decodes and parses a JWT (JSON Web Token)
:::

::: FixedLine
function parseJwt(jwt) {
:::

::: FixedLine
  const base64Url = jwt.split(\".\")\[1\];
:::

::: FixedLine
  const base64 = base64Url.replace(/-/g, \"+\")
:::

::: FixedLine
                          .replace(/\_/g, \"/\");
:::

::: FixedLine
  return JSON.parse(atob(base64));
:::

::: FixedLine
}
:::
::::::::::

:::: {.Caption lang="en"}
::: CaptionContent
[Example 11-2b]{.CaptionNumber}

[background.js]{#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm78}
:::
::::
::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::

[]{#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_PB399 .pagebreak aria-label="399" role="doc-pagebreak" title="399"}[]{#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_PB400 .pagebreak aria-label="400" role="doc-pagebreak" title="400"}
::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::

:::::::::::::::::::::: {#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_Par88 .Para}
[*Console output*]{#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm79}*:*

::::::::::::::::::::: {#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_PC10 .ProgramCode}
:::::::::::::::::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
{
:::

::: FixedLine
  \"iss\": \"https://accounts.google.com\",
:::

::: FixedLine
  \"azp\": \"\...\",
:::

::: FixedLine
  \"aud\": \"\...\",
:::

::: FixedLine
  \"sub\": \"\...\",
:::

::: FixedLine
  \"email\": \"XXXXX@gmail.com\",
:::

::: FixedLine
  \"email_verified\": true,
:::

::: FixedLine
  \"nonce\": \"\...\",
:::

::: FixedLine
  \"name\": \"Matt Frisbie\",
:::

::: FixedLine
  \"picture\": \"\...\",
:::

::: FixedLine
  \"given_name\": \"Matt\",
:::

::: FixedLine
  \"family_name\": \"Frisbie\",
:::

::: FixedLine
  \"locale\": \"en\",
:::

::: FixedLine
  \"iat\": \...,
:::

::: FixedLine
  \"exp\": \...,
:::

::: FixedLine
  \"jti\": \"\...\"
:::

::: FixedLine
}[]{#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm80}
:::
::::::::::::::::::::
:::::::::::::::::::::
::::::::::::::::::::::
:::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::

:::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::: {#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_Sec24 .section .Section2 .RenderAsSection2 aria-labelledby="Sec24Heading"}
### Auth0 PKCE Authentication {#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_Sec24Heading .Heading}

:::: {#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_FPar10 .FormalPara .FormalParaRenderingStyle1 .ParaTypeImportant}
::: Heading
Tip
:::

This example is based off the Auth0 documentation page: [[[[https://auth0.com/docs/get-started/authentication-and-authorization-flow/call-your-api-using-the-authorization-code-flow-with-pkce#create-code-verifier]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional}]{.RefSource}](https://auth0.com/docs/get-started/authentication-and-authorization-flow/call-your-api-using-the-authorization-code-flow-with-pkce#create-code-verifier)]{.ExternalRef}.
::::

:::: {#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_Par90 .Para}
[]{#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_PB401 .pagebreak aria-label="401" role="doc-pagebreak" title="401"}PKCE (Proof Key for Code [Exchange]{#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm81}) is an extension of OAuth 2.0 designed to improve security when handling authentication flows in public clients, such as browser extensions, where client secrets cannot be securely stored. Unlike traditional OAuth [authorization flows]{#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm82} that require a client secret, PKCE replaces the need for a static secret with a dynamically generated code challenge, preventing interception attacks and improving overall security. This example implements an Auth0 authentication flow using [PKCE]{#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm83} with [launchWebAuthFlow()]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional}. Clicking the toolbar icon triggers the authentication process, and upon successful authentication, the user's profile information is retrieved and logged. Some things to note:

::: UnorderedList
-   This method does not require any special permissions in [manifest.json]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional}, making it an ideal solution for extensions that want to authenticate users without requesting intrusive access.

-   This flow does not require a client secret and instead uses a dynamically generated verifier and challenge.

-   While PKCE has become the recommended standard for public clients, not every [OAuth]{#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm84} provider has implemented it. Some providers still rely on traditional **Authorization Code Flow with Client Secrets**, which is **not suitable** for browser extensions because the client secret cannot be securely stored.
:::
::::

::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::: {#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_Par94 .Para}
:::::::::::::::: {#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_PC11 .ProgramCode}
::::::::::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
{
:::

::: FixedLine
  \"name\": \"Auth0 PKCE Example\",
:::

::: FixedLine
  \"version\": \"0.0.1\",
:::

::: FixedLine
  \"manifest_version\": 3,
:::

::: FixedLine
  \"action\": {},
:::

::: FixedLine
  \"background\": {
:::

::: FixedLine
    \"service_worker\": \"background.js\",
:::

::: FixedLine
    \"type\": \"module\"
:::

::: FixedLine
  }
:::

::: FixedLine
}
:::
:::::::::::::

:::: {.Caption lang="en"}
::: CaptionContent
[Example 11-3a]{.CaptionNumber}

manifest.json
:::
::::
::::::::::::::::

[]{#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_PB402 .pagebreak aria-label="402" role="doc-pagebreak" title="402"}

:::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::: {#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_PC12 .ProgramCode}
::::::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
chrome.action.onClicked.addListener(async () =\> {
:::

::: FixedLine
  const redirectUrl = chrome.identity.getRedirectURL();
:::

::: FixedLine
  const config = {
:::

::: FixedLine
    domain: \"\<YOUR_AUTH0_SUBDOMAIN\>.us.auth0.com\",
:::

::: FixedLine
    clientId: \"\<YOUR_AUTH0_CLIENT_ID\>\",
:::

::: FixedLine
  };[]{#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm85}
:::
:::::::::

::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
  const { verifier, codeChallenge } = await generatePkceChallenge();
:::

::: FixedLine
  const authUrl = buildAuthUrl(config, redirectUrl, codeChallenge);
:::
:::::

::::::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
  chrome.identity.launchWebAuthFlow(
:::

::: FixedLine
    { url: authUrl, interactive: true },
:::

::: FixedLine
    async (authResponseUrl) =\> {
:::

::: FixedLine
      if (!authResponseUrl) {
:::

::: FixedLine
        return;
:::

::: FixedLine
      }
:::
:::::::::

::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
      const params = new URLSearchParams(new URL(authResponseUrl).search);
:::

::: FixedLine
      const code = params.get(\"code\");
:::
:::::

::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
      const tokenData = await exchangeCodeForToken(config, redirectUrl, code, verifier);
:::

::: FixedLine
      const userInfo = await fetchUserInfo(config, tokenData.access_token);
:::
:::::

::::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
      console.log**(**userInfo);
:::

::: FixedLine
    }[]{#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm86}
:::

::: FixedLine
  );
:::

::: FixedLine
});
:::
:::::::

::::::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
// Utility function to generate random bytes
:::

::: FixedLine
function getRandomBytes() {
:::

::: FixedLine
  const rndArray = new Uint8Array(44);
:::

::: FixedLine
  window.crypto.getRandomValues(rndArray);
:::

::: FixedLine
  return rndArray;
:::

::: FixedLine
}
:::
:::::::::

:::::::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
// Utility function to convert buffer to base64 URL-safe encoding
:::

::: FixedLine
function buf2Base64(buffer) {
:::

::: FixedLine
  return btoa(String.fromCharCode.apply(null, new Uint8Array(buffer)))
:::

::: FixedLine
    .replace(/\\+/g, \"-\")
:::

::: FixedLine
    .replace(/\\//g, \"\_\")
:::

::: FixedLine
    .replace(/=/g, \"\");
:::

::: FixedLine
}
:::
::::::::::

::::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
// Utility function to compute SHA-256 hash
:::

::: FixedLine
async function windowSha256(buffer) {
:::

::: FixedLine
  return await window.crypto.subtle.digest(\"SHA-256\", buffer);
:::

::: FixedLine
}
:::
:::::::

::::::::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
// Function to generate PKCE challenge
:::

::: FixedLine
async function generatePkceChallenge() {
:::

::: FixedLine
  const inputBytes = getRandomBytes();
:::

::: FixedLine
  const verifier = buf2Base64(inputBytes);
:::

::: FixedLine
  const shaHash = await windowSha256(inputBytes);
:::

::: FixedLine
  const codeChallenge = buf2Base64(shaHash);
:::

::: FixedLine
  return { verifier, codeChallenge };
:::

::: FixedLine
}[]{#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm87}
:::
:::::::::::

:::::::::::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
// Function to build the Auth0 authorization URL
:::

::: FixedLine
function buildAuthUrl(config, redirectUri, codeChallenge) {
:::

::: FixedLine
  const authUrl = new URL(\`https://\${config.domain}/authorize\`);
:::

::: FixedLine
  const params = {
:::

::: FixedLine
    client_id: config.clientId,
:::

::: FixedLine
    redirect_uri: redirectUri,
:::

::: FixedLine
    response_type: \"code\",
:::

::: FixedLine
    scope: \"openid\",
:::

::: FixedLine
    code_challenge: codeChallenge,
:::

::: FixedLine
    code_challenge_method: \"S256\",
:::

::: FixedLine
  };
:::
::::::::::::::

:::::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
  Object.entries(params).forEach((\[key, value\]) =\> {
:::

::: FixedLine
    authUrl.searchParams.set(key, value);
:::

::: FixedLine
  });
:::

::: FixedLine
  return authUrl.href;
:::

::: FixedLine
}
:::
::::::::

::::::::::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
// Function to exchange authorization code for an access token
:::

::: FixedLine
async function exchangeCodeForToken(config, redirectUri, code, verifier) {
:::

::: FixedLine
  const tokenUrl = \`https://\${config.domain}/oauth/token\`;
:::

::: FixedLine
  const body = {
:::

::: FixedLine
    redirect_uri: redirectUri,
:::

::: FixedLine
    grant_type: \"authorization_code\",
:::

::: FixedLine
    client_id: config.clientId,
:::

::: FixedLine
    code_verifier: verifier,
:::

::: FixedLine
    code: code,
:::

::: FixedLine
  };[]{#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm88}
:::
:::::::::::::

:::::::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
  const response = await fetch(tokenUrl, {
:::

::: FixedLine
    method: \"POST\",
:::

::: FixedLine
    body: new URLSearchParams(body),
:::

::: FixedLine
    headers: {
:::

::: FixedLine
      \"Content-Type\": \"application/x-www-form-urlencoded\"
:::

::: FixedLine
    },
:::

::: FixedLine
  });
:::
::::::::::

::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
  return response.json();
:::

::: FixedLine
}[]{#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm89}
:::
:::::

:::::::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
// Function to fetch user info from Auth0
:::

::: FixedLine
async function fetchUserInfo(config, accessToken) {
:::

::: FixedLine
  const response = await fetch(\`https://\${config.domain}/userinfo\`, {
:::

::: FixedLine
    headers: { Authorization: \`Bearer \${accessToken}\` },
:::

::: FixedLine
  });
:::

::: FixedLine
  return response.json();
:::

::: FixedLine
}
:::
::::::::::

:::: {.Caption lang="en"}
::: CaptionContent
[Example 11-3b]{.CaptionNumber}

background.js
:::
::::
::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::

[]{#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_PB405 .pagebreak aria-label="405" role="doc-pagebreak" title="405"}
:::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::

:::::::::::::: {#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_Par95 .Para}
*// Example console output*

::::::::::::: {#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_PC13 .ProgramCode}
:::::::::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
{
:::

::: FixedLine
  \"sub\": \"auth0\|63f1b2a4c123456789abcdef\",
:::

::: FixedLine
  \"nickname\": \"john_doe\",
:::

::: FixedLine
  \"name\": \"John Doe\",
:::

::: FixedLine
  \"picture\": \"https://s.gravatar.com/avatar/1234567890abcdef?s=480&r=pg&d=identicon\",
:::

::: FixedLine
  \"updated_at\": \"2024-03-12T10:30:45.678Z\",
:::

::: FixedLine
  \"email\": \"john.doe@example.com\",
:::

::: FixedLine
  \"email_verified\": true
:::

::: FixedLine
}[]{#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm90}
:::
::::::::::::
:::::::::::::
::::::::::::::
::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::

:::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::: {#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_Sec25 .section .Section2 .RenderAsSection2 aria-labelledby="Sec25Heading"}
### Extension Authentication with Firebase {#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_Sec25Heading .Heading}

:::: {#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_Par96 .Para}
[]{#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_PB406 .pagebreak aria-label="406" role="doc-pagebreak" title="406"}[Firebase Authentication]{#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm91} supports multiple methods for signing users into a Chrome extension. For most authentication flows, Chrome extensions can use the [firebase/auth]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional} module, making integration straightforward. The following methods work within the extension's service worker and content scripts without requiring extra permissions.

::: UnorderedList
-   Email and password authentication

-   Anonymous authentication

-   Custom authentication tokens (for integrating with external identity providers)
:::
::::

The following code snippet is a simple example of how a [background script]{#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm92} might implement username/password auth:

::::::::::::::::::::::::: {#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_Par101 .Para}
*background.js*

:::::::::::::::::::::::: {#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_PC14 .ProgramCode}
::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
import { initializeApp } from \"firebase/app\";
:::

::: FixedLine
import { getAuth, signInWithEmailAndPassword } from \"firebase/auth\";
:::
:::::

:::::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
const firebaseConfig = {
:::

::: FixedLine
  apiKey: \"YOUR_API_KEY\",
:::

::: FixedLine
  authDomain: \"YOUR_PROJECT_ID.firebaseapp.com\",
:::

::: FixedLine
  projectId: \"YOUR_PROJECT_ID\",
:::

::: FixedLine
};
:::
::::::::

::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
const app = initializeApp(firebaseConfig);
:::

::: FixedLine
const auth = getAuth(app);
:::
:::::

::::::::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
async function signInUser(email, password) {
:::

::: FixedLine
  try {
:::

::: FixedLine
    const userCredential = await **signInWithEmailAndPassword(auth, email, password)**;
:::

::: FixedLine
    console.log(\"User signed in:\", userCredential.user);
:::

::: FixedLine
  } catch (error) {
:::

::: FixedLine
    console.error(\"Error signing in:\", error);
:::

::: FixedLine
  }
:::

::: FixedLine
}[]{#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm93}
:::
:::::::::::
::::::::::::::::::::::::
:::::::::::::::::::::::::

[]{#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_PB407 .pagebreak aria-label="407" role="doc-pagebreak" title="407"}Other authentication methods, such as signing in with Google or other OAuth providers via [signInWithPopup]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional}, require code to be loaded from outside of the extension package. To address this need, [Offscreen Documents]{#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm94} allow the extension to load an authentication page inside an invisible iframe. This iframe loads the external code, runs the authentication process, and relays the results back to the extension.

For [federated authentication]{#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm95} (Google, Apple, SAML, OIDC), Chrome extensions must be explicitly authorized in the Firebase console by adding the extension ID to the list of Authorized Domains. Additionally, the extension's [manifest.json file]{#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm96} should include necessary security policies to allow communication with Firebase's authentication servers.

::::::::::::::::::: {#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_Par104 .Para}
*manifest.json*

:::::::::::::::::: {#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_PC15 .ProgramCode}
::::::::::::::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
{
:::

::: FixedLine
  \"name\": \"Firebase Auth Example\",
:::

::: FixedLine
  \"manifest_version\": 3,
:::

::: FixedLine
  \"version\": \"0.0.1\",
:::

::: FixedLine
  \"background\": {
:::

::: FixedLine
    \"service_worker\": \"background.js\",
:::

::: FixedLine
    \"type\": \"module\"
:::

::: FixedLine
  },
:::

::: FixedLine
  \"permissions\": \[\"**offscreen**\", \"identity\"\],
:::

::: FixedLine
  \"oauth2\": {
:::

::: FixedLine
    \"client_id\": \"\<YOUR_CLIENT_ID\>\",
:::

::: FixedLine
    \"scopes\": \[\"openid\", \"email\", \"profile\"\]
:::

::: FixedLine
  }
:::

::: FixedLine
}
:::
:::::::::::::::::
::::::::::::::::::
:::::::::::::::::::

:::::::::::::::::: {#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_Par105 .Para}
[]{#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_PB408 .pagebreak aria-label="408" role="doc-pagebreak" title="408"}*offscreen-document.js*[]{#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm97}

::::::::::::::::: {#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_PC16 .ProgramCode}
:::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
import { getAuth, signInWithPopup, GoogleAuthProvider } from \"firebase/auth\";
:::
::::

:::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
async function signInWithGoogle() {
:::

::: FixedLine
  const auth = getAuth();
:::

::: FixedLine
  const provider = new GoogleAuthProvider();
:::
::::::

:::::::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
  try {
:::

::: FixedLine
    const result = await signInWithPopup(auth, provider);
:::

::: FixedLine
    console.log(\"User signed in:\", result.user);
:::

::: FixedLine
  } catch (error) {
:::

::: FixedLine
    console.error(\"Authentication failed:\", error);
:::

::: FixedLine
  }
:::

::: FixedLine
}[]{#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm98}
:::
::::::::::
:::::::::::::::::
::::::::::::::::::

:::::: {#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_Par106 .Para}
To [enable]{#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm99} authentication within a Chrome extension, in the Firebase Console, navigate to *Authentication* ➤ *Settings*. Under *Authorized Domains*, add your extension's ID:

::::: {#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_PC17 .ProgramCode}
:::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
chrome-extension://\<YOUR_EXTENSION_ID\>
:::
::::
:::::
::::::

To allow communication with Firebase authentication servers, add the necessary Content Security Policy (CSP[]{#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm100}[]{#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm101}):

::::::::::: {#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_Par108 .Para}
[]{#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_PB409 .pagebreak aria-label="409" role="doc-pagebreak" title="409"}*manifest.json*

:::::::::: {#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_PC18 .ProgramCode}
::::::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
{
:::

::: FixedLine
  \...,
:::

::: FixedLine
  \"content_security_policy\": {
:::

::: FixedLine
    **\"extension_pages\": \"script-src \'self\' https://www.gstatic.com https://apis.google.com https://www.googleapis.com; object-src \'self\'\"**
:::

::: FixedLine
  }
:::

::: FixedLine
}
:::
:::::::::
::::::::::
:::::::::::

:::: {#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_FPar11 .FormalPara .FormalParaRenderingStyle1 .ParaTypeImportant}
::: Heading
Note
:::

The full code for Firebase extension authentication is outside the scope of this book. The Firebase [documentation]{#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm102} covers the entire process here: [[[[https://firebase.google.com/docs/auth/web/chrome-extension]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional}]{.RefSource}](https://firebase.google.com/docs/auth/web/chrome-extension)]{.ExternalRef}.
::::
::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::
::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::

:::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::: {#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_Sec26 .section .Section1 .RenderAsSection1 aria-labelledby="Sec26Heading"}
## Networking APIs {#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_Sec26Heading .Heading}

:::: {#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_Par110 .Para}
Browser extensions are granted access to some powerful APIs that can be used to inspect and modify traffic flow inside the browser. These methods are the reason ad blocking extensions are so effective. This section will examine three [APIs]{#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm103}:

::: UnorderedList
-   The [webNavigation]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional} [API]{#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm104} allows you to observe top-level browser navigation events in very granular detail.

-   The [webRequest]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional} [API]{#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm105} allows you to intercept and modify traffic in a blocking fashion, meaning that for any network request, the extension can inject a JavaScript function that may or may not modify or cancel the []{#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_PB410 .pagebreak aria-label="410" role="doc-pagebreak" title="410"}request entirely. The "blocking" designator indicates that the network request will wait for the JavaScript function to return before continuing.

-   The [declarativeNetRequest]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional} [API]{#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm106} allows you to build rulesets that instruct the browser how it should natively handle network requests. These rules may tell the browser to modify or block a request. This [API]{#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm107} is the successor to the [webRequest]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional} API and is considerably less powerful in several ways. This API is only available in Manifest V3.
:::
::::

:::: {#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_FPar12 .FormalPara .FormalParaRenderingStyle1 .ParaTypeImportant}
::: Heading
Note
:::

In Manifest V3, support for [webRequestBlocking]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional} is fragmented. For example, [Firefox]{#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm108} will continue to support this API, whereas [Chromium browsers]{#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm109} only allow this API in limited situations, such as enterprise policy-installed extensions.
::::

:::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::: {#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_Sec27 .section .Section2 .RenderAsSection2 aria-labelledby="Sec27Heading"}
### The webNavigation API {#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_Sec27Heading .Heading}

:::: {#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_Par115 .Para}
The [webNavigation]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional} API allows an extension to see navigation events from the browser. The API allows an extension to add event handler for the following [navigation lifecycle events]{#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm110}:

::: UnorderedList
-   [onBeforeNavigate]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional}

-   [onCommitted]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional}

-   [onCompleted]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional}

-   [onCreatedNavigationTarget]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional}

-   [onDOMContentLoaded]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional}

-   [onErrorOccurred]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional}[]{#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm111}

-   [onHistoryStateUpdated]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional}

-   [onReferenceFragmentUpdated]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional}

-   [onTabReplaced]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional}
:::
::::

::: {#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_Par125 .Para}
Figure [[11-11](#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_Fig11)]{.InternalRef} shows the flow of [events]{#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm112}.

<figure id="Fig11" class="Figure">
<div id="534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_MO11" class="MediaObject">
<img src="/app/temp/1773197898499_images/images/534317_2_En_11_Chapter/534317_2_En_11_Fig11_HTML.jpg" class="ImageStyle3" alt="Flow chart illustrating a sequence of events in a network request process. The flow begins with &quot;onBeforeRequest&quot; and proceeds through &quot;onBeforeSendHeaders,&quot; &quot;onSendHeaders,&quot; and &quot;onHeadersReceived.&quot; It then branches to &quot;onResponseStarted&quot; and &quot;onErrorOccurred,&quot; with further paths leading to &quot;onCompleted,&quot; &quot;onBeforeRedirect,&quot; and &quot;onAuthRequired.&quot; Red and black arrows indicate different pathways and conditions." />
</div>
<figcaption><div class="CaptionContent">
<span class="CaptionNumber">Figure 11-11</span>
<p>The webNavigation event flow</p>
</div></figcaption>
</figure>
:::

:::: {#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_FPar13 .FormalPara .FormalParaRenderingStyle1 .ParaTypeImportant}
::: Heading
Note
:::

[]{#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_PB411 .pagebreak aria-label="411" role="doc-pagebreak" title="411"}Details of each of these events can be found in the MDN [documentation]{#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm113}: [[[[https://developer.mozilla.org/docs/Mozilla/Add-ons/WebExtensions/API/webNavigation]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional}]{.RefSource}](https://developer.mozilla.org/docs/Mozilla/Add-ons/WebExtensions/API/webNavigation)]{.ExternalRef}.
::::

:::: {#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_Par127 .Para}
Most of the time, extensions will only care to set handlers for the [primary lifecycle events]{#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm114} fired by normal browser navigation:

::: OrderedList
1.  ::: ItemNumber
    1\.
    :::

    ::: ItemContent
    [onBeforeNavigate]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional}
    :::

    ::: ClearBoth
     
    :::

2.  ::: ItemNumber
    2\.
    :::

    ::: ItemContent
    [onCommitted]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional}
    :::

    ::: ClearBoth
     
    :::

3.  ::: ItemNumber
    3\.
    :::

    ::: ItemContent
    [onDOMContentLoaded]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional}
    :::

    ::: ClearBoth
     
    :::

4.  ::: ItemNumber
    4\.
    :::

    ::: ItemContent
    [onCompleted]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional}
    :::

    ::: ClearBoth
     
    :::
:::

[]{#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_PB412 .pagebreak aria-label="412" role="doc-pagebreak" title="412"}
::::

:::::::::::::::::::::::: {#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_Par132 .Para}
The following simple extension will log to the extension console any time a tab in the browser visits a new URL. Load the extension, open a new tab, and navigate to any website to see navigation events in real time.

:::::::::::::::: {#534317_2_En_11_Chapter.xhtml_PC19 .ProgramCode}
::::::::::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
{
:::

::: FixedLine
  \"name\": \"WebNavigation Example\",
:::

::: FixedLine
  \"version\": \"0.0.1\",
:::

::: FixedLine
  \"manifest_version\": 3,
:::

::: FixedLine
  \"background\": {
:::

::: FixedLine
    \"service_worker\": \"background.js\",
:::

::: FixedLine
