This is commonly used for essential tools like ad blockers, password managers, or third-party extensions required for employees.

-   []{#534317_2_En_13_Chapter.xhtml_PB526 .pagebreak aria-label="526" role="doc-pagebreak" title="526"}[[ExtensionInstallBlocklist]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional}]{#534317_2_En_13_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm122} restricts the installation of specific extensions. Typically, this is set to block all extensions except those explicitly listed in [ExtensionInstallAllowlist]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional} or [ExtensionInstallForcelist]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional}.

-   [[ExtensionAllowedTypes]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional}]{#534317_2_En_13_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm123} limits the types of extensions that can be installed. In most [cases]{#534317_2_En_13_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm124}, all necessary extensions fall under the standard **\"**[extension]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional}**\"** type, making this setting rarely needed.
:::
::::

:::: {#534317_2_En_13_Chapter.xhtml_Par121 .Para}
[Chrome Enterprise]{#534317_2_En_13_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm125} also provides granular control over **permissions** via the [ExtensionSettings]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional} policy. These settings can be applied either globally or to individual extensions. When configured correctly, they enhance security by limiting excessive permissions, but overly broad restrictions may disrupt legitimate extensions:

::: UnorderedList
-   [[blocked_permissions]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional}]{#534317_2_En_13_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm126} specifies which permissions are prohibited. Extensions that request these permissions will be completely denied access to the corresponding browser interfaces or JavaScript APIs.

-   [[runtime_allowed_hosts]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional}]{#534317_2_En_13_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm127} defines a whitelist of hostnames an extension is permitted to access. This is useful when you know in advance which domains an extension needs to interact with.

-   [[runtime_blocked_hosts]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional}]{#534317_2_En_13_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm128} restricts extensions from accessing certain domains. This is particularly useful for protecting sensitive resources, such as corporate intranets, financial websites, or private email domains.
:::
::::
:::::::
:::::::::

::: {#534317_2_En_13_Chapter.xhtml_Sec34 .section .Section1 .RenderAsSection1 aria-labelledby="Sec34Heading"}
## Summary {#534317_2_En_13_Chapter.xhtml_Sec34Heading .Heading}

[]{#534317_2_En_13_Chapter.xhtml_PB527 .pagebreak aria-label="527" role="doc-pagebreak" title="527"}In this chapter, we discussed a number of topics involved with building and publishing an extension. We discussed a range of tactics that can be used to better understand what is going on inside your local extension. Next, we went through the different testing formats that work well for browser extensions. We went through all the important parts involved with publishing and updating extensions in the [Chrome Web Store]{#534317_2_En_13_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm129}. Finally, we discussed how analytics tools can be effectively folded into your browser extension and how to manage extensions at an enterprise scale.

In the next chapter, we will cover all the details involved with building a browser extension that targets more than one browser.
:::
::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::

[]{#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml}

::::: ChapterContextInformation
:::: {#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_b979-8-8688-1594-2_14 .ContextInformation}
::: ChapterCopyright
© The Author(s), under exclusive license to APress Media, LLC, part of Springer Nature 2025
:::

[M. Frisbie]{.ContextInformationAuthorEditorNames}[[Building Browser Extensions]{.BookTitle}]{.ContextInformationBookTitles}[<https://doi.org/10.1007/979-8-8688-1594-2_14>]{.ChapterDOI}
::::
:::::

`<!--Begin Abstract-->`{=html}

::: MainTitleSection
# 14. Cross-Browser Extensions {#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_chaptertitle .ChapterTitle lang="en"}
:::

:::::::: AuthorGroup
::: AuthorNames
[[Matt Frisbie]{.AuthorName}^[1](#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_Aff2) [ ]{.ContactIcon}^]{.Author}
:::

:::::: Affiliations
:::: {#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_Aff2 .Affiliation}
[(1)]{.AffiliationNumber}

::: AffiliationText
Glen Ellyn, IL, USA
:::
::::

::: ClearBoth
 
:::
::::::
::::::::

`<!--End Abstract-->`{=html}

:::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::: Fulltext
[]{#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_PB529 .pagebreak aria-label="529" role="doc-pagebreak" title="529"}When it comes to supporting multiple platforms, browser extensions exist somewhere in between websites and mobile apps. One end of the spectrum is the website, which enjoys a "[write-once-run-anywhere]{#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm1}" developer experience. On the other end of the spectrum is the mobile app, which is written in a proprietary language and will only work on a specific operating system and a subset of device types. In this chapter, we will discuss what challenges and tradeoffs are involved when supporting an extension on multiple browsers.

::: {#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_Sec1 .section .Section1 .RenderAsSection1 aria-labelledby="Sec1Heading"}
## Introduction to Cross-Browser Support {#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_Sec1Heading .Heading}

Website code enjoys a [consistent]{#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm2} host environment. You can write a website once, load it on any OS or device or browser, and expect that it will work correctly. There are some corner cases where browsers diverge, but these are relatively rare. All browser vendors spend a lot of time ensuring that their product conforms to the W3C standards and will render a web page more or less in the same way as any other browser.

[]{#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_PB530 .pagebreak aria-label="530" role="doc-pagebreak" title="530"}Mobile apps run in a proprietary host environment. If you want to have a mobile app run on multiple operating systems, code written for Android is nearly useless when it comes to building for iOS. Apps are distributed in different [places]{#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm3}, written in different languages, built for different devices, and utilize completely different SDKs.

Browser extensions borrow a little bit from each of these modalities. Extensions use an API that is standardized across browsers, but not all browsers support the full API. Extensions all are built with HTML, JavaScript, and CSS, but the manifest support between browsers has certain idiosyncrasies and incompatibilities. Extensions are distributed in browser-specific app stores, but a majority of web browsers are built atop Chromium -- meaning they all can install apps [distributed]{#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm4} by the Chrome Web Store.
:::

:::::::: {#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_Sec2 .section .Section1 .RenderAsSection1 aria-labelledby="Sec2Heading"}
## Browser Coverage Tradeoffs {#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_Sec2Heading .Heading}

When choosing if and to what extent you will support multiple [browsers]{#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm5}, you should begin by asking yourself who your target user is. For a majority of extensions, the answer to this question will be "as many people as possible"! Supporting all major browsers is certainly possible, but depending on your extension, this may be overkill. Variable API support, multiple app stores, and idiomatic JS/CSS engines can ensnare a developer's time.

:::: {#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_Sec3 .section .Section2 .RenderAsSection2 aria-labelledby="Sec3Heading"}
### Browser Share {#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_Sec3Heading .Heading}

::: {#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_Par7 .Para}
Fortunately, for extensions that are just getting started, you can very easily support a majority of users with a single code base. Consider the metrics for desktop [browser share]{#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm6} in 2025 shown in Figure [[14-1](#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_Fig1)]{.InternalRef}.[]{#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_PB531 .pagebreak aria-label="531" role="doc-pagebreak" title="531"}

<figure id="Fig1" class="Figure">
<div id="534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_MO1" class="MediaObject">
<img src="/app/temp/1773197898499_images/images/534317_2_En_14_Chapter/534317_2_En_14_Fig1_HTML.jpg" class="ImageStyle3" alt="Bar chart showing desktop browser market share worldwide for February 2025. Chrome leads with 65.55%, followed by Edge at 13.9%, Safari at 8.65%, Firefox at 6.36%, Opera at 2.9%, and 360 Safe at 0.88%. Background features a world map outline." />
</div>
<figcaption><div class="CaptionContent">
<span class="CaptionNumber">Figure 14-1</span>
<p>Desktop browser <span id="534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm7">market</span> share in 2025</p>
</div></figcaption>
</figure>
:::

Google Chrome dominates the market at \~66%. If you were to deploy your [extension]{#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm8} to just the Chrome Web Store, you are automatically covering two-thirds of the market.
::::

::::: {#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_Sec4 .section .Section2 .RenderAsSection2 aria-labelledby="Sec4Heading"}
### Chromium Browser Extension Sharing {#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_Sec4Heading .Heading}

Fortunately, the [modern]{#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm9} browser landscape is working to your advantage. Many major browsers such as Edge, Opera, Vivaldi, and Brave are all built upon the open source Chromium browser. As a result, all these browsers feature nearly perfect compatibility with extensions built for Google Chrome. Each of these browsers can either directly install from or be configured to install from the [Chrome Web Store]{#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm10}. Therefore, an extension published only to the Chrome Web Store can in fact be installed by [Chrome]{#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm11}, Edge, and Opera: ***over 80%*** of desktop traffic!

:::: {#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_FPar1 .FormalPara .FormalParaRenderingStyle1 .ParaTypeImportant}
::: Heading
Note
:::

There are minor differences between Google Chrome and other Chromium browsers, such as the [identity]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional} API. Nevertheless, the overall API parity between all Chromium browsers is nearly seamless.
::::
:::::
::::::::

::::::::::::::::::: {#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_Sec5 .section .Section1 .RenderAsSection1 aria-labelledby="Sec5Heading"}
## Adapting Your Code Base {#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_Sec5Heading .Heading}

[]{#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_PB532 .pagebreak aria-label="532" role="doc-pagebreak" title="532"}Depending on how [broad]{#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm12} you want your extension support to be, organizing your code base may require some finesse. In this section, we'll cover a few simple principles that can be used.

::::::::::::::: {#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_Sec6 .section .Section2 .RenderAsSection2 aria-labelledby="Sec6Heading"}
### API Probing {#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_Sec6Heading .Heading}

Not all browsers support the full WebExtensions [API]{#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm13}. If a particular method is critical to your extension, and a browser does not support it, then there is little you can do. In situations like these, it is probably best to *not* support the browser and encourage the user to use a supported browser.

:::::::::::::: {#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_Par13 .Para}
If, however, the API you wish to use is not critical, and the extension can function without it (even in a degraded state), then you can safely probe for an API or a method to check if it is available. For example:

::::::::::::: {#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_PC1 .ProgramCode}
:::::::::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
if (chrome.storage.session) {
:::

::: FixedLine
  // API is available, proceed normally
:::

::: FixedLine
} else {
:::

::: FixedLine
  // API is not available. UX options:
:::

::: FixedLine
  // - Fall back to similar API
:::

::: FixedLine
  // - Disable or hide buttons
:::

::: FixedLine
  // - Notify user
:::

::: FixedLine
  // - Silently degrade[]{#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm14}
:::

::: FixedLine
}
:::
::::::::::::
:::::::::::::
::::::::::::::
:::::::::::::::

::::: {#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_Sec7 .section .Section2 .RenderAsSection2 aria-labelledby="Sec7Heading"}
### Differential Manifests {#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_Sec7Heading .Heading}

When working in development mode, you will find that most browsers are relatively permissive when it comes to manifest structure. However, when deploying to a marketplace, you may run into a situation where there are strict incompatibilities between what two different marketplaces allow or disallow.

[]{#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_PB533 .pagebreak aria-label="533" role="doc-pagebreak" title="533"}To address this, you will need to generate [differential manifests]{#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm15} for each deployment bundle. A common pattern is to maintain a single "master" manifest and then perform ad hoc modifications specific to each marketplace when generating the deployment bundles.

:::: {#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_FPar2 .FormalPara .FormalParaRenderingStyle1 .ParaTypeImportant}
::: Heading
Tip
:::

Differential [manifest]{#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm16} generation can be quite tedious. Extension development platforms such as Plasmo and WXT can automate this process for you.
::::
:::::
:::::::::::::::::::

::::::::::::::::::: {#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_Sec8 .section .Section1 .RenderAsSection1 aria-labelledby="Sec8Heading"}
## Extension Marketplaces {#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_Sec8Heading .Heading}

Just as mobile apps must be installed from an app store, extensions must be installed from an extension [marketplace]{#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm17}. All major browsers maintain their own extension store.

::::: {#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_Sec9 .section .Section2 .RenderAsSection2 aria-labelledby="Sec9Heading"}
### Marketplace Similarities {#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_Sec9Heading .Heading}

:::: {#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_Par18 .Para}
The following is a list of [features]{#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm18} that are consistent between extension marketplaces:

::: UnorderedList
-   **A developer account is required.** No marketplace allows you to publish a browser extension anonymously. You can always create a separate developer account that does not identify you personally.

-   **All new extensions and extension updates undergo review.** Every extension version submitted will undergo some kind of review process before it is approved. Some reviews will be automated, and some will be manual. The permissions you request in your extension manifest may change how closely your extension is scrutinized.

-   []{#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_PB534 .pagebreak aria-label="534" role="doc-pagebreak" title="534"}**All extensions require some copy and assets for the listing.** Icons, titles, descriptions, [promotional]{#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm19} images, and links to host websites are just a few of the things you can provide when publishing an extension. Some marketplaces will automatically draw a portion of this content from the manifest automatically. Additionally, different marketplaces will require different content.

-   **All marketplaces support manual publishing (except Safari).** This involves uploading a zip file containing all the assets and files for the extension. The store will perform an automated [analysis]{#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm20} of this zip file to check if it is valid, and if it is, add it to the review queue.

-   **All marketplaces support automated publishing.** This is covered in Chapter [[[13]{.RefSource}](#534317_2_En_13_Chapter.xhtml)]{.ExternalRef}.
:::
::::
:::::

::::: {#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_Sec10 .section .Section2 .RenderAsSection2 aria-labelledby="Sec10Heading"}
### Marketplace Differences {#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_Sec10Heading .Heading}

:::: {#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_Par24 .Para}
The following is a list of features that are [different]{#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm21} between extension marketplaces:

::: UnorderedList
-   **Marketplaces have different automated file checking processes.** Linting, filetype checks, file sizes, and manifest validation are just a few of the categories that each marketplace will implement slightly differently.

-   **Marketplaces will review submissions at different speeds.** The Chrome Web Store typically takes a few days to approve submissions, whereas the Mozilla Add-ons marketplace approves submissions almost instantly.
:::
::::
:::::

:::: {#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_Sec11 .section .Section2 .RenderAsSection2 aria-labelledby="Sec11Heading"}
### Chrome Web Store {#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_Sec11Heading .Heading}

[[[[https://chromewebstore.google.com]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional}]{.RefSource}](https://chromewebstore.google.com)]{.ExternalRef}

::: {#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_Par28 .Para}
[]{#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_PB535 .pagebreak aria-label="535" role="doc-pagebreak" title="535"}The [Chrome Web Store]{#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm22}[]{#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm23} is by far the largest and most popular marketplace for browser extensions (Figure [[14-2](#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_Fig2)]{.InternalRef}). It has 138,000 Google Chrome extensions available, which is roughly 7% of the Apple App Store's 1.9 million mobile apps. As mentioned earlier in the chapter, all Chromium browsers are capable of installing extensions directly from this marketplace. To publish an extension, you will need a Chrome Web Store developer account. It [requires]{#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm24}[]{#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm25} a one-time \$5 registration fee.

The page features a search bar, categories list, and a main section displaying a banner ad for a language learning extension. Below, there is a &quot;Recommended For You&quot; section showcasing various extensions like &quot;Custom Cursor for Chrome,&quot; &quot;HTTPS Everywhere,&quot; and &quot;ColorZilla,&quot; each with ratings and user reviews. The interface includes navigation options and a sign-in button." />
</div>

</div></figcaption>
</figure>
:::
::::

:::: {#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_Sec12 .section .Section2 .RenderAsSection2 aria-labelledby="Sec12Heading"}

The Safari Extension [Store]{#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm31}[]{#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm32} is only accessible through the Safari browser (Figure [[14-5](#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_Fig5)]{.InternalRef}). Publishing an extension to the App Store requires an Apple Developer Account, which is \$99/year. You will also need Xcode installed to build the extension and deploy it to the app store.[]{#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_PB538 .pagebreak aria-label="538" role="doc-pagebreak" title="538"}

::::::::::::: {#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_Sec16 .section .Section1 .RenderAsSection1 aria-labelledby="Sec16Heading"}
## Mobile Extensions {#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_Sec16Heading .Heading}

Support for [extensions]{#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm37} is limited on mobile devices. Major browsers like Google Chrome do not support it on any OS, and Apple forbids non-Safari browsers from installing third-party extensions on iOS. However, on both Android and iOS devices, it is still possible to install browser extensions if you are using the appropriate browser. API support is mixed on mobile devices.

::::: {#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_Sec17 .section .Section2 .RenderAsSection2 aria-labelledby="Sec17Heading"}
### Mobile Extension User Interfaces {#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_Sec17Heading .Heading}

:::: {#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_Par37 .Para}
With respect to WebExtensions API [support]{#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm38}, mobile browsers that allow extensions generally have parity with their desktop equivalents. Popups, options pages, content scripts, and background pages all will still run and render. However, due to the mobile form factor, there are some UX differences to consider:[]{#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_PB540 .pagebreak aria-label="540" role="doc-pagebreak" title="540"}

::: UnorderedList
-   The toolbar action button used to open the popup is accessible through the browser's menu button.

-   The options page is generally accessed through a "Settings" button in the extension's management view. This is fairly hard to find for the user, so do not expect this to be a common entrypoint.

-   Safari extensions are packaged as apps. Therefore, they include an additional native app user interface. This is discussed later in the chapter.

-   On mobile devices, the popup page will typically render either as a stand-alone web page (Firefox) or as a native modal (Safari).

-   The options [page]{#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm39} will always render as stand-alone web page.
:::
::::
:::::

::: {#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_Sec18 .section .Section2 .RenderAsSection2 aria-labelledby="Sec18Heading"}
### Mobile Chrome Extensions {#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_Sec18Heading .Heading}

In the previous edition of this book, the Kiwi Browser was the only Android browser that would allow for Chrome [extensions]{#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm40} to be installed on a mobile device. Kiwi Browser was discontinued in January 2025, and the extensions code for Kiwi Browser was integrated into Microsoft Edge Canary. At the time of the writing of this book, MS Edge Canary offers a limited selection of hand-selected extensions to install, but there appears to be no reliable method of installing arbitrary Chrome extensions onto an Android mobile device. This may change in the future.
:::

:::: {#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_Sec19 .section .Section2 .RenderAsSection2 aria-labelledby="Sec19Heading"}
### Firefox Mobile {#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_Sec19Heading .Heading}

::: {#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_Par44 .Para}
Extensions published on the Firefox marketplace can choose to be installable for Firefox mobile (Figures [[14-7](#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_Fig7)]{.InternalRef} and [[14-8](#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_Fig8)]{.InternalRef}). You can directly install extensions from the Firefox Add-ons marketplace. Extensions on the [Firefox Mobile browser]{#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm41} are only possible on Android.

<figure id="Fig7" class="Figure">
<div id="534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_MO7" class="MediaObject">
<img src="/app/temp/1773197898499_images/images/534317_2_En_14_Chapter/534317_2_En_14_Fig7_HTML.jpg" class="ImageStyle10" alt="Screenshot of a mobile browser settings page showing options for privacy and appearance. Privacy settings include options like disabling pre-fetching, hyperlink auditing, and blocking CSP reports. Appearance settings allow theme customization. Some options are checked, indicating they are enabled. The status bar shows the time as 12:30 and battery at 91%." />
</div>
<figcaption><div class="CaptionContent">
<span class="CaptionNumber">Figure 14-7</span>
<p>The uBlock <span id="534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm42">Origin</span> options page in Firefox Mobile</p>
</div></figcaption>
</figure>

<figure id="Fig8" class="Figure">
<div id="534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_MO8" class="MediaObject">
<img src="/app/temp/1773197898499_images/images/534317_2_En_14_Chapter/534317_2_En_14_Fig8_HTML.jpg" class="ImageStyle10" alt="Screenshot of the uBlock Origin app interface on a mobile device. The screen displays a large power button icon at the top, followed by a unique identifier string. Below, it shows statistics: &quot;Blocked on this page: 0,&quot; &quot;Domains connected: 0 out of 0,&quot; and &quot;Blocked since install: 0.&quot; There are options labeled &quot;More&quot; and &quot;Less,&quot; and a button to &quot;Open the dashboard.&quot; The device status bar shows the time as 12:30 and battery at 91%." />
</div>

:::
::::

:::::: {#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_Sec20 .section .Section2 .RenderAsSection2 aria-labelledby="Sec20Heading"}
### iOS Safari {#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_Sec20Heading .Heading}

[]{#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_PB543 .pagebreak aria-label="543" role="doc-pagebreak" title="543"}Safari [extensions]{#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm44} can be installed on iOS devices through the App Store. These are structured differently than other mobile extensions. Publishing a Safari extension for iOS involves a considerable amount of extra effort, but at its core, it still includes the same web architecture as other desktop extensions (Figures [[14-9](#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_Fig9)]{.InternalRef} and [[14-10](#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_Fig10)]{.InternalRef}).

:::: {#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_FPar3 .FormalPara .FormalParaRenderingStyle1 .ParaTypeImportant}
::: Heading
Note
:::

Safari app development is covered in depth later in the chapter.
::::

::: {#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_Par45 .Para}
<figure id="Fig9" class="Figure">
<div id="534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_MO9" class="MediaObject">

::::::
:::::::::::::

::::::: {#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_Sec21 .section .Section1 .RenderAsSection1 aria-labelledby="Sec21Heading"}
## Automated Deployment {#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_Sec21Heading .Heading}

:::: {#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_Par47 .Para}
[]{#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_PB546 .pagebreak aria-label="546" role="doc-pagebreak" title="546"}As of 2022, all major extension marketplaces support [automated deployment]{#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm47}. The details of deploying to each store are out of the scope of this book, but you can find links to the documentation for each marketplace:

::: UnorderedList
-   **Chrome Web Store:** [[[[https://developer.chrome.com/docs/webstore/api]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional}]{.RefSource}](https://developer.chrome.com/docs/webstore/api)]{.ExternalRef}

-   **Microsoft Edge Add-ons Marketplace:** [[[[https://learn.microsoft.com/microsoft-edge/extensions-chromium/update/api/using-addons-api]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional}]{.RefSource}](https://learn.microsoft.com/microsoft-edge/extensions-chromium/update/api/using-addons-api)]{.ExternalRef}

-   **Firefox Add-ons Marketplace:** [[[[https://mozilla.github.io/addons-server/topics/api/addons.html]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional}]{.RefSource}](https://mozilla.github.io/addons-server/topics/api/addons.html)]{.ExternalRef}

-   **Apple App Store:** [[[[https://developer.apple.com/documentation/appstoreconnectapi]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional}]{.RefSource}](https://developer.apple.com/documentation/appstoreconnectapi)]{.ExternalRef}[]{#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm48}
:::
::::

:::: {#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_FPar4 .FormalPara .FormalParaRenderingStyle1 .ParaTypeImportant}
::: Heading
Tip
:::

Some extension development platforms such as Plasmo ([[[[https://www.plasmo.com/]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional}]{.RefSource}](https://www.plasmo.com/)]{.ExternalRef}) can automate deployment of your extension to these marketplaces.
::::
:::::::

::::: {#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_Sec22 .section .Section1 .RenderAsSection1 aria-labelledby="Sec22Heading"}
## WebExtensions API Support {#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_Sec22Heading .Heading}

:::: {#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_Par53 .Para}
Not all [browsers]{#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm49} fully support the WebExtensions API, and some have support for nonstandardized API features. These are changing all the time, so this book will only link to the following pages that catalog each browser's API support:[]{#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_PB547 .pagebreak aria-label="547" role="doc-pagebreak" title="547"}

::: UnorderedList
-   **MDN table showing support across all browsers:** [[[[https://developer.mozilla.org/docs/Mozilla/Add-ons/WebExtensions/Browser_support_for_JavaScript_APIs]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional}]{.RefSource}](https://developer.mozilla.org/docs/Mozilla/Add-ons/WebExtensions/Browser_support_for_JavaScript_APIs)]{.ExternalRef}

-   **Google Chrome API reference:** [[[[https://developer.chrome.com/docs/extensions/reference/]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional}]{.RefSource}](https://developer.chrome.com/docs/extensions/reference/)]{.ExternalRef}

-   **Microsoft Edge API support:** [[[[https://docs.microsoft.com/microsoft-edge/extensions-chromium/developer-guide/api-support]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional}]{.RefSource}](https://docs.microsoft.com/microsoft-edge/extensions-chromium/developer-guide/api-support)]{.ExternalRef}

-   **Safari WebExtensions API compatibility:** [[[[https://developer.apple.com/documentation/safariservices/assessing-your-safari-web-extension-s-browser-compatibility]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional}]{.RefSource}](https://developer.apple.com/documentation/safariservices/assessing-your-safari-web-extension-s-browser-compatibility)]{.ExternalRef}

-   **Opera API support:** [[[[https://help.opera.com/extensions/apis/]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional}]{.RefSource}](https://help.opera.com/extensions/apis/)]{.ExternalRef}[]{#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm50}
:::
::::
:::::

::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::: {#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_Sec23 .section .Section1 .RenderAsSection1 aria-labelledby="Sec23Heading"}
## Safari Extension Development {#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_Sec23Heading .Heading}

[Non-Safari browser extensions]{#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm51}[]{#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm52} are just raw HTML, JS, and CSS, and they can be loaded into the browser directly. Safari's browser extension implementation allows you to use an HTML/JS/CSS code base, but running that code base inside an Apple device requires a healthy amount of extra work. This section will cover the extra steps required to build a browser extension for Safari.

:::: {#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_FPar5 .FormalPara .FormalParaRenderingStyle1 .ParaTypeImportant}
::: Heading
Note
:::

This section assumes you are either building a Safari extension from scratch or porting a non-Safari HTML/JS/CSS extension code base over to Safari.
::::

::::: {#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_Sec24 .section .Section2 .RenderAsSection2 aria-labelledby="Sec24Heading"}
### Prerequisites {#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_Sec24Heading .Heading}

:::: {#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_Par61 .Para}
[]{#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_PB548 .pagebreak aria-label="548" role="doc-pagebreak" title="548"}Safari [browser]{#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm53} extensions are developed and installed as apps and published to the App Store. Therefore, they must be built on Apple's app development infrastructure. You will need the following to develop and publish a Safari extension:

::: UnorderedList
-   A Mac computer with Xcode installed for development

-   A developer certificate to sign your code (\$99/year)
:::
::::
:::::

:::::: {#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_Sec25 .section .Section2 .RenderAsSection2 aria-labelledby="Sec25Heading"}
### Architecture {#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_Sec25Heading .Heading}

According to the [Safari]{#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm54} developer documentation:

::::: {#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_Par65 .Para}
::: UnorderedList
-   A Safari web extension consists of three parts that operate independently in their own sandboxed environments:
:::

::: UnorderedList
-   *A macOS or iOS app that can have a user interface*

-   *JavaScript code and web files that work in the browser*

-   *A native app extension that mediates between the macOS or iOS app and the JavaScript code*
:::
:::::

This may sound confusing at first. To better understand how each of these parts works together, let's generate a new Xcode extension project, install it on an Apple [device]{#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm55}, and analyze each piece.
::::::

:::::::: {#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_Sec26 .section .Section2 .RenderAsSection2 aria-labelledby="Sec26Heading"}
### Creating an Extension Project {#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_Sec26Heading .Heading}

::: {#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_Par71 .Para}
In Xcode, create a new project using the "Safari Extension App" [template]{#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm56}[]{#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm57}. For your first project, let's choose Swift as the language (Figures [[14-11](#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_Fig11)]{.InternalRef}, [[14-12](#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_Fig12)]{.InternalRef}, and [[14-13](#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_Fig13)]{.InternalRef}).

<figure id="Fig11" class="Figure">
<div id="534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_MO11" class="MediaObject">
<img src="/app/temp/1773197898499_images/images/534317_2_En_14_Chapter/534317_2_En_14_Fig11_HTML.jpg" class="ImageStyle3" alt="Screenshot of the Xcode interface displaying a dialog box titled &quot;Choose a template for your new project.&quot; The options include &quot;App,&quot; &quot;Document App,&quot; &quot;Game,&quot; and &quot;Safari Extension App&quot; under the &quot;Application&quot; section, with &quot;Safari Extension App&quot; highlighted. The &quot;Framework &amp; Library&quot; section shows &quot;Swift Package.&quot; The interface includes buttons for &quot;Cancel,&quot; &quot;Previous,&quot; and &quot;Next.&quot;" />
</div>
<figcaption><div class="CaptionContent">
<span class="CaptionNumber">Figure 14-11</span>
<p>The <span id="534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm58">Xcode Safari Extension App template</span></p>
</div></figcaption>
</figure>

<figure id="Fig12" class="Figure">
<div id="534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_MO12" class="MediaObject">
<img src="/app/temp/1773197898499_images/images/534317_2_En_14_Chapter/534317_2_En_14_Fig12_HTML.jpg" class="ImageStyle3" alt="Screenshot of an Xcode interface displaying a dialog box for creating a new project. Fields include &quot;Product Name&quot; with the entry &quot;MVX,&quot; &quot;Team&quot; with &quot;Matthew Frisbie,&quot; &quot;Organization Identifier&quot; as &quot;com.matt-frisbie,&quot; &quot;Bundle Identifier&quot; as &quot;com.matt-frisbie.MVX,&quot; and &quot;Language&quot; set to &quot;Swift.&quot; Buttons for &quot;Cancel,&quot; &quot;Previous,&quot; and &quot;Next&quot; are at the bottom." />
</div>
<figcaption><div class="CaptionContent">
<span class="CaptionNumber">Figure 14-12</span>
<p>Setting options for the new <span id="534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm59">project</span></p>
</div></figcaption>
</figure>

<figure id="Fig13" class="Figure">
<div id="534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_MO13" class="MediaObject">
<img src="/app/temp/1773197898499_images/images/534317_2_En_14_Chapter/534317_2_En_14_Fig13_HTML.jpg" class="ImageStyle3" alt="Screenshot of a code editor displaying a manifest file for a browser extension. The JSON file includes keys like &quot;manifest_version&quot;, &quot;default_locale&quot;, &quot;name&quot;, &quot;description&quot;, and &quot;version&quot;. It specifies icons with various sizes, a background script &quot;background.js&quot;, and content scripts matching a URL pattern. The file also defines an action with a popup and default icon. The editor shows a file directory on the left with folders for different platforms and resources." />
</div>
<figcaption><div class="CaptionContent">
<span class="CaptionNumber">Figure 14-13</span>
<p>The new extension project’s code</p>
</div></figcaption>
</figure>
:::

:::: {#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_Par72 .Para}
[]{#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_PB550 .pagebreak aria-label="550" role="doc-pagebreak" title="550"}Let's go through what each of these generated [folders]{#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm60} is:

::: UnorderedList
-   **iOS (App)** and **macOS (App)**[]{#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm61} are the formal apps that the OS will install. On iOS, this will show up as an app tile on the home screen. The generated version of these apps is just a simple web view.

-   [**Shared (App)**]{#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm62} is the web view content. The generated version is just the default icon, some text, and a button.

-   **iOS (Extension)** and **macOS (Extension)**[]{#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm63} are the native app extensions that mediate between the JS and the native app.

-   [**Shared (Extension)**]{#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm64} is the formal extension code: manifest.json and JS/CSS/HTML, assets, locales, etc. This directory also includes an [ExtensionHandler swift file]{#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm65}[]{#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm66} that is used to handle messaging sent between the JS and the native app.
:::
::::

:::: {#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_FPar6 .FormalPara .FormalParaRenderingStyle1 .ParaTypeImportant}
::: Heading
Note
:::

[]{#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_PB551 .pagebreak aria-label="551" role="doc-pagebreak" title="551"}[Xcode]{#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm67} has separate build targets for iOS and macOS. This should make sense; although these apps share code, you are developing two entirely separate apps for two separate platforms.
::::
::::::::

::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::: {#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_Sec27 .section .Section2 .RenderAsSection2 aria-labelledby="Sec27Heading"}
### Writing the App {#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_Sec27Heading .Heading}

:::: {#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_Par78 .Para}
The generated [extension]{#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm68} doesn't do much out of the box. Let's modify it to do the following:

::: UnorderedList
-   Exchange messages between the popup, background, and content script

-   Show an options page

-   Send messages to the native app
:::
::::

:::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::: {#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_Par82 .Para}
Update the Xcode project files to match the following code:

:::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::: {#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_PC2 .ProgramCode}
::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
{
:::

::: FixedLine
    \"manifest_version\": 3,
:::

::: FixedLine
    \"default_locale\": \"en\",
:::

::: FixedLine
    \"name\": \"\_\_MSG_extension_name[]{#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm69}\_\_\",
:::

::: FixedLine
    \"description\": \"\_\_MSG_extension_description\_\_\",
:::

::: FixedLine
    \"version\": \"1.0\",
:::

::: FixedLine
    \"icons\": {
:::

::: FixedLine
        \"48\": \"images/icon-48.png\",
:::

::: FixedLine
        \"96\": \"images/icon-96.png\",
:::

::: FixedLine
        \"128\": \"images/icon-128.png\",
:::

::: FixedLine
        \"256\": \"images/icon-256.png\",
:::

::: FixedLine
        \"512\": \"images/icon-512.png\"
:::

::: FixedLine
    },
:::

::: FixedLine
    \"options_ui\": {
:::

::: FixedLine
      \"page\": \"options.html\"
:::

::: FixedLine
    },
:::

::: FixedLine
    \"background\": {
:::

::: FixedLine
        **\"service_worker\": \"background.js**\"
:::

::: FixedLine
    },
:::

::: FixedLine
    \"content_scripts\": \[{
:::

::: FixedLine
        \"js\": \[ \"content.js\" \],
:::

::: FixedLine
        **\"matches\": \[ \"\<all_urls\>\" \]**
:::

::: FixedLine
    }\],
:::

::: FixedLine
    \"action\": {
:::

::: FixedLine
        \"default_popup\": \"popup.html\",
:::

::: FixedLine
        \"default_icon\": \"images/toolbar-icon.svg\"
:::

::: FixedLine
    },
:::

::: FixedLine
    **\"permissions\": \[\"tabs\",** **\"nativeMessaging\"\],**
:::

::: FixedLine
    **\"host_permissions\": \[\"\<all_urls\>\"\]**
:::

::: FixedLine
}
:::
:::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::

:::: {.Caption lang="en"}
::: CaptionContent
[Example 14-1a]{.CaptionNumber}

manifest.json
:::
::::
::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::

[]{#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_PB552 .pagebreak aria-label="552" role="doc-pagebreak" title="552"}

:::::::::::::::: {#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_PC3 .ProgramCode}
::::::::::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
\<!DOCTYPE html\>
:::

::: FixedLine
\<html\>
:::

::: FixedLine
\<head\>
:::

::: FixedLine
    \<meta name=\"viewport\" content=\"width=device-width\"\>
:::

::: FixedLine

:::

::: FixedLine
\</head\>
:::

::: FixedLine
\<body\>
:::

::: FixedLine
    \<h1\>I\'m the options page!\</h1\>
:::

::: FixedLine
\</body\>

\</html\>

:::: {.Caption lang="en"}

[Example 14-1b]{.CaptionNumber}

[options.html]{#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm70}
:::
::::
::::::::::::::::

[]{#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_PB553 .pagebreak aria-label="553" role="doc-pagebreak" title="553"}

:::::::::::::::::::: {#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_PC4 .ProgramCode}
::::::::::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
\<!DOCTYPE html\>
:::

::: FixedLine
\<html\>
:::

::: FixedLine
\<head\>
:::

::: FixedLine
    \<meta charset=\"UTF-8\"\>
:::

::: FixedLine
    \<link rel=\"stylesheet\" href=\"popup.css\"\>
:::

::: FixedLine
    \<script type=\"module\" src=\"popup.js\"\>\</script\>
:::

::: FixedLine
\</head\>
:::

::: FixedLine
\<body\>
:::

::: FixedLine
    \<h1\>I\'m the popup page!\</h1\>
:::

::: FixedLine
    \<button id=\"set-bg-color\"\>Change background color\</button\>
:::
:::::::::::::

:::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
    \<button id=\"send-native-message\"\>Send native message\</button\>
:::

::: FixedLine
  \</body\>
:::

::: FixedLine
\</html\>
:::
::::::

:::: {.Caption lang="en"}
::: CaptionContent
[Example 14-1c]{.CaptionNumber}

[popup.html]{#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm71}
:::
::::
::::::::::::::::::::

:::::::::::::::::::::::::::: {#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_PC5 .ProgramCode}
:::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
document.querySelector(\"#set-bg-color\").addEventListener(\"click\", () =\> {
:::
::::

::::::::::::::::::::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
const randomColor = \"#\" + Math.floor(Math.random()\*(2 \*\*
:::

::: FixedLine
24 - 1)).toString(16);
:::

::: FixedLine
  chrome.tabs.query({
:::

::: FixedLine
    active: true,
:::

::: FixedLine
    currentWindow: true
:::

::: FixedLine
  }, (tabs) =\> {
:::

::: FixedLine
    chrome.tabs.sendMessage(
:::

::: FixedLine
      tabs\[0\].id,
:::

::: FixedLine
      { backgroundColor: randomColor },
:::

::: FixedLine
      (response) =\> console.log(response)
:::

::: FixedLine
    );
:::

::: FixedLine
  });
:::

::: FixedLine
});
:::

::: FixedLine
document.querySelector(\"#send-native-message\").addEventListener(\"click\", () =\> {
:::

::: FixedLine
  **chrome.runtime.sendNativeMessage(**
:::

::: FixedLine
    **\"com.matt-frisbie.MVX\",**
:::

::: FixedLine
    **{msg: \"foobar\"},**
:::

::: FixedLine
    **(response) =\> console.log(response)**
:::

::: FixedLine
  **);**
:::

::: FixedLine
});
:::
:::::::::::::::::::::::

:::: {.Caption lang="en"}
::: CaptionContent
[Example 14-1d]{.CaptionNumber}

[popup.js]{#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm72}
:::
::::
::::::::::::::::::::::::::::

[]{#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_PB554 .pagebreak aria-label="554" role="doc-pagebreak" title="554"}

:::::::::::::::::: {#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_PC6 .ProgramCode}
::::::::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
console.log(\'Content script initialized!\');
:::

::: FixedLine
chrome.runtime.sendMessage({ greeting: \"hello\" }).then((response) =\> {
:::

::: FixedLine
  console.log(\"Content script received response: \", response);
:::

::: FixedLine
});
:::

::: FixedLine
chrome.runtime.onMessage.addListener((request, sender, sendResponse) =\> {
:::

::: FixedLine
  console.log(\"Content script received request: \", request);
:::

::: FixedLine
  console.log(\"Sender: \", sender);
:::

::: FixedLine
  if (request.backgroundColor) {
:::
:::::::::::

:::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
    document.body.style.backgroundColor = request.backgroundColor;
:::

::: FixedLine
  }
:::

::: FixedLine
});
:::
::::::

:::: {.Caption lang="en"}
::: CaptionContent
[Example 14-1e]{.CaptionNumber}

[content.js]{#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm73}
:::
::::
::::::::::::::::::

::::::::::::: {#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_PC7 .ProgramCode}
:::::::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
chrome.runtime.onMessage.addListener((request, sender, sendResponse) =\> {
:::

::: FixedLine
  console.log(\"Background received request: \", request);
:::

::: FixedLine
  console.log(\"Sender: \", sender);
:::

::: FixedLine
  if (request.greeting === \"hello\") {
:::

::: FixedLine
    return sendResponse({ farewell: \"goodbye\" });
:::

::: FixedLine
  }
:::

::: FixedLine
});
:::
::::::::::

:::: {.Caption lang="en"}
::: CaptionContent
[Example 14-1f]{.CaptionNumber}

[background.js]{#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm74}
:::
::::
:::::::::::::

[]{#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_PB555 .pagebreak aria-label="555" role="doc-pagebreak" title="555"}

:::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::: {#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_PC8 .ProgramCode}
::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
import SafariServices
:::

::: FixedLine
import os.log
:::
:::::

:::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
class SafariWebExtensionHandler: NSObject, NSExtensionRequestHandling {
:::
::::

::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
    func beginRequest(with context: NSExtensionContext) {
:::

::: FixedLine
        let request = context.inputItems.first as? NSExtensionItem
:::
:::::

::::::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
        let profile: UUID?
:::

::: FixedLine
        if #available(iOS 17.0, macOS 14.0, \*) {
:::

::: FixedLine
            profile = request?.userInfo?\[SFExtensionProfileKey\] as? UUID
:::

::: FixedLine
        } else {
:::

::: FixedLine
            profile = request?.userInfo?\[\"profile\"\] as? UUID
:::

::: FixedLine
        }
:::
:::::::::

::::::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
        let message: Any?[]{#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm75}
:::

::: FixedLine
        if #available(iOS 15.0, macOS 11.0, \*) {
:::

::: FixedLine
            message = request?.userInfo?\[SFExtensionMessageKey\]
:::

::: FixedLine
        } else {
:::

::: FixedLine
            message = request?.userInfo?\[\"message\"\]
:::

::: FixedLine
        }
:::
:::::::::

:::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
        os_log(.default, \"Received message from browser.runtime.sendNativeMessage: %@ (profile: %@)\", String(describing: message), profile?.uuidString ?? \"none\")
:::
::::

::::::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
        let response = NSExtensionItem()
:::

::: FixedLine
        if #available(iOS 15.0, macOS 11.0, \*) {
:::

::: FixedLine
            response.userInfo = \[ SFExtensionMessageKey: \[ \"echo\": message \] \]
:::

::: FixedLine
        } else {
:::

::: FixedLine
            response.userInfo = \[ \"message\": \[ \"echo\": message \] \]
:::

::: FixedLine
        }
:::
:::::::::

:::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
        context.completeRequest(returningItems: \[ response \], completionHandler: nil)[]{#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm76}
:::

::: FixedLine
    }
:::

::: FixedLine
}
:::
::::::

:::: {.Caption lang="en"}
::: CaptionContent
[Example 14-1g]{.CaptionNumber}

SafariWebExtensionHandler.swift
:::
::::
::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::

[]{#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_PB556 .pagebreak aria-label="556" role="doc-pagebreak" title="556"}
::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::

:::: {#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_Par83 .Para}
Most of this code should match what you've seen elsewhere in this [book]{#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm77}. For the most part, this is a conventional background/popup/options/content script setup for an extension. Bolded in the code are the portions needed to send messages from the extension JavaScript code to the native app Swift code. Some things to note here:

::: UnorderedList
-   The special permission [nativeMessaging]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional} is needed to dispatch native messages.

-   The [chrome.runtime.sendNativeMessage]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional} is used to dispatch a native message.

-   To target a native app, you must use the native app's name. For Safari [extensions]{#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm78}, this is the App Bundle Identifier. In this example, the identifier is [com.matt-frisbie.MVX]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional}.
:::
::::
:::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::

:::::::::::::: {#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_Sec28 .section .Section2 .RenderAsSection2 aria-labelledby="Sec28Heading"}
### Testing on macOS {#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_Sec28Heading .Heading}

:::: {#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_FPar7 .FormalPara .FormalParaRenderingStyle1 .ParaTypeImportant}
::: Heading
Note
:::

[]{#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_PB557 .pagebreak aria-label="557" role="doc-pagebreak" title="557"}If you wish to develop Safari extensions without signing the app, you will need to enable Safari to load unsigned extensions. To do so, choose Safari ➤ Preferences, click Advanced, then select "Show Develop menu in menu bar." Next, open the Develop menu and check "Allow unsigned extensions."
::::

::: {#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_Par88 .Para}
Let's first test this on desktop Safari. In Xcode, build the app for [macOS]{#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm79} (Figure [[14-14](#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_Fig14)]{.InternalRef}).

<figure id="Fig14" class="Figure">
<div id="534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_MO14" class="MediaObject">
<img src="/app/temp/1773197898499_images/images/534317_2_En_14_Chapter/534317_2_En_14_Fig14_HTML.jpg" class="ImageStyle3" alt="Screenshot of an Xcode project interface for a macOS application named &quot;MVX.&quot; The interface displays project settings, including identity, deployment target, app icons, and supported intents. The left sidebar shows a file directory with folders like &quot;Shared,&quot; &quot;iOS,&quot; and &quot;macOS,&quot; containing files such as &quot;ViewController&quot; and &quot;AppDelegate.&quot; A dropdown menu at the top allows selection between &quot;My Mac&quot; and &quot;My Mac (Rosetta).&quot; The right panel includes text settings and project document details." />
</div>
<figcaption><div class="CaptionContent">
<span class="CaptionNumber">Figure 14-14</span>
<p>Building the extension for <span id="534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm80">macOS</span></p>
</div></figcaption>
</figure>
:::

::: {#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_Par89 .Para}
When the build [finishes]{#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm81}, you will see the extension app open (Figure [[14-15](#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_Fig15)]{.InternalRef}).[]{#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_PB558 .pagebreak aria-label="558" role="doc-pagebreak" title="558"}

<figure id="Fig15" class="Figure">
<div id="534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_MO15" class="MediaObject">
<img src="/app/temp/1773197898499_images/images/534317_2_En_14_Chapter/534317_2_En_14_Fig15_HTML.jpg" class="ImageStyle3" alt="Screenshot of a notification window titled &quot;MVX&quot; with a yellow lightning bolt icon. The message states, &quot;MVX&#39;s extension is currently off. You can turn it on in Safari Extensions preferences.&quot; A button below reads, &quot;Quit and Open Safari Extensions Preferences…&quot;" />
</div>
<figcaption><div class="CaptionContent">
<span class="CaptionNumber">Figure 14-15</span>
<p>The macOS extension app</p>
</div></figcaption>
</figure>
:::

::: {#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_Par90 .Para}
The view you are seeing is the native OS app displaying a web view. [Xcode]{#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm82} will have automatically installed the extension for Safari, so click this "Quit and Open..." button to open the browser's preferences. Check the box next to "MVX" to enable the extension (Figure [[14-16](#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_Fig16)]{.InternalRef}).[]{#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_PB559 .pagebreak aria-label="559" role="doc-pagebreak" title="559"}

<figure id="Fig16" class="Figure">
<div id="534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_MO16" class="MediaObject">
<img src="/app/temp/1773197898499_images/images/534317_2_En_14_Chapter/534317_2_En_14_Fig16_HTML.jpg" class="ImageStyle3" alt="Screenshot of the Safari Extensions settings window showing the MVX extension. The extension is version 1.0 from MVX, with options to uninstall or access preferences. A highlighted section indicates that the extension can read and alter webpages, including sensitive information like passwords and credit card details. Users can change these settings in Safari Websites preferences." />
</div>
<figcaption><div class="CaptionContent">
<span class="CaptionNumber">Figure 14-16</span>
<p>The enabled Safari <span id="534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm83">extension</span></p>
</div></figcaption>
</figure>
:::

:::: {#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_FPar8 .FormalPara .FormalParaRenderingStyle1 .ParaTypeImportant}
::: Heading
Note
:::

This app is requesting the [\<all_urls\>]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional} host permission, so Safari will ask you to confirm the permission before enabling the extension.
::::

::: {#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_Par92 .Para}
Click the "Preferences" [button]{#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm84}, and your options page will open (Figure [[14-17](#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_Fig17)]{.InternalRef}).[]{#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_PB560 .pagebreak aria-label="560" role="doc-pagebreak" title="560"}

<figure id="Fig17" class="Figure">
<div id="534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_MO17" class="MediaObject">
<img src="/app/temp/1773197898499_images/images/534317_2_En_14_Chapter/534317_2_En_14_Fig17_HTML.jpg" class="ImageStyle3" alt="A browser window displaying a webpage with the text &quot;I&#39;m the options page!&quot; in bold. The browser interface includes navigation buttons, a URL bar, and a refresh icon. The page has a plain white background." />
</div>
<figcaption><div class="CaptionContent">
<span class="CaptionNumber">Figure 14-17</span>
<p>The options page</p>
</div></figcaption>
</figure>
:::

::: {#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_Par93 .Para}
Next, [navigate]{#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm85} to a valid web page like wikipedia.org. Open the console to verify the content script is completing the message handshake with the background script (Figure [[14-18](#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_Fig18)]{.InternalRef}).[]{#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_PB561 .pagebreak aria-label="561" role="doc-pagebreak" title="561"}

<figure id="Fig18" class="Figure">
<div id="534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_MO18" class="MediaObject">
<img src="/app/temp/1773197898499_images/images/534317_2_En_14_Chapter/534317_2_En_14_Fig18_HTML.jpg" class="ImageStyle3" alt="Screenshot of the Wikipedia homepage in a web browser. The page displays the Wikipedia logo and the phrase &quot;The Free Encyclopedia&quot; at the top. Below, there are links to different language versions of Wikipedia, including English, Español, Deutsch, 日本語, Русский, and Français, each with the number of articles available. The browser&#39;s developer console is open at the bottom, showing a log with messages: &quot;Content script initialized!&quot; and &quot;Content script received response: {farewell: &#39;goodbye&#39;}&quot;." />
</div>
<figcaption><div class="CaptionContent">
<span class="CaptionNumber">Figure 14-18</span>
<p>The content script logs</p>
</div></figcaption>
</figure>
:::

::: {#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_Par94 .Para}
The default [extension]{#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm86} icon is the encircled lightning bolt; click it to open the popup page. Click the "Change background color" to set the background to a random color. You should see the content script log the message it received (Figure [[14-19](#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_Fig19)]{.InternalRef}).[]{#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_PB562 .pagebreak aria-label="562" role="doc-pagebreak" title="562"}

<figure id="Fig19" class="Figure">
<div id="534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_MO19" class="MediaObject">
<img src="/app/temp/1773197898499_images/images/534317_2_En_14_Chapter/534317_2_En_14_Fig19_HTML.jpg" class="ImageStyle3" alt="Screenshot of a Wikipedia page with a green background and a popup message saying &quot;I&#39;m the popup page!&quot; featuring buttons labeled &quot;Change background color&quot; and &quot;Send native message.&quot; The browser&#39;s developer console is open, displaying logs related to content scripts, including messages about script initialization and requests with JSON data. The Wikipedia logo and text in multiple languages are visible in the background." />
</div>
<figcaption><div class="CaptionContent">
<span class="CaptionNumber">Figure 14-19</span>
<p>The popup page after a background color change</p>
</div></figcaption>
</figure>
:::

::: {#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_Par95 .Para}
The second popup button will send a message to the background app. To check the logs for the background app, you will need to [open]{#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm87} the macOS Console app and filter for messages from the extension. Filter for messages from the MVX process and for messages containing our dummy "foobar" message. Figure [[14-20](#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_Fig20)]{.InternalRef} shows the log output you should expect to see after clicking "Send native message."[]{#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_PB563 .pagebreak aria-label="563" role="doc-pagebreak" title="563"}

<figure id="Fig20" class="Figure">
<div id="534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_MO20" class="MediaObject">
<img src="/app/temp/1773197898499_images/images/534317_2_En_14_Chapter/534317_2_En_14_Fig20_HTML.jpg" class="ImageStyle3" alt="Console window screenshot showing a message log. The sidebar lists devices and report types, including &quot;Matt&#39;s MacBook Pro&quot; and &quot;Matt&#39;s iPhone,&quot; along with various report categories like &quot;Crash Reports&quot; and &quot;Log Reports.&quot; The main panel displays a message with columns for Type, Time, Process, and Message. A specific entry shows the time &quot;14:31:36.206290-0500,&quot; process &quot;MVX Extension,&quot; and message &quot;MVX App received: { msg = foobar; }.&quot; The top bar includes options for filtering and saving logs." />
</div>
<figcaption><div class="CaptionContent">
<span class="CaptionNumber">Figure 14-20</span>
<p>The os_log output from the macOS extension’s native <span id="534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm88">app</span></p>
</div></figcaption>
</figure>
:::
::::::::::::::

::::::::::: {#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_Sec29 .section .Section2 .RenderAsSection2 aria-labelledby="Sec29Heading"}
### Testing on iOS {#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_Sec29Heading .Heading}

::: {#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_Par96 .Para}
In Xcode, build the app for [iOS]{#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm89}, and use a simulator (Figure [[14-21](#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_Fig21)]{.InternalRef}).

<figure id="Fig21" class="Figure">
<div id="534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_MO21" class="MediaObject">
<img src="/app/temp/1773197898499_images/images/534317_2_En_14_Chapter/534317_2_En_14_Fig21_HTML.jpg" class="ImageStyle3" alt="Screenshot of an Xcode development environment showing a project named &quot;MVX.&quot; The interface includes a sidebar with project files like ViewController and Resources. The main window displays project settings, including deployment info and build configurations. A dropdown menu lists various iOS simulators, such as iPhone 11 and iPhone 13. The top bar shows options for editing schemes and managing schemes. The right panel contains identity and type settings for the project." />
</div>
<figcaption><div class="CaptionContent">
<span class="CaptionNumber">Figure 14-21</span>
<p>Build the extension for iOS and iPhone 11</p>
</div></figcaption>
</figure>
:::

::: {#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_Par97 .Para}
[]{#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_PB564 .pagebreak aria-label="564" role="doc-pagebreak" title="564"}The simulator will [dump]{#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm90} you right into the native app (Figure [[14-22](#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_Fig22)]{.InternalRef}). The view you are seeing is the native OS app displaying a web view.

<figure id="Fig22" class="Figure">
<div id="534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_MO22" class="MediaObject">
<img src="/app/temp/1773197898499_images/images/534317_2_En_14_Chapter/534317_2_En_14_Fig22_HTML.jpg" class="ImageStyle12" alt="Screenshot of a mobile screen displaying a message about enabling an extension. The time is shown as 2:39 at the top. Below, there is an icon with a yellow lightning bolt inside a white square. The text reads, &quot;You can turn on MVX’s Safari extension in Settings.&quot;" />
</div>
<figcaption><div class="CaptionContent">
<span class="CaptionNumber">Figure 14-22</span>
<p>The native iOS <span id="534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm91">app</span></p>
</div></figcaption>
</figure>
:::

::: {#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_Par98 .Para}
[]{#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_PB565 .pagebreak aria-label="565" role="doc-pagebreak" title="565"}Xcode will have automatically [installed]{#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm92} the extension. You can see this app by viewing the home screen (Figure [[14-23](#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_Fig23)]{.InternalRef}).

<figure id="Fig23" class="Figure">
<div id="534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_MO23" class="MediaObject">
<img src="/app/temp/1773197898499_images/images/534317_2_En_14_Chapter/534317_2_En_14_Fig23_HTML.jpg" class="ImageStyle12" alt="A smartphone home screen displaying several app icons against a blurred background. The top row includes icons for Shortcuts, Contacts, Files, and Watch. Below, there is an icon labeled &quot;MVX.&quot; The bottom dock features icons for Safari and Messages. The time displayed is 2:41, with Wi-Fi and battery indicators in the top right corner." />
</div>
<figcaption><div class="CaptionContent">
<span class="CaptionNumber">Figure 14-23</span>
<p>The extension app on the home screen</p>
</div></figcaption>
</figure>
:::

::: {#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_Par99 .Para}
[]{#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_PB566 .pagebreak aria-label="566" role="doc-pagebreak" title="566"}Safari extension [apps]{#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm93} can be managed by opening the Safari settings and selecting "Extensions" (Figures [[14-24](#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_Fig24)]{.InternalRef} and [[14-25](#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_Fig25)]{.InternalRef}).

The screen shows options under &quot;Allow Safari to Access&quot; with &quot;Siri &amp; Search&quot; enabled. Under &quot;Search,&quot; options include &quot;Search Engine&quot; set to Google, &quot;Search Engine Suggestions,&quot; &quot;Safari Suggestions,&quot; &quot;Quick Website Search,&quot; and &quot;Preload Top Hit,&quot; all toggled on. The &quot;General&quot; section includes &quot;AutoFill,&quot; &quot;Favorites,&quot; &quot;Block Pop-ups,&quot; &quot;Extensions,&quot; and &quot;Downloads,&quot; with &quot;Favorites&quot; and &quot;Block Pop-ups&quot; toggled on. The time displayed is 2:42." />
</div>
<figcaption><div class="CaptionContent">
<span class="CaptionNumber">Figure 14-24</span>

</div></figcaption>

The permission is set to &quot;Allow&quot; for all websites." />
</div>

:::

::: {#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_Par100 .Para}
[]{#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_PB568 .pagebreak aria-label="568" role="doc-pagebreak" title="568"}Clicking "Extension Settings" will open the [options]{#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm94} page (Figure [[14-26](#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_Fig26)]{.InternalRef}).

<figure id="Fig26" class="Figure">
<div id="534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_MO26" class="MediaObject">
<img src="/app/temp/1773197898499_images/images/534317_2_En_14_Chapter/534317_2_En_14_Fig26_HTML.jpg" class="ImageStyle13" alt="Screenshot of a mobile browser displaying a webpage with the text &quot;I&#39;m the options page!&quot; at the top. The browser interface includes a search bar at the bottom with icons for navigation, sharing, bookmarks, and voice search. The time and settings icon are visible at the top." />
</div>
<figcaption><div class="CaptionContent">
<span class="CaptionNumber">Figure 14-26</span>
<p>The options page on iOS Safari</p>
</div></figcaption>
</figure>
:::

::: {#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_Par101 .Para}
[]{#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_PB569 .pagebreak aria-label="569" role="doc-pagebreak" title="569"}Next, [navigate]{#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm95} to a valid web page like wikipedia.org. When iOS Safari has extensions installed, the URL bar will display a puzzle piece. Click this to reveal the menu, which should contain an [MVX extension option]{#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm96} (Figure [[14-27](#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_Fig27)]{.InternalRef}).

<figure id="Fig27" class="Figure">
<div id="534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_MO27" class="MediaObject">

The page is available in multiple languages, with article counts listed for each. A settings menu is open, showing options like &quot;Show Top Address Bar,&quot; &quot;Manage Extensions,&quot; &quot;Privacy Report,&quot; and &quot;Website Settings.&quot; The URL bar displays &quot;wikipedia.org,&quot; and the time is shown as 2:54." />
</div>
<figcaption><div class="CaptionContent">
<span class="CaptionNumber">Figure 14-27</span>

</div></figcaption>
</figure>
:::

::: {#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_Par102 .Para}
[]{#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_PB570 .pagebreak aria-label="570" role="doc-pagebreak" title="570"}This MVX [option]{#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm97}[]{#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm98} functions as the toolbar button, and selecting it will open the extension's popup page. Click the "Change background color" button to test that the content script is functioning (Figure [[14-28](#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_Fig28)]{.InternalRef}).

<figure id="Fig28" class="Figure">
<div id="534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_MO28" class="MediaObject">
