The page lists article counts in various languages, including English, Español, Русский, Deutsch, Français, Italiano, 中文, and Português. A popup at the bottom reads &quot;I&#39;m the popup page!&quot; with options to &quot;Change background color&quot; and &quot;Send native message.&quot; The top bar shows the time as 2:54 and includes settings and search icons." />
</div>
<figcaption><div class="CaptionContent">
<span class="CaptionNumber">Figure 14-28</span>
<p>The popup page modifies the web page’s background color</p>
</div></figcaption>
</figure>
:::

::: {#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_Par103 .Para}
[]{#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_PB571 .pagebreak aria-label="571" role="doc-pagebreak" title="571"}You can track the log [messages]{#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm99} created by clicking the "Send native message" button through the Console app in the same way as macOS Safari (Figure [[14-29](#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_Fig29)]{.InternalRef}).

<figure id="Fig29" class="Figure">
<div id="534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_MO29" class="MediaObject">
<img src="/app/temp/1773197898499_images/images/534317_2_En_14_Chapter/534317_2_En_14_Fig29_HTML.jpg" class="ImageStyle3" alt="Console window displaying a log message for an iPhone 11. The message shows a process from the MVX Extension with the text &quot;MVX App received: { msg = foobar; }&quot;. The file path and details such as subsystem, category, activity ID, thread ID, and PID are visible. The timestamp is 2022-08-31 14:59:35.287962-0500. The interface includes options for filtering and saving logs." />
</div>
<figcaption><div class="CaptionContent">
<span class="CaptionNumber">Figure 14-29</span>
<p>The os_log output from the iOS extension’s native app</p>
</div></figcaption>
</figure>
:::
:::::::::::

:::::: {#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_Sec30 .section .Section2 .RenderAsSection2 aria-labelledby="Sec30Heading"}
### Deploying to the App Store {#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_Sec30Heading .Heading}

:::: {#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_Par104 .Para}
You will need to [deploy]{#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm100} separate apps for iOS and macOS. The deployment process is no different than publishing regular iOS or macOS apps. At a high level, you'll need to perform the following steps:

::: OrderedList
1.  ::: ItemNumber
    1\.
    :::

    ::: ItemContent
    Create a project archive
    :::

    ::: ClearBoth
     
    :::

2.  ::: ItemNumber
    2\.
    :::

    ::: ItemContent
    Build and sign the bundle
    :::

    ::: ClearBoth
     
    :::

3.  ::: ItemNumber
    3\.
    :::

    ::: ItemContent
    Validate the bundle
    :::

    ::: ClearBoth
     
    :::

4.  ::: ItemNumber
    4\.
    :::

    ::: ItemContent
    Push to the App Store to begin the approval process
    :::

    ::: ClearBoth
     
    :::
:::
::::

::: {#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_Par109 .Para}
An example [macOS deploy flow]{#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm101} is shown in Figures [[14-30](#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_Fig30)]{.InternalRef}, [[14-31](#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_Fig31)]{.InternalRef}, and [[14-32](#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_Fig32)]{.InternalRef}.

<figure id="Fig30" class="Figure">
<div id="534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_MO30" class="MediaObject">
<img src="/app/temp/1773197898499_images/images/534317_2_En_14_Chapter/534317_2_En_14_Fig30_HTML.jpg" class="ImageStyle3" alt="Screenshot of a macOS application archive window. The main section lists an archive named &quot;MVX (macOS)&quot; with a creation date of August 31, 2022, and version 1.0. The sidebar on the left shows categories like Products, Reports, Insights, and Metrics. The right panel displays details about the selected archive, including version, identifier, type, team, and architectures, with options to &quot;Distribute App&quot; and &quot;Validate App.&quot; The description field is empty." />
</div>
<figcaption><div class="CaptionContent">
<span class="CaptionNumber">Figure 14-30</span>
<p>The generated project archive used for <span id="534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm102">publishing</span></p>
</div></figcaption>
</figure>

<figure id="Fig31" class="Figure">
<div id="534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_MO31" class="MediaObject">
<img src="/app/temp/1773197898499_images/images/534317_2_En_14_Chapter/534317_2_En_14_Fig31_HTML.jpg" class="ImageStyle3" alt="Screenshot of a software interface displaying the contents of &quot;MVX.pkg.&quot; The window shows a file tree with &quot;MVX.app&quot; and &quot;MVX Extension&quot; listed. A summary section details the team name, certificate, profile, symbols, architectures, and version. The team is identified as Matthew Frisbie, with a certificate for Cloud Managed Apple Distribution expiring on 8/28/23. The profile is linked to a Mac Team Store Provisioning Profile. Architectures include Intel 64-bit and arm64, with version 1.0 (1). An &quot;Upload&quot; button is visible at the bottom." />
</div>
<figcaption><div class="CaptionContent">
<span class="CaptionNumber">Figure 14-31</span>
<p>The packaged project ready to be published</p>
</div></figcaption>
</figure>

<figure id="Fig32" class="Figure">
<div id="534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_MO32" class="MediaObject">
<img src="/app/temp/1773197898499_images/images/534317_2_En_14_Chapter/534317_2_En_14_Fig32_HTML.jpg" class="ImageStyle3" alt="Screenshot of a software interface showing a confirmation dialog with a green checkmark indicating successful upload of the &quot;Browser Extension Explorer&quot; app. The message reads, &quot;App &#39;Browser Extension Explorer&#39; successfully uploaded. Monitor the status of your app on App Store Connect.&quot; Options to &quot;Export&quot; and &quot;Done&quot; are visible. The background shows a blurred interface with categories like &quot;Crashes,&quot; &quot;Energy,&quot; and &quot;Metrics.&quot;" />
</div>
<figcaption><div class="CaptionContent">
<span class="CaptionNumber">Figure 14-32</span>
<p>An example success screen when your project is successfully <span id="534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm103">published</span></p>
</div></figcaption>
</figure>
:::
::::::

::::::::: {#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_Sec31 .section .Section2 .RenderAsSection2 aria-labelledby="Sec31Heading"}
### Converting an Existing Extension {#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_Sec31Heading .Heading}

:::::: {#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_Par110 .Para}
[]{#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_PB573 .pagebreak aria-label="573" role="doc-pagebreak" title="573"}Xcode has a command-line [utility]{#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm104} built in that will automatically convert an existing browser extension code base into a Safari extension. The following command will generate an [Xcode extension project]{#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm105} including your existing files:

::::: {#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_PC9 .ProgramCode}
:::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
xcrun safari-web-extension-converter /path/to/extension
:::
::::
:::::
::::::

:::: {#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_FPar9 .FormalPara .FormalParaRenderingStyle1 .ParaTypeImportant}
::: Heading
Tip
:::

More details on this CLI tool can be found here: [[[[https://developer.apple.com/documentation/safariservices/safari_web_extensions/converting_a_web_extension_for_safari]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional}]{.RefSource}](https://developer.apple.com/documentation/safariservices/safari_web_extensions/converting_a_web_extension_for_safari)]{.ExternalRef}.
::::
:::::::::
:::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::

::::::::::::::::::::::::: {#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_Sec32 .section .Section1 .RenderAsSection1 aria-labelledby="Sec32Heading"}
## Firefox Idiosyncrasies {#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_Sec32Heading .Heading}

[]{#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_PB574 .pagebreak aria-label="574" role="doc-pagebreak" title="574"}Other than Safari, which is covered thoroughly in the previous extension, the [Firefox add-on ecosystem]{#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm106} has some notable differences that you should be aware of when developing for it.

::::::::::::::::: {#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_Sec33 .section .Section2 .RenderAsSection2 aria-labelledby="Sec33Heading"}
### Manifest Versions {#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_Sec33Heading .Heading}

Unlike Chrome, which has fully transitioned to **Manifest V3**[]{#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm107} and removed support for blocking web requests via the [webRequestBlocking]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional} [API]{#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm108}, **Firefox has explicitly committed to maintaining this functionality**. Mozilla has recognized that many developers, especially those building privacy and security extensions, rely on [webRequestBlocking]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional} to modify or block network requests in real time. As a result, while Firefox is adopting Manifest V3, it will continue supporting **key Manifest V2 capabilities that Chromium browsers have deprecated**.

:::: {#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_Par114 .Para}
Additionally, Firefox [extensions]{#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm109} need an add-on ID in your manifest. The MDN documentation includes the following:

::: UnorderedList
-   *All* [*Manifest V3 extensions*]{#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm110} *need an add-on ID in their manifest.json when submitted to AMO. Contrary to Manifest V2 extensions, AMO will not accept Manifest V3 extensions without an ID and it will not automatically embed this ID in the signed packaged extension.*
:::
::::

The add-on [ID]{#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm111} is declared under [browser_specific_settings.gecko.id.]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional} For example:

:::::::::::::: {#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_Par117 .Para}
*manifest.json*

::::::::::::: {#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_PC10 .ProgramCode}
:::::::::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
{
:::

::: FixedLine
  \...
:::

::: FixedLine
  \"browser_specific_settings\": {
:::

::: FixedLine
    \"gecko\": {
:::

::: FixedLine
      \"id\": \"browserextensionexplorer@buildingbrowserextensions.com\"
:::

::: FixedLine
    }
:::

::: FixedLine
  },
:::

::: FixedLine
  \...
:::

::: FixedLine
}
:::
::::::::::::
:::::::::::::
::::::::::::::
:::::::::::::::::

:::: {#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_Sec34 .section .Section2 .RenderAsSection2 aria-labelledby="Sec34Heading"}
### Sidebars {#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_Sec34Heading .Heading}

::: {#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_Par118 .Para}
[]{#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_PB575 .pagebreak aria-label="575" role="doc-pagebreak" title="575"}[Firefox sidebars]{#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm112}[]{#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm113} are extension-controlled containers that are defined similarly to popups, and appear similar to side panels, but are a totally distinct implementation. They are declared in the manifest under the [sidebar_action]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional} key and appear alongside the active web page on either side (Figure [[14-33](#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_Fig33)]{.InternalRef}).

<figure id="Fig33" class="Figure">
<div id="534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_MO33" class="MediaObject">
<img src="/app/temp/1773197898499_images/images/534317_2_En_14_Chapter/534317_2_En_14_Fig33_HTML.jpg" class="ImageStyle3" alt="Screenshot of the Mozilla Firefox browser debugging page. The left sidebar displays the text &quot;I&#39;m a sidebar!&quot; and a tab labeled &quot;MVX.&quot; The main section shows the Firefox logo with version 104.0, options for &quot;Setup&quot; and &quot;This Firefox,&quot; and a section for &quot;Temporary Extensions&quot; with one extension listed as &quot;MVX.&quot; Details include location, extension ID, internal UUID, and manifest URL. Options to &quot;Load Temporary Add-on&quot; and &quot;Inspect&quot; are visible. USB is disabled, and no devices are discovered." />
</div>
<figcaption><div class="CaptionContent">
<span class="CaptionNumber">Figure 14-33</span>
<p>A Mozilla Firefox <span id="534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm114">sidebar</span></p>
</div></figcaption>
</figure>
:::
::::

::::::: {#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_Sec35 .section .Section2 .RenderAsSection2 aria-labelledby="Sec35Heading"}
### API Additions {#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_Sec35Heading .Heading}

:::: {#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_Par119 .Para}
[]{#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_PB576 .pagebreak aria-label="576" role="doc-pagebreak" title="576"}Firefox is unlike other browsers in that it adds a significant number of [APIs]{#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm115} to the extension [API namespace]{#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm116}:

::: UnorderedList
-   [captivePortal]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional}

-   [contextualIdentities]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional}

-   [find]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional}

-   [menus]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional}

-   [pcks11]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional}

-   [sidebarAction]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional}

-   [theme]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional}
:::
::::

:::: {#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_FPar10 .FormalPara .FormalParaRenderingStyle1 .ParaTypeImportant}
::: Heading
Tip
:::

For a summary of these API differences, refer to this link: [[[[https://developer.mozilla.org/docs/Mozilla/Add-ons/WebExtensions/Firefox_differentiators]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional}]{.RefSource}](https://developer.mozilla.org/docs/Mozilla/Add-ons/WebExtensions/Firefox_differentiators)]{.ExternalRef}.
::::
:::::::
:::::::::::::::::::::::::

::: {#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_Sec36 .section .Section1 .RenderAsSection1 aria-labelledby="Sec36Heading"}
## Summary {#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_Sec36Heading .Heading}

In this chapter, we covered all the complicated aspects of creating an extension that supports multiple browsers. We discussed how an extension built on the [Chrome Web Store]{#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm117} can support a vast majority of desktop browsers that are based off the Chromium project. We also discussed all the different places you can deploy a browser extension as well as different ways extensions can be used on mobile.

[]{#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_PB577 .pagebreak aria-label="577" role="doc-pagebreak" title="577"}This chapter also covered various [browser idiosyncrasies]{#534317_2_En_14_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm118}. It described in detail how to develop and publish extensions for Safari on iOS and macOS, as well as all the unique aspects of developing for Firefox.

In the next chapter, we will cover all the different tools, platforms, and frameworks that you can use to make extension development and deployment easier.
:::
::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::

[]{#534317_2_En_15_Chapter.xhtml}

:::

[M. Frisbie]{.ContextInformationAuthorEditorNames}[[Building Browser Extensions]{.BookTitle}]{.ContextInformationBookTitles}[<https://doi.org/10.1007/979-8-8688-1594-2_15>]{.ChapterDOI}
::::
:::::

`<!--Begin Abstract-->`{=html}

::: MainTitleSection
# 15. Tooling and Frameworks {#534317_2_En_15_Chapter.xhtml_chaptertitle .ChapterTitle lang="en"}
:::

:::::::: AuthorGroup
::: AuthorNames
[[Matt Frisbie]{.AuthorName}^[1](#534317_2_En_15_Chapter.xhtml_Aff2) [ ]{.ContactIcon}^]{.Author}
:::

:::::: Affiliations
:::: {#534317_2_En_15_Chapter.xhtml_Aff2 .Affiliation}
[(1)]{.AffiliationNumber}

::: AffiliationText
Glen Ellyn, IL, USA
:::
::::

::: ClearBoth
 
:::
::::::
::::::::

`<!--End Abstract-->`{=html}

:::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::: Fulltext
[]{#534317_2_En_15_Chapter.xhtml_PB579 .pagebreak aria-label="579" role="doc-pagebreak" title="579"}Modern browser extensions are rarely written from scratch. There is a wealth of developer tools at your disposal that allow you to efficiently build and test extensions in your chosen JavaScript framework and seamlessly publish them to multiple extension marketplaces.

::::::::::::::::::::::::: {#534317_2_En_15_Chapter.xhtml_Sec1 .section .Section1 .RenderAsSection1 aria-labelledby="Sec1Heading"}
## Building Extensions with React {#534317_2_En_15_Chapter.xhtml_Sec1Heading .Heading}

:::: {#534317_2_En_15_Chapter.xhtml_Par3 .Para}
For developers who wish to build a browser extension with a [JavaScript framework]{#534317_2_En_15_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm1}, the most common choice is React. How you ultimately fold React into your extension will largely depend on the scope and complexity of your application. Some things to [consider]{#534317_2_En_15_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm2}:

::: UnorderedList
-   **Does your extension have one or many entrypoints?** Here, "entrypoint" can mean either an HTML file that will be loaded (popup.html or options.html) or a UI rendered via a content script. A single HTML entrypoint will greatly simplify the architecture of your extension.

-   []{#534317_2_En_15_Chapter.xhtml_PB580 .pagebreak aria-label="580" role="doc-pagebreak" title="580"}**Does your extension UI need to share state between different views?** Extensions only have an [asynchronous shared storage mechanism]{#534317_2_En_15_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm3}, which makes using tools like Redux more complicated.

-   **Does your extension require a React UI inside a content script?** Mounting a single page application in the page is different than rendering it inside a controlled HTML file.

-   **How will your single page application use routing?** Content scripts, options pages, and popup pages all have different routing considerations that may affect how you roll out your React app.
:::
::::

::::: {#534317_2_En_15_Chapter.xhtml_Sec2 .section .Section2 .RenderAsSection2 aria-labelledby="Sec2Heading"}
### Reactive State Management {#534317_2_En_15_Chapter.xhtml_Sec2Heading .Heading}

:::: {#534317_2_En_15_Chapter.xhtml_Par8 .Para}
When managing state for a complex single page application, most developers will reach for [reactive state management]{#534317_2_En_15_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm4} such as Redux or Zustand. It is often advantageous to persist and rehydrate state, allowing for the application to remain consistent across reloads. There are two primary challenges in the context of browser extensions:

::: UnorderedList
-   The typical storage APIs that we would use such as [localStorage]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional} or [IndexedDB]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional} are not shared between all extension pieces. For example, popup [localStorage]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional} will be isolated from content script [localStorage]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional} running on a different origin.

-   The only shared storage mechanism, [chrome.storage]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional}, is asynchronous.
:::
::::

[]{#534317_2_En_15_Chapter.xhtml_PB581 .pagebreak aria-label="581" role="doc-pagebreak" title="581"}Often, you may only [need]{#534317_2_En_15_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm5} to share a small piece of state, such as authentication data, between multiple extension views. Things like route state, server-loaded data, and other view-centric information are likely bound to a specific extension view, and therefore, persisting it in something like [localStorage]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional} may be acceptable. For example, a popup that loads a user's preferences from the server is safe to store that data locally if it will not be displayed anywhere else.

In situations where state must be shared between components, it is possible to configure Redux or Zustand to use [chrome.storage]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional} as an asynchronous store. The [chrome.storage.onChanged]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional} [event]{#534317_2_En_15_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm6}[]{#534317_2_En_15_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm7} means that each view can react to other views mutating the store.
:::::

::::::::::::::::::: {#534317_2_En_15_Chapter.xhtml_Sec3 .section .Section2 .RenderAsSection2 aria-labelledby="Sec3Heading"}
### Routing {#534317_2_En_15_Chapter.xhtml_Sec3Heading .Heading}

:::: {#534317_2_En_15_Chapter.xhtml_Par13 .Para}
When [managing]{#534317_2_En_15_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm8} view state for a complex single page application, most developers will reach for a routing solution such as [React Router]{#534317_2_En_15_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm9}. There are two primary challenges in the context of browser extensions:

::: UnorderedList
-   In views such as popups and options pages, the browser is loading an HTML file directly, so using an out-of-the-box routing solution will lead to invalid paths such as [index.html/foo/bar]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional} that will break on reload.

-   In views rendered via content scripts, you should not modify the host page URL whatsoever. Assume the host page will use the URL hash or query string fully, which can break any routing values you append to it.
:::
::::

Fortunately, both of these problems have straightforward solutions.

::::::::::::: {#534317_2_En_15_Chapter.xhtml_Sec4 .section .Section3 .RenderAsSection3 aria-labelledby="Sec4Heading"}
#### Extension Views and HashRouter {#534317_2_En_15_Chapter.xhtml_Sec4Heading .Heading}

:::::: {#534317_2_En_15_Chapter.xhtml_Par17 .Para}
[]{#534317_2_En_15_Chapter.xhtml_PB582 .pagebreak aria-label="582" role="doc-pagebreak" title="582"}Across all [browsers]{#534317_2_En_15_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm10}, [extension views]{#534317_2_En_15_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm11} have a URL structure similar to the following:

::::: {#534317_2_En_15_Chapter.xhtml_PC1 .ProgramCode}
:::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
extension-protocol://path/to/file.html
:::
::::
:::::
::::::

:::::: {#534317_2_En_15_Chapter.xhtml_Par18 .Para}
All major single page application routers support some form of URL hash routing, which will not interfere with the extension path and can survive page reloads:

::::: {#534317_2_En_15_Chapter.xhtml_PC2 .ProgramCode}
:::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
extension-protocol://path/to/file.html**#/your/app/route**
:::
::::
:::::
::::::

For example, React Router can implement this routing strategy using [[HashRouter]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional}]{#534317_2_En_15_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm12}.

:::: {#534317_2_En_15_Chapter.xhtml_FPar1 .FormalPara .FormalParaRenderingStyle1 .ParaTypeImportant}
::: Heading
Tip
:::

Documentation on [HashRouter]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional} can be found here: [[[[https://v5.reactrouter.com/web/api/HashRouter]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional}]{.RefSource}](https://v5.reactrouter.com/web/api/HashRouter)]{.ExternalRef}.
::::

If you want the popup to always open the most recent URL, you can [dynamically]{#534317_2_En_15_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm13} update your popup URL via [[chrome.action.setPopup()]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional}]{#534317_2_En_15_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm14} and include the hash route.
:::::::::::::

::::: {#534317_2_En_15_Chapter.xhtml_Sec5 .section .Section3 .RenderAsSection3 aria-labelledby="Sec5Heading"}
#### Content Scripts and MemoryRouter {#534317_2_En_15_Chapter.xhtml_Sec5Heading .Heading}

For views that [render]{#534317_2_En_15_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm15} via a [content script]{#534317_2_En_15_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm16}, you may have a complicated user interface that can benefit from routing, but modifying the page URL bar is not an option. All major single page application routers support some form of in-memory routing. These routers are often intended for native applications that do not have a URL, but they work equally well for extension content script views. React Router can implement this routing strategy using [[MemoryRouter]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional}]{#534317_2_En_15_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm17}.

[]{#534317_2_En_15_Chapter.xhtml_PB583 .pagebreak aria-label="583" role="doc-pagebreak" title="583"}If you need your router state to survive page reloads, you can persist your router state in your app state. Be aware that you may need to account for multiple route [states]{#534317_2_En_15_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm18} across different tabs.

:::: {#534317_2_En_15_Chapter.xhtml_FPar2 .FormalPara .FormalParaRenderingStyle1 .ParaTypeImportant}
::: Heading
Tip
:::

Documentation on [MemoryRouter]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional} can be found here: [[[[https://v5.reactrouter.com/web/api/MemoryRouter]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional}]{.RefSource}](https://v5.reactrouter.com/web/api/MemoryRouter)]{.ExternalRef}.
::::
:::::
:::::::::::::::::::
:::::::::::::::::::::::::

::::::: {#534317_2_En_15_Chapter.xhtml_Sec6 .section .Section1 .RenderAsSection1 aria-labelledby="Sec6Heading"}
## Mozilla Tools {#534317_2_En_15_Chapter.xhtml_Sec6Heading .Heading}

[Mozilla]{#534317_2_En_15_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm19} maintains a suite of tools geared for developing browser extensions. To a certain extent they are geared toward developing for Firefox, but in general, they can be effectively used to develop for any browser.

::::: {#534317_2_En_15_Chapter.xhtml_Sec7 .section .Section2 .RenderAsSection2 aria-labelledby="Sec7Heading"}
### web-ext {#534317_2_En_15_Chapter.xhtml_Sec7Heading .Heading}

[[[https://​github.​com/​mozilla/​web-ext]{.RefSource}](https://github.com/mozilla/web-ext)]{.ExternalRef}

:::: {#534317_2_En_15_Chapter.xhtml_Par27 .Para}
The [web-ext]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional} [project]{#534317_2_En_15_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm20}[]{#534317_2_En_15_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm21} is a suite of CLI tools for developing and publishing browser extensions. It features these commands:

::: UnorderedList
-   [web-ext build]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional} creates an extension package from source.

-   [web-ext sign]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional} signs the extension so it can be installed in Firefox.

-   [web-ext run]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional} runs the extension.

-   [web-ext lint]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional} validates the extension source.

-   [web-ext docs]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional} opens the web-ext documentation in a browser.
:::
::::

[]{#534317_2_En_15_Chapter.xhtml_PB584 .pagebreak aria-label="584" role="doc-pagebreak" title="584"}There is also a Webpack plug-in that wraps [web-ext]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional} (not maintained by Mozilla): [[[[https://github.com/hiikezoe/web-ext-webpack-plugin]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional}]{.RefSource}](https://github.com/hiikezoe/web-ext-webpack-plugin)]{.ExternalRef}.

Running [Webpack]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional} by itself will build the extension, effectively running [web-ext]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional}[]{#534317_2_En_15_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm22}[]{#534317_2_En_15_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm23} build on the output of your Webpack build.
:::::

::: {#534317_2_En_15_Chapter.xhtml_Sec8 .section .Section2 .RenderAsSection2 aria-labelledby="Sec8Heading"}
### webextension-polyfill {#534317_2_En_15_Chapter.xhtml_Sec8Heading .Heading}

[[[[https://github.com/mozilla/webextension-polyfill]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional}]{.RefSource}](https://github.com/mozilla/webextension-polyfill)]{.ExternalRef}

[Browsers]{#534317_2_En_15_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm24}[]{#534317_2_En_15_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm25} are gradually moving the WebExtensions API over to an async/await friendly format, but the transition is not yet complete. Mozilla maintains this polyfill library to make the entire API return promises, eliminating the need for callbacks.
:::
:::::::

::::: {#534317_2_En_15_Chapter.xhtml_Sec9 .section .Section1 .RenderAsSection1 aria-labelledby="Sec9Heading"}
## Hot Module Replacement (HMR) {#534317_2_En_15_Chapter.xhtml_Sec9Heading .Heading}

:::: {#534317_2_En_15_Chapter.xhtml_Par37 .Para}
Hot Module Replacement [(HMR)]{#534317_2_En_15_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm26}[]{#534317_2_En_15_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm27} enhances the extension development experience by allowing extension code to update dynamically without requiring a full reload. This reduces development friction by preserving the extension's state while applying code updates. However, due to the architecture of browser extensions, not all extension code changes can be applied via HMR. HMR will vary depending on what bundler and framework you are using, but in general, the following rules will apply:

::: UnorderedList
-   :::: {#534317_2_En_15_Chapter.xhtml_Par38 .Para}
    **Changes that can be applied via HMR**

    ::: UnorderedList
    -   Content scripts

    -   Scripts [injected]{#534317_2_En_15_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm28}[]{#534317_2_En_15_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm29} into web pages via [chrome.scripting.executeScript]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional} (may need explicit reinjection)

    -   All extension HTML pages (if not explicitly listed in [manifest.json]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional})

    -   []{#534317_2_En_15_Chapter.xhtml_PB585 .pagebreak aria-label="585" role="doc-pagebreak" title="585"}CSS and JS referenced in dynamically loaded HTML pages
    :::
    ::::

-   :::: {#534317_2_En_15_Chapter.xhtml_Par43 .Para}
    **Changes that require an extension reload**

    ::: UnorderedList
    -   Any changes to manifest.json

    -   Any changes to the background service worker script

    -   Any changes to [\_locales]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional} files

    -   Any [HTML]{#534317_2_En_15_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm30}[]{#534317_2_En_15_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm31}, JS, or CSS files explicitly referenced in [manifest.json]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional}
    :::
    ::::
:::
::::
:::::

:::::::: {#534317_2_En_15_Chapter.xhtml_Sec10 .section .Section1 .RenderAsSection1 aria-labelledby="Sec10Heading"}
## Bundlers {#534317_2_En_15_Chapter.xhtml_Sec10Heading .Heading}

When building browser extensions, managing multiple scripts, styles, and assets efficiently can quickly become complex. Modern JavaScript tools like React, Vue, and TypeScript introduce additional compilation steps. [**Bundlers**]{#534317_2_En_15_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm32} streamline the development process by handling dependency resolution, optimizing code for production, and ensuring cross-browser compatibility. They provide features like hot-reloading, tree shaking, and modular workflows, improving both performance and developer experience.

::: {#534317_2_En_15_Chapter.xhtml_Sec11 .section .Section2 .RenderAsSection2 aria-labelledby="Sec11Heading"}
### Vite {#534317_2_En_15_Chapter.xhtml_Sec11Heading .Heading}

[Home page:]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional} [[[[https://vite.dev/]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional}]{.RefSource}](https://vite.dev/)]{.ExternalRef}

[Getting started:]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional} [[[[https://vite.dev/guide/]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional}]{.RefSource}](https://vite.dev/guide/)]{.ExternalRef}

[Vite]{#534317_2_En_15_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm33}[]{#534317_2_En_15_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm34} is a modern, high-performance build tool that offers a fast and efficient development experience for browser extensions. Unlike Webpack, which relies on complex configuration files, Vite takes an "on-demand" []{#534317_2_En_15_Chapter.xhtml_PB586 .pagebreak aria-label="586" role="doc-pagebreak" title="586"}[approach]{#534317_2_En_15_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm35} by serving files directly via ES modules in development and using Rollup for optimized production builds. This results in near-instant startup times, native support for modern JavaScript features like TypeScript and JSX, and an overall streamlined [workflow]{#534317_2_En_15_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm36}[]{#534317_2_En_15_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm37}. Vite requires a plug-in like CRXJS to support extension development.
:::

::: {#534317_2_En_15_Chapter.xhtml_Sec12 .section .Section2 .RenderAsSection2 aria-labelledby="Sec12Heading"}
### Parcel {#534317_2_En_15_Chapter.xhtml_Sec12Heading .Heading}

[Home page:]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional} [[[[https://parceljs.org/]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional}]{.RefSource}](https://parceljs.org/)]{.ExternalRef}

[Getting started:]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional} [[[[https://parceljs.org/recipes/web-extension/]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional}]{.RefSource}](https://parceljs.org/recipes/web-extension/)]{.ExternalRef}

[Parcel]{#534317_2_En_15_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm38}[]{#534317_2_En_15_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm39} is a powerful tool for browser extension development, offering strong support for Manifest V3, multiple entrypoints, and modern web technologies like TypeScript, React, Vue, and Sass. Unlike Webpack, which relies on an extensive configuration file, Parcel eliminates the need for explicit setup by using pre-configured NPM packages that automatically detect how to build and bundle an application. By installing the [\@parcel/config-webextension package]{#534317_2_En_15_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm40}, Parcel can read [manifest.json]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional} to determine entrypoints, parse linked HTML files, and generate the necessary JavaScript and CSS bundles for each component of the extension. It also simplifies development by handling file translation seamlessly, allowing developers to use TypeScript, JSX, Vue, and Sass directly in their project without requiring manual [compilation]{#534317_2_En_15_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm41}[]{#534317_2_En_15_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm42} steps.
:::

::::: {#534317_2_En_15_Chapter.xhtml_Sec13 .section .Section2 .RenderAsSection2 aria-labelledby="Sec13Heading"}
### Webpack {#534317_2_En_15_Chapter.xhtml_Sec13Heading .Heading}

[Home page:]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional} [[[[https://webpack.js.org/]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional}]{.RefSource}](https://webpack.js.org/)]{.ExternalRef}

[Getting started:]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional} [[[[https://webpack.js.org/guides/getting-started/]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional}]{.RefSource}](https://webpack.js.org/guides/getting-started/)]{.ExternalRef}

[Webpack]{#534317_2_En_15_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm43}[]{#534317_2_En_15_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm44} is often the go-to choice for website development, and it certainly continues to be a sensible choice for browser extension development. Unlike Parcel, Webpack favors explicitly defining behavior []{#534317_2_En_15_Chapter.xhtml_PB587 .pagebreak aria-label="587" role="doc-pagebreak" title="587"}inside a config file. For particularly complicated extensions, it may be advantageous for you to have the ability to explicitly dictate how Webpack should compile and bundle your extension files. Webpack also has a significantly larger community behind it, which may be an important [consideration]{#534317_2_En_15_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm45}[]{#534317_2_En_15_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm46} for long-term support.

:::: {#534317_2_En_15_Chapter.xhtml_FPar3 .FormalPara .FormalParaRenderingStyle1 .ParaTypeImportant}
::: Heading
Warning
:::

There are many GitHub repositories containing outdated Webpack browser extension templates. These target older versions of Webpack or are strictly limited to Manifest V2. Be careful when choosing a starter repository; it can often be very painful to move away from it.
::::
:::::
::::::::

:::::: {#534317_2_En_15_Chapter.xhtml_Sec14 .section .Section1 .RenderAsSection1 aria-labelledby="Sec14Heading"}
## Frameworks {#534317_2_En_15_Chapter.xhtml_Sec14Heading .Heading}

While bundlers optimize code compilation, extension **frameworks** provide structured project setups, pre-configured toolchains, and built-in support for WebExtensions APIs. These frameworks simplify handling [manifest.json]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional}[]{#534317_2_En_15_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm47}, managing background and content scripts, and ensuring cross-browser compatibility, allowing developers to focus on features rather than boilerplate setup. Common [benefits]{#534317_2_En_15_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm48} include JS framework-agnostic support, standardized file structures, automatic manifest generation, optimized hot-reloading, environment variable handling, automatic third-party code bundling, built-in API utilities, and streamlined deployment.

::: {#534317_2_En_15_Chapter.xhtml_Sec15 .section .Section2 .RenderAsSection2 aria-labelledby="Sec15Heading"}
### WXT {#534317_2_En_15_Chapter.xhtml_Sec15Heading .Heading}

[Home page:]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional} [[[[https://wxt.dev]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional}]{.RefSource}](https://wxt.dev)]{.ExternalRef}

[Getting started:]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional} [[[[https://wxt.dev/guide/introduction]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional}]{.RefSource}](https://wxt.dev/guide/introduction)]{.ExternalRef}

[]{#534317_2_En_15_Chapter.xhtml_PB588 .pagebreak aria-label="588" role="doc-pagebreak" title="588"}[WXT]{#534317_2_En_15_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm49}[]{#534317_2_En_15_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm50} (Web Extension Toolkit) is an opinionated framework designed for structured and scalable browser extension development, taking inspiration from Nuxt.js in the way it automates project structure, configuration, and optimizations. It dynamically generates the extension manifest, eliminating the need for manual updates, and uses [esbuild]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional} for lightning-fast bundling with tree shaking and dead code elimination. Unlike lighter solutions, WXT provides robust multi-browser support and integrated testing tools, making it well-suited for developers working on complex, feature-rich extensions. It's ideal for those who prefer TypeScript-first [development]{#534317_2_En_15_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm51}[]{#534317_2_En_15_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm52} and structured workflows with automated optimizations.
:::

::: {#534317_2_En_15_Chapter.xhtml_Sec16 .section .Section2 .RenderAsSection2 aria-labelledby="Sec16Heading"}
### Plasmo {#534317_2_En_15_Chapter.xhtml_Sec16Heading .Heading}

[Home page:]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional} [[[[https://www.plasmo.com]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional}]{.RefSource}](https://www.plasmo.com)]{.ExternalRef}

[Getting started:]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional} [[[[https://docs.plasmo.com/framework]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional}]{.RefSource}](https://docs.plasmo.com/framework)]{.ExternalRef}

[Plasmo]{#534317_2_En_15_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm53}[]{#534317_2_En_15_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm54} is a React-first extension framework that treats extension development like building a modern React web app, similar to how Next.js simplifies frontend development. It provides file-based routing, allowing developers to define extension components (popup, options, background scripts) by simply placing them in designated folders. A standout feature is zero-config setup: Plasmo automatically generates the manifest and streamlines dependency management. Additionally, it includes Plasmo's cloud deployment, making it an attractive choice for developers who want an easy path to publishing their extensions across multiple marketplaces. Its [integration]{#534317_2_En_15_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm55}[]{#534317_2_En_15_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm56} with modern web tooling makes it ideal for frontend-heavy extensions with complex UIs.
:::

::: {#534317_2_En_15_Chapter.xhtml_Sec17 .section .Section2 .RenderAsSection2 aria-labelledby="Sec17Heading"}
### Extension.js {#534317_2_En_15_Chapter.xhtml_Sec17Heading .Heading}

[Home page:]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional} [[[[https://extension.js.org]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional}]{.RefSource}](https://extension.js.org)]{.ExternalRef}

[Getting started:]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional} [[[[https://extension.js.org/docs/getting-started/immediately]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional}]{.RefSource}](https://extension.js.org/docs/getting-started/immediately)]{.ExternalRef}

[]{#534317_2_En_15_Chapter.xhtml_PB589 .pagebreak aria-label="589" role="doc-pagebreak" title="589"}[Extension.js]{#534317_2_En_15_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm57}[]{#534317_2_En_15_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm58} is a command-line tool designed to simplify the development of cross-browser extensions by integrating a modern development stack with built-in support for TypeScript, WebAssembly, and next-generation JavaScript. It enables developers to leverage native browser extension APIs while streamlining project setup and management. The [create]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional} command facilitates the initialization of new extension projects, allowing developers to specify templates via the [\--template]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional} flag, which provides pre-built structures, components, and configurations tailored to different needs. For those with existing extensions, Extension.js can be installed as a package, allowing manual script [configuration]{#534317_2_En_15_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm59}[]{#534317_2_En_15_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm60} to align with current workflows.
:::
::::::

::::: {#534317_2_En_15_Chapter.xhtml_Sec18 .section .Section1 .RenderAsSection1 aria-labelledby="Sec18Heading"}
## Useful Sites {#534317_2_En_15_Chapter.xhtml_Sec18Heading .Heading}

:::: {#534317_2_En_15_Chapter.xhtml_Par69 .Para}
::: UnorderedList
-   [[[[https://buildingbrowserextensions.com]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional}]{.RefSource}](https://buildingbrowserextensions.com)]{.ExternalRef} is the companion website of this book. It includes a link to the Example Chrome Extension, a Chrome extension that consists of open source demos for each extension API.

-   [[[[https://developer.chrome.com/]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional}]{.RefSource}](https://developer.chrome.com/)]{.ExternalRef} is Chrome's official site to help you build Extensions, publish on the Chrome Web Store, optimize your website, and more.

-   [[[[https://extensionworkshop.com/]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional}]{.RefSource}](https://extensionworkshop.com/)]{.ExternalRef} helps with creating and publishing Firefox add-ons that make browsing smarter, safer, and faster. You'll find the resources you need, whether you're getting started with extension development, preparing to launch your innovation, or developing a custom enterprise solution.

-   [[[[https://webext.eu/]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional}]{.RefSource}](https://webext.eu/)]{.ExternalRef} quickly generates browser extension templates with just a few clicks. It is maintained by Mozilla. It is very handy for quickly prototyping extensions.
:::
::::
:::::

::: {#534317_2_En_15_Chapter.xhtml_Sec19 .section .Section1 .RenderAsSection1 aria-labelledby="Sec19Heading"}
## Summary {#534317_2_En_15_Chapter.xhtml_Sec19Heading .Heading}

[]{#534317_2_En_15_Chapter.xhtml_PB590 .pagebreak aria-label="590" role="doc-pagebreak" title="590"}In this chapter, we explored the essential tools and frameworks that streamline browser extension development. We began by examining how React can be effectively integrated into an extension project, covering key considerations such as routing, state management, and UI rendering across different extension views. Next, we delved into various bundlers including Webpack, Vite, and Parcel, discussing their strengths and how they handle the complexities of extension development. We also introduced frameworks like WXT, Plasmo, and [Extension.js]{#534317_2_En_15_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm61}, which provide structured workflows and automation to simplify extension deployment.
:::
::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::

[]{#Backmatter_001.xhtml}
